Dialogue:
Judge: I understand, but my hypothetical assumes that you know all that, your clients know all that, maybe even intend it.
Now whether there -- there are allegations in this complaint sufficient, put that aside.
But, if you know or intend all of that, then what?
Lawyer of Defendant: I still don't think that establishes proximate cause when you have an intervening independent crime.
And I'll point you to Hemi Group, Your Honor.
Judge: Yeah.
Lawyer of Defendant: In Hemi Group, the underlying statute was the filing of the tax reports.
The entire purpose of the tax reports was to allow governmental entities to collect tax revenue from cigarette sales online that weren't otherwise subject to sales taxes. But the plurality held that that independent intervening act still broke proximate cause.
I think it goes back to the Court's 1876 decision in the St. Paul Railway case, where you made clear that if there is a sufficient and independent cause --
Judge: It wasn't me.
Lawyer of Defendant: Your Honor, I -- I -- I think of the Court as a collective body that operates across time.

Lawyer of Defendant: And it made clear -- it made --
Judge: Justice Gorsuch doesn't believe that.

Judge: Yes, I do.
Lawyer of Defendant: And it made quite clear way back in 1876 when you do have that, and I'm quoting, a sufficient and independent cause operating between the wrong and the injury, that does break the chain of causation even if it's eminently foreseeable, just like many, many, many years later in the Hemi Group case, the Court concluded the same even though it was eminently foreseeable that the retailer's failure to file the tax reports could lead to lost tax revenue.
Judge: Mr. Francisco, can I just put a point on this? Because I want to make sure I understand the -- the line of questions you've been asked. So it seems to me as Justice Thomas began that when we're talking about the statute that was the violation at the beginning, for the predicate, it has to be a statute that was specifically applicable to the sale or marketing of the product, the gun.
Lawyer of Defendant: Yes.
Judge: Justice Sotomayor asked you.
So that's the retailer selling it to the bad guy, right?
Lawyer of Defendant: Yes.
Judge: Okay.
That's where the proximate cause inquiry comes in.
Your client -- the theory, right? The theory is that your client under 2, under Example 2, has aided and abetted as the manufacturer. Proximate cause doesn't appear in that portion of the statute.
It's only in the predicate portion.
So, if we accept that framing of the theory, the framing of the complaint, we're really only asking about proximate cause, as Justice Gorsuch was asking you, between the retailer, the sale, and the harm ultimately caused to Mexico.
And then we're looking at the chain of events that you're talking at right now: sale to the bad guy, smuggled across the border, misuse.
Lawyer of Defendant: Mm-hmm.
So I think I have two responses, Your Honor.
The first is I actually disagree with how you framed it.
But the second is, even if I accept how you framed it, I still think there's no proximate cause.
The statute says that there has to be proximate cause between our violation and Mexico's injury.
Our violation is not the illegal sale itself.
It's the actions that we undertake to aid and abet it, the violative aiding and abetting conduct.
So --
Judge: How does that fit in the statute, though?
Lawyer of Defendant: Your Honor, because I think the statute says it's a -- the statute requires that -- sets forth the exception where a seller has knowingly violated an applicable statute and the violation, referring to the seller's violation --
Judge: But it's not just applicable statute.
It's statute applicable to the sale or marketing of the product.
So that seems to me to refer to a specific statute relating to the manufacture, sale, distribution of guns, not the aiding-and-abetting statute, right?
Lawyer of Defendant: Well, I think it's got to refer to our violation.
It refers back to the seller's violation.
And, here, the seller hasn't specifically -- and this maybe goes to the confusion that Justice Thomas pointed out. But the seller's violation is not the actual retail sale.
We're not retail sellers.
Judge: Right.
Lawyer of Defendant: The seller's violation here is the aiding and abetting of that retail sale.
And I presume what they are invoking is the federal aiding-and-abetting statute, and they're trying to combine that with the actual specific sale.
So I think there are all kinds of problems with their theory --
Judge: I guess I don't get that, Mr. Francisco.
I mean, aiding and abetting is a form of vicarious liability.
Why wouldn't you just say the aiding-and-abetting violation is the violation that is aided and abetted, which, here, as Justice Barrett said, is the retail sale, say the sale to a straw purchaser?
Lawyer of Defendant: Sure, Your Honor.
I think it's -- I'm just trying to construct the statute properly, and I think, as a matter of proper statutory construction, that's where you begin the proximate cause, with our violation.
But I don't really want to fight about it because --
Judge: Well, your violation, as a matter of vicarious liability, is the violation that the retail seller, you know, sells to the straw purchaser.
Lawyer of Defendant: Your Honor, I'm not sure that's right, but -- but, again, I'm willing to assume for the sake of argument that it is right because I still think that there's no proximate cause in between the illegal retail sale and Mexico's injury off in Mexico.
And I sure don't think that they've come anywhere close to establishing aiding-and-abetting liability. I've already explained why I think there are multiple independent crimes after the retail sale, in addition to the smuggle across an international border and the murder and mayhem committed independently by cartels in Mexico.
To me, that is more than sufficient to break that chain.
But, in any event, I think that their theory of aiding-and-abetting liability is equally farfetched.
Judge: So, with reference to aiding and abetting, could you just explain to me the sort of structure of this industry? Who are these distributors? Are they pass-through entities? Are they completely independent? Might they be both? What -- what -- what -- what's the --
Lawyer of Defendant: They're independent entities, Your Honor.
It's possible that there might be some internal distribution, but, by and large, a manufacturer makes the firearms.
Then there are distributors who purchase the firearms from different manufacturers.
Those distributors then sell firearms to retailers.
Everyone in this chain is fully licensed.
The retailers then are fully licensed and they sell to purchasers.
The allegation is some small percentage of those sales are illegal and we know it.

Dialogue:
Judge: Well, but if -- why -- your voluntary dismissal was without prejudice, right? Why do you have to reopen that? Can't you just bring another proceeding?
Lawyer of Defendant: Well, no, because of the limitation periods.
Judge: So this is a way of avoiding the -- the statute of limitations?
Lawyer of Defendant: Well, it's -- it's -- it's an application arguing that the application of the limitation periods to this case is inequitable given the circumstances of the case, which is an -- an -- an argument that the district court accepted in granting relief under Rules 60(b)(6) and (b)(1) as well.
But, under (b)(6), that was the ground that the dissent would have affirmed.
And so, on remand, that --that will be whatever arguments remain as to whether the reopening of the case under Rule 60(b)(6) --
Judge: I'm sorry, I'm just trying to get a handle on exactly what happened.
If you had filed another ADEA challenge, that would have been barred by the statute of limitations?
Lawyer of Defendant: That's right.
Judge: So you're reopening your voluntary dismissal to avoid that consequence?
Lawyer of Defendant: It's -- well, a new case could not have been filed on the merits because of the limitation period.
And a new application under the FAA also could not have been filed because that has also a limitation period of 90 days.
And so, considering all these factors, the --the law, and --and the other issues that are discussed by the district court, the district court reopened the case.
And that is an issue that is, A, not jurisdictional, B, was not addressed below, and C, could be considered on remand.
Judge: Counsel, I --I see two jurisdictional issues here.
One, what's the district court's jurisdiction? What's the subject matter --what gives it subject matter jurisdiction to consider the 60(b) motion at all? And the second jurisdictional issue I see is: What gives it subject matter jurisdiction to vacate an arbitrable award? So they chose to go, the Tenth Circuit, on the 60(b).
That's what we granted cert on.
And so I'm assuming that you must think that the jurisdiction over the motion to reopen stems from the original ADEA case?
Lawyer of Defendant: That's right.
Judge: All right.
So that has been opened.
What would have given the district court jurisdiction to vacate the -- an arbitrable award?
Lawyer of Defendant: Right.
Judge: Because I don't think the jurisdiction that's granted by the federal statute or the ADEA is simply to order parties to go to arbitration.
So what gives it subject matter jurisdiction to vacate the award?
Lawyer of Defendant: Well, once the case is reopened, it's a federal case, and --and our - - first, it has jurisdiction because of that.
And --
Judge: It has jurisdiction to reopen the ADEA case, but this is no longer an ADEA case.
Lawyer of Defendant: So --
Judge: At least the motion to vacate is not related to anything that's happened in the rights and responsibilities between the two of you with respect to the claim.
Lawyer of Defendant: Well, it's related in the sense that it --as the Court looked at the question in Badgerow, the question of -- of a -- a motion to --to vacate an award, and there, of course, the merits were not before the Court, it was a standalone motion to vacate.
And that was the premise of the question.
And the Court said in that circumstances you need independent subject matter jurisdiction. It didn't speak to whether, if the case -- the merits were before the Court and the case were reopened, there would be a need for an independent -- for subject matter jurisdiction.
Justice Breyer, in dissent, noted there -- there may not be.
And I -- I didn't read the majority -- the opinion of the Court to say otherwise.
Kokkonen itself, upon which Badgerow was based, made very clear --
Judge: Well, assume that -- if you assume that -- I think that Badgerow would prevent you from just relying on the federal statute to vacate the arbitration award.
My understanding of the facts here is that you're going to try to get in through diversity.
Is that right?
Lawyer of Defendant: We do have diversity.
The amount in controversy was not pleaded, and -- and the court --whether the court would forgive that is a matter below.
Judge: Uh -huh.
And if -- at any rate, even if you can't --even if you don't have jurisdiction to vacate the arbitration award, because Badgerow says you can't do it based on the federal statute and they're not going to allow you to replead your diversity claim, you know, even assuming that, I take it that what you're saying is that the jurisdictional question as to 60(b) is entirely different?
Lawyer of Defendant: That's exactly right.
It -- none of these issues are -- are obstacles to resolving the question presented.
I'm -- I'm taking them as -- as -- as given, of course.
Judge: Yeah.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff's goal or motive behind their words is to reopen a previously dismissed case (ADEA case) to avoid the statute of limitations and the limitation period for filing a new ADEA challenge or a new application under the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA). The Defendant is seeking to reopen the case under Rule 60(b)(6) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, arguing that the application of the limitation periods to this case is inequitable given the circumstances. The Defendant's goal is to reopen the case to address issues that were not addressed in the previous case, and to argue that the district court has jurisdiction to consider the 60(b) motion and to vacate an arbitrable award, even though the case is no longer an ADEA case.

Dialogue:
Judge: Tell me what happened.
Plaintiff: Okay, so how many children did they have there?
Plaintiff: One child.
Judge: One child? How old was the child?
Plaintiff: She was 2 and a half at the time.
Judge: Okay, so what happened?
Plaintiff: She put in her 30-day notice because of an incident.
Judge: Tell me about that.
Plaintiff: She had come to pick up her daughter at 6:00, and I had already told her in advance that she hadn’t had a nap the whole day. And when she put her into the car, she had a meltdown. She took her out of the car and faced her towards my daycare, and she said, “You’re in timeout.”
Judge: This is before the notice?
Plaintiff: Yes, this was the day before the notice. Meanwhile, Amelia is standing there screaming at the top of her lungs, and my husband got sick of hearing it, so he went outside and signaled for her to come to the daycare area. He signaled for who to come to the daycare?
Plaintiff: He signaled for Naomi to come. He goes, “Come here.” She came to him, and he said, “This can’t happen here.” And she looked at him and—I heard this from my window—she said, “Come on, Amelia, let’s go.” So they went to the car and drove off.
Judge: Later on that night, I got a text message saying that we needed to talk about the incident that happened with Phil, my husband. So we met at Starbucks. She discussed that it was inappropriate how Phil talked to her.
Judge: Well, I mean, I wouldn’t like somebody to go like that to me either. You know, it’s a little bit rude, what, saying, “Come here”?
Plaintiff: Yeah.
Judge: Okay. Well, we’re getting far astray of things. So, for whatever reason, she decides she’s leaving. This has nothing to do with them, but I thought you were going to start telling me when you said the incident about the problem where your friend was thrown out.
Plaintiff: Audrey, the other little girl got it. There was a problem between you and the dad. It was a communication problem, and I terminated him.
Judge: Okay. And that child is your daughter’s best friend, and it was sort of unpleasant.
Plaintiff: Correct.
Judge: Oh yeah, it was very... Am I correct from reading your material that you basically texted the dad and said, “Your daughter’s stuff will be out on the porch, pick it up by 5:30, you’re not welcome here. I’m terminating you.”
Plaintiff: That’s a little rude, don’t you think?
Defendant: What’s rude? What I just said?
Judge: Yes, that sounds a bit rude.
Defendant: No, I don’t think so. Because we had already had a discussion. You give a 30-day notice. I did. The contract requires it. And then you pay the first half of the month, and then on the 15th, you pay the second half of the month, and then you stop payment on that check.
Judge: Why?
Defendant: When I dropped off the check on the 15th, the morning of, I spoke with Nicole and said, “I’m going to be coming late.” At 6:00, I come to pick my daughter up, and there’s an access code that lets you into the daycare. So I put the access code in; it didn’t work, so I couldn’t get in to my child. So I hollered over the fence and got one of the kids’ attention to let them know that I’m here and to have somebody open the gate.
Judge: There’s a doorbell to ring.
Defendant: Okay. And they were outside anyways. So we opened the gate for them. I said, “Nicole, the key code doesn’t work. Any… you know, it didn’t work.” She said, “I changed it.” So I asked her, “What’s the code?” And she said, “I can’t give it to you.” She said that I was still associated with Emily and Justin, the Audrey’s dad and his girlfriend, because I had picked up Audrey’s stuff. They called me and asked me to pick up the stuff because it was sitting outside on the ground. She said when I picked up that bag, I chose Justin and Emily, and that she couldn’t trust me with the code.
Judge: At that point, I said, “This is unacceptable.” You know, you denied me access to my child, not giving me notice. I said, “I’m not coming back. I’m putting a stop payment on the check.” She said, “Go ahead,” and I left.
Judge: Tell me about this. What happened?
Defendant: So the defense here is this daycare person was really rude to the parents. Is that a defense to not paying for the services?
Judge: No. Why?
Plaintiff: Because attitude has nothing to do with her services.
Judge: Is that true for daycare? Attitude has nothing to do with it?
Defendant: What do you—who’s a parent? Who’s a parent?
Judge: Quick, quick, quick, what do you think? Does attitude have anything to do with it?
Plaintiff: I don’t think so.
Defendant: No.
Judge: No? Well, therefore, a parent has spoken. There you go. Going inside the courtroom.
Defendant: She asked me for the code, just like she said, and I told her that I was not giving her the code because she was too closely involved with Justin and Emily.
Judge: How? By doing what?
Defendant: By serving as a broker to make sure the two of you don’t have contact and picking up the stuff that you very—I think rudely—just put on the porch.
Judge: No, I was worried that the code was going to be breached, and I didn’t want someone else to have the code to my home and come in and vandalize my property. They made threatening phone calls.
Judge: Why would you conclude that that might happen?
Defendant: Because he called after I terminated him. He called and made threatening phone calls.
Judge: Saying what?
Defendant: “You’ve made the wrong decision by terminating my daughter. I’m going to put bad reviews over the internet about you.”
Judge: Oh, why do people think that’s threats?
Defendant: Threats is, “I’m going to break your legs.”
Judge: Well, no, of course. I think I understand what you’re saying. You know, you felt unsafe, and you didn’t want somebody you have bad blood with potentially doing something like that. You have kids there, so you don’t want him having the code. What do I do with the idea that you made her feel like…?
Defendant: Wait, wait, wait, wait. I’m a paying customer. All of a sudden, she’s a persona non grata, and why should she have to patronize you for two more weeks and abide by the contract when you’ve changed the rules on her, telling her, “I don’t want you to have the code”?
Defendant: I was more than willing to open the door. You just felt offended, like you felt like it was rude.
Plaintiff: Well, and also, too, there’s two days that had gone by, and she never notified us.
Judge: Well, apparently, it hadn’t affected you.
Plaintiff: Yeah, because I didn’t think it was that big of a deal.
Judge: I have to sort of consider that when I’m trying to figure out how big a deal this is. I think what it really is is the insult. I mean, maybe I’m wrong, but it felt like an insult to you.
Defendant: And there was no…
Judge: Did you really say you picked Justin?
Defendant: What I said was, when you picked up the bag, she said, “I don’t want to get in the middle of you, Justin, and Emily.” I said, “When you picked up that bag…”
Judge: You really have an attitude! You must be really good at what you do because I’d have dropped you in a week. You must be absolutely fabulous at daycare if people are there with you a whole year. You do a great job with the kids. I don’t… because there was a lot of good.
Defendant: It’s got to be. It’s got to be because otherwise I wouldn’t tolerate people talking to me like that. Let me hear from you.
Plaintiff: I’m sorry. I think it was a change in atmosphere that we felt. If they were going to treat us this way, what would they be treating our child like?
Judge: Yeah, but you had no evidence they were going to treat her that way.
Defendant: No, that’s true. Don’t get me wrong. You know, you as parents have the obligation and responsibility to make the decisions that you think are best for your child. What I have to decide is, is that a material breach of the contract that you don’t have to abide by the contract?
Judge: And I really, I don’t—I don’t find it to be such a material breach. It’s insulting and everything, and I agree with you, I would feel insulted. But all you have to do is call ahead like you have before and give a little holler, and they’ll come over. In other words, I would have done the same thing you were doing, and I would not have stopped payment, I guess is what I’m saying.
Judge: I’m not going to order them to pay $500 in late fees because when the late fees are more than the fees, that’s excessive. So I am going to order them to pay the $341.25 plus your board cost. That’s my judgment. Good luck.
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff's statements suggest that they had a daycare arrangement for their 2.5-year-old daughter. An incident occurred where the Plaintiff's husband spoke to the Defendant (daycare provider) in a manner that the Plaintiff found inappropriate. This incident led to the Plaintiff giving a 30-day notice to terminate the daycare services.

The Plaintiff's goal or motive behind their words appears to be seeking justice for the perceived disrespect shown by the Defendant towards them and their family, particularly the incident involving their daughter and the subsequent termination of their daycare arrangement.
DEFENDANT:

The Defendant also mentions a history of threatening phone calls from the father, which they believe justifies their concern about the Plaintiff giving the access code to someone who might vandalize their property.

The Defendant's goal or motive behind their words appears to be to justify their actions, particularly the decision to stop payment on the check and leave the daycare, by emphasizing the perceived rudeness and disrespect of the Plaintiff.

Dialogue:
Judge: Is your position— is there any daylight between Texas's position and the government's position here?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Well, some—
Judge: You're representing the government?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yeah. To the extent there was a suggestion that there could be an injunction to pay money, we would disagree with that because of the Appropriations Clause, I think. The Fifth Amendment cannot be read—
Judge: So would it be—
Defendant: 's Lawyer: —to allow that.
Judge: —a matter of semantics, you can't take this property? You have to stop flooding it? You have to do—
Defendant: 's Lawyer: You have to—you have to stop whatever it is that would constitute a taking. And—
Judge: Right. And just to clarify your answer to the previous question in my head, you're saying it's the Tucker Act plus the statute—
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yes.
Judge: —mandating payment that gets you into court?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: That is—that's—that's correct, and it's certainly not the—it's certainly not the other provision itself, the just—the just compensation clause or the other statute, which by definition—
Judge: So that's your half-point/half-point—
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yes.
Judge: —equals one?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yes.
Judge: Okay.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Sorry.
Judge: Any other questions?
Judge: Two questions. First, the rogue state example—why shouldn't we worry about that? Why shouldn't we worry about the incentive structure we create that would allow states to withdraw compensation schemes, and maybe the federal government too, to exploit this loophole?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: With respect, it's not a loophole. It's a—it's a fundamental aspect of the Constitution that the Constitution does not—does not require this. And the rogue state is answered by the fact that it's a prohibition, and if—if Congress does not provide the condition necessary to render it lawful, you have an injunction—injunctive action. And as the Court said in Knick, that was the way—
Judge: Okay.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: —that just compensation issues were raised before.
Judge: Okay. And then, second, this may be a question better directed to the other party when they speak on rebuttal, but the procedural history of this case is interesting. The complaint has two counts about takings. One is under the state constitution, and the other is under the federal Constitution.
How do we read what the Fifth Circuit did here? Did it only dismiss the second, the federal claim, and is the first claim under, what is it, City of Bayview and the Texas Constitution, still live? Do they even need to amend their complaint to add it? Is it already there?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: There's a footnote in the court of appeals' opinion that says that the Texas Constitution or Texas provides a cause of action. And that is not further elaborated upon, but it's—
Judge: No. Exactly.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: —it's remanded for further proceedings.
Judge: So do you take it that that first count under the state constitution is still alive and available to the plaintiffs?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I—it is still alive and available. If it required an amendment to the complaint, I—
Judge: Do you think it requires an amendment—
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I—
Judge: —to the complaint, or because it was remanded for further proceedings and the court only expressly addressed the federal Constitution, that that first count is still alive?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I think it would depend on whether that first count, in— in relying on the state constitution, was just relying on a state substantive right to compensation or whether it was also relying—
Judge: Well, Texas—
Defendant: 's Lawyer: —on a cause of action.
Judge: —has represented to us that it provides a cause of action—
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Right. And—
Judge: —right? So—
Defendant: 's Lawyer: —so, yes, if—if—if the complaint is read to be invoking the state cause of action for the federal taking, then, yes, I think that would be open on remand.
Judge: Thank you.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff's statements suggest that they are representing the government and are in disagreement with the idea of an injunction to pay money due to the Appropriations Clause and the Fifth Amendment. They argue that the Constitution does not require this, and it's not a loophole but a fundamental aspect of the Constitution. The Plaintiff also clarifies that the first count under the state constitution, which is still alive and available to the plaintiffs, is relying on a state substantive right to compensation, not just a cause of action.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant's lawyer's statements suggest that they disagree with the possibility of an injunction to pay money due to the Appropriations Clause and the Fifth Amendment. They argue that the Constitution does not require this, and it's not a loophole but a fundamental aspect of the Constitution. They also clarify that the first count under the state constitution, which is still alive and available to the plaintiffs, is relying on a state substantive right to compensation, not just a cause of action.

Judge: So I'm just still wondering if your theory about this provision might be too narrow in a sense because you've got evidence going and spoliation in a sense. What I’m trying to work out in my mind is whether you would still have a decent argument if this 1512 language is read to prohibit the corrupt tampering with things that are used to conduct an official proceeding with the intent of undermining the integrity of the thing or access to the thing and thereby obstructing the proceeding. It’s not just evidence. It’s a proceeding. (c)(1) is an example of, you know, the corrupt tampering with certain things. And (c)(2) broadens it out a bit. It’s not just documents and records. What do you think about that?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Well, I think that’s a correct reading, Your Honor. I mean, as 1512(f) demonstrates, it doesn’t—1512(f) we would submit actually supports our position because it says the evidence need not be admissible or free of a privilege claim. Now what would that mean about what the statute is addressing if it’s not evidence? But (c)(2) has been applied, and occasionally (c)(1) has been applied.
Judge: In a non-evidentiary way?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yeah, to things that could become evidence, to the efforts to shape someone’s grand jury testimony.
Judge: All right.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: To answers to interrogatories.
Judge: Let me ask you about the question that was asked earlier. You suggested that it has to be to the document, but—in other words, the activity has to be actually to the document, but I don’t know why that’s the case under (c)(2). Justice Alito says, well, one of the commonalities between (c)(1) and (c)(2) could be the impairment of the object’s integrity or availability. Justice Barrett posits a scenario in which you have someone who is impairing the availability by doing something to prevent the object from getting to the proceeding. Why wouldn’t that count under (c)(2)? So this is—this is, you know, preventing Congress from counting the electoral votes, for example. Let’s say it’s being done. She says it’s in an envelope going to the— the vice president’s desk and someone does something to impair or prevent that from happening. Why isn’t that what (c)(2) could cover?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Well, first, it’s not affecting the integrity of the document, Your Honor, or—the—
Judge: Availability is also in the statute.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Availability it says too, but, as I mentioned earlier, simply delaying the arrival of evidence at the courthouse—
Judge: No, not delay. Let’s say the person steals the envelope and takes it away.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Then it gets harder, I agree. If they steal the envelope, they take it away, they rip it up, all of those things, which is certainly not what happened here, and it’s not in the indictment, the—the ballots or the—the vote count is not even in the indictment.
Judge: Well, we wouldn’t have to decide that.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Right.
Judge: We could send it back if we clarified that that is what the statute means. I’m trying to understand if you agree that that’s what the statute could mean.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: No, I don’t agree that that’s what the statute could mean.
Judge: Why not?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: The reason is that if you look at 1512, it is about a direct effect or, in some senses, an indirect effect but in a limited way on evidence that’s to be used in a proceeding, right, and—and "proceeding," as I mentioned earlier—
Judge: So as to limit its availability.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: So as to limit—
Judge: I’m suggesting is, in (c)(2), if you’re doing something to limit its— to—to limit its availability, why doesn’t it count?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Because we’re limiting the availability of its use by a fact finder in a proceeding. Again, that’s the way to marry 1519, which covers all kinds of investigations and all kinds of other events, with 1512. 1512 is talking about evidence that’s going to a formal convocation, some kind of hearing, before the Congress or before any other body—
Judge: Thank you.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Thank you.
Judge: Thank you, counsel.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Thank you.
Judge: General, please proceed.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: On January 6th, 2021, a violent mob stormed the United States Capitol and disrupted the peaceful transition of power. Many crimes occurred that day, but in plain English, the fundamental wrong committed by many of the rioters, including Petitioner, was a deliberate attempt to stop the joint session of Congress from certifying the results of the election. That is, they obstructed Congress’s work in that proceeding. The government accordingly charged Petitioner with violating Section 1512(c)(2), an obstruction offense that directly reads onto his conduct. The case as it comes to this Court presents a straightforward question of statutory interpretation: Did Petitioner obstruct, influence, or impede the joint session of Congress? The answer is equally straightforward. Yes, he obstructed that official proceeding. The terms of the statute unambiguously encompass his conduct. Petitioner doesn’t really argue that his actions fall outside the plain meaning of what it is to obstruct. Instead, he asks this Court to impose an atextual limit on the actus reus. In his view, because Section 1512(c)(1) covers tampering with documents and other physical evidence, the separate prohibition in Section 1512(c)(2) should be limited to acts of evidence impairment. But that limit has no basis in the text or tools of construction. His reading hinges on the word "otherwise," but that word means in a different manner, not in the same manner. And the two prohibitions in Section 1512(c)(2) aren’t unified items on a list where you could apply associated words canons. They’re separate provisions. They have their own sets of verbs and their own nouns. They each independently prohibit attempts, which would be duplication that makes no sense on Petitioner’s reading. And Congress included a distinct mental state requirement in (c)(1) that it chose not to repeat in (c)(2). Section 1512(c)(2) by its terms is not limited to evidence impairment. Instead, it’s a classic catchall. (c)(1) covers specified acts that obstruct an official proceeding, and (c)(2) covers all other acts that obstruct an official proceeding in a different manner. The Court should say so and allow this case to proceed to trial. I welcome the Court’s questions.
Judge: General, there have been many violent protests that have interfered with proceedings. Has the government applied this provision to other protests in the past, and has this been the government's position throughout the lifespan of this statute?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: It has certainly been the government's position since the enactment of 1512(c)(2) that it covers the myriad forms of obstructing an official proceeding and that it's not limited to some kind of evidence impairment gloss.
Judge: Have you—so have you—have you enforced it in that manner?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: We have enforced it in a variety of prosecutions that don’t focus on evidence tampering. Now I can’t give you an example of enforcing it in a situation where people have violently stormed a building in order to prevent an official proceeding, a specified one, from occurring with all of the elements like intent to obstruct, knowledge of the proceeding, having the corruptly mens rea, but that’s just because I’m not aware of that circumstance ever happening prior to January 6th. But just to give you flavor—you a flavor of some of the other circumstances where we have prosecuted under this provision, for example, there are situations where we’ve brought (c)(2) charges because someone tipped off the subject of an investigation to the grand jury's hearings. There was another case where someone tipped off about the identity of an undercover law enforcement officer. And in those situations, there’s no specific evidence, no, you know, concrete testimony or physical evidence that the conduct is interfering with. Instead, it’s more general obstruction of the proceeding.

The Plaintiff's goal or motive behind their words is to convince the court that the Defendant's actions were a deliberate attempt to stop the joint session of Congress from certifying the results of the election, and therefore, the Defendant violated Section 1512(c)(2).

DEFENDANT: The Defendant's lawyer argues that the statute (1512) is intended to protect the integrity and availability of evidence used in formal proceedings, specifically those before Congress or other bodies. They contend that the statute is not meant to cover actions that limit the availability of objects or documents outside of the context of a formal proceeding. Judge: And Franklin, I think, is kind of our north star here at least as I've unpacked the case.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Right. But -- but I think Franklin, you could either read it as being a case about significant interference based on the record, as I pointed out, or I think what this Court said is it just engaged in statutory interpretation. It said we've got a federal statute that says national banks may accept savings deposits and the word "savings" matters. It's the label that Congress used for these accounts. And states can't pose a serious practical impediment to that by saying you can't use that same label. And so that case could be understood on statutory construction grounds based on the express statutory power that was granted by the statute, and we have nothing like that here.
Judge: Thank you, counsel. Anything further?
Judge: Well, the way you just described Franklin sounds to me an awful lot like what the Second Circuit did here.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: No.
Judge: They -- they -- they said that the bank has, the national bank has a certain power, and the state conditions the exercise of that national power on compliance with a state requirement, and that's enough to prove that there's preemption. That's what I just understood you to say.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: No, Judge. If -- my understanding of what the --
Judge: I must have -- maybe I -- I misunderstood you, so maybe you could just clarify.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: No, I was -- I was simply trying to clarify that Franklin National Bank could be understood based on specific statutory text that is nothing like any statutory text that Bank of America has identified.
Judge: I thought you were saying -- and, again, correct me if I misunderstood you because it's important to my thinking about this -- that the issue -- that Franklin Bank can be understood as deciding this issue without examining the empirical question of the extent to which there was an impact on the operation of the bank. I thought that's what you said.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I guess I would put it a little -- little bit differently then, Judge. I think that the Court, in its opinion, it notes the -- the -- the record that had been amassed on this question as to the practical consequences of the law, and I think that record gave it some comfort and confirmed why it was significant that Congress would have used the statutory term "savings." But, ultimately, its opinion rests on, you know, statutory analysis of the word "savings" and a specific statutory interpretation that is -- would present a sort of -- I mean, you could think of it as being a conflict in that -- in that sense and is nothing like the kind of conflict that we have here.
Judge: All right. Thank you.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Thank you.
Judge: The government asked us to vacate and remand and let the Second Circuit apply whatever we say is the correct test.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Mm-hmm.
Judge: You're asking us to reverse. What's the difference, and why don't we do what the -- the U.S. is recommending?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: We would be happy with a vacatur, and I think it's the most modest way for this Court to decide the -- the question before it. The reason why we're asking for reversal is we think that as long as there's a requirement that the practical effect of the law be examined, that Bank of America has failed to make that showing, and since it's failed to make that showing, then its motion to dismiss should be denied, and it can make the showing at a later stage of the litigation or put in some declarations or something and seek summary judgment if it thinks it can meet --
Judge: I don't know whether I --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: -- the statutory --
Judge: I mean --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: But I don't think --
Judge: -- the statute doesn't speak in terms of practical effects. It talks about preventing or significantly interfering with the exercise of a national bank power. So I do think that there is a difference between practical effect and that language.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Well, I think that language, in ordinary parlance, could only be understood to -- to say that to be able to answer that question, you've got to know what the practical effect of the law is. And you don't have to necessarily know what the degree is. I mean, that -- you know, people can disagree about that, but, at a minimum, you've got to -- it's got to take some account of what the practical effect is. And once you recognize that --
Judge: So your -- at what point -- you mentioned earlier that the OCC could decide some of these preemption issues because, under your take of this law now, that national banks -- all state laws would apparently apply to national banks, unless and until those banks obtain final judgments of preemption state by state, correct?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I think that is correct, but --
Judge: Now the other side is saying that's an alarming unpredictability. And some of my colleagues are concerned about that. Why don't you address that straight on and -- but you mentioned in your opening that you thought the OCC could do it. Well, the OCC has done it here. There's a question of whether they've applied the right standard in doing it.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yeah.
Judge: But they have done it.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Well, the statute -- they haven't done it consistent with the procedures set up by the statute, and I don't even think Bank of America is arguing they've done it consistent with the procedures set up by -- by the statute. But if -- I think it would be appropriate for a court on remand to look at what the OCC has said about the effect of this law. You'll find that there's not much there in -- in either the 2011 rulemaking or the 2004 rulemaking or in the amicus brief that the OCC submitted below. But we think, you know, it would be appropriate for a court to consider that as part of the analysis.
Judge: Whether we like the case-by-case approach, the statute requires it?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: The statute requires it.
Judge: I think I would have expected you to say that --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Oh.
Judge: -- to start off.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: We -- we certainly think that you should read the statute and apply it as written.
Judge: Okay. Could you give me an example of a non-discriminatory state law that would be preempted as a significant interference?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I don't know that I can answer that question in the abstract. But, I mean, I -- well, I guess I can. Barnett Bank would be -- would be an example. So even if that's non-discriminatory, it poses a clear conflict because of the total --
Judge: Yeah. So you've separated that out as a case that poses a clear conflict.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff's (Defendant's Lawyer) statements suggest that they are arguing for a reversal of the court's decision in favor of Bank of America, rather than vacating and remanding the case to the Second Circuit. The Plaintiff argues that the court's decision in Franklin National Bank, which the judge seems to compare the case at hand to, is based on a specific statutory interpretation, whereas the case at hand does not have the same basis. The Plaintiff argues that the court should reconsider its decision because Bank of America has not made a sufficient showing that the state law significantly interferes with the exercise of a national bank's power, as required by the statute.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant's lawyer argues that the case at hand is not similar to the Franklin case, as the issue in this case does not involve a specific statutory text like in Franklin. Instead, the Defendant's lawyer suggests that the case should be decided based on the practical consequences of the law, which the Defendant argues has not been adequately demonstrated by the government. The Defendant's lawyer also argues that the OCC, which has the power to decide preemption issues, has not followed the proper procedures in doing so.

Judge: And, Your Honor, again, it is a concern -- two responses. First, it is a concern that can be addressed directly.

Judge: Unless there's a divestiture?
Lawyer of Defendant: Unless there's a divestiture.

Lawyer of Defendant: Mm-hmm.

Dialogue:
Judge: Miss Johnson, you and your business Retro Girls Hawaiian Ice Shack are suing Miss Hassan and her business, Main Experience. Miss Hassan, you're a hairstylist, and you're counterclaiming for $10,000 for harassment, stalking, defamation, and property damage. Let's hear from you first, Miss Johnson. What is going on?
Plaintiff: I moved into my storefront in the fall of 2015 or leased it in 2016. I was there to open up a storefront so that I could help the community. I do a lot of givebacks in the community. I host storytime for children, breast cancer awareness dinners, and I sell ice cream.
Judge: So it's a dessert shop, but you do other community outreach as well. Do you also live there?
Plaintiff: Yes, I live upstairs from the storefront. Main Experience is like 10 or 15 feet across from my door.
Judge: So are they next door or a couple of stores down?
Plaintiff: Next door. Right next door.
Judge: Okay. When did the problems start between you two?
Plaintiff: The problem started before Miss Hassan moved in, originally with the previous owner of the hair salon in the spring of 2016.
Judge: Is that the present owner?
Plaintiff: That’s the current owner.
Judge: Is she in court today?
Plaintiff: No, she is not.
Judge: All right, so when Miss Hassan moves in, you start not getting along with her next door. Why?
Plaintiff: She moved in, and she would be in the storefront until 2 or 3 AM, playing loud music, making noise, cursing, and her children would be outside running around being disruptive. She and her daughter would have fights in and outside the storefront. It was very disruptive to the quiet area we had before.
Judge: So then how do we get to a problem with her specifically? Because you're suing her, not the prior owner.
Plaintiff: When she moved into the hair salon in December 2017, it wasn’t an issue at first. But when she started living inside the shop, she would come in at 2 or 3 AM, slamming doors, making noise, which woke me up.
Judge: Did you call the police?
Plaintiff: Yes, very often for noise violations.
Judge: Do you have any reports showing the police took action?
Plaintiff: The police came and spoke with her. One officer said he would cite her for loud noise.
Judge: Were you ever cited for noise violation, Miss Hassan?
Defendant: No, I was never cited.
Judge: Do you have proof that she was cited, Miss Johnson?
Plaintiff: No, I just heard the officer say he would.
Judge: Then what happened next?
Plaintiff: We were hosting a Juneteenth celebration in my storefront. A patron of hers parked illegally for six hours in a five-minute parking zone. When the ticket officer arrived, they assumed I had called them. Her patron confronted me, cursing at me.
Judge: But you’re suing her, not her patron. Are business owners responsible for their patrons’ actions?
Plaintiff: She also put trash in front of my storefront.
Judge: Do you have proof of her doing that?
Plaintiff: I have images of her family members putting trash.
Judge: But do you have proof of *her* putting trash there?
Plaintiff: Not directly, but I have a security camera from December 2016.
Judge: Do you have video of her putting trash there?
Plaintiff: I have images of her family members doing it.
Judge: What about the harassment, stalking, and defamation claims?
Plaintiff: I have images of her filming my storefront and walking past.
Judge: Pick your best video and show me.
Defendant: Your Honor, I don’t know where this hostility comes from. When I first moved in and rented a booth in 2016, I noticed Miss Johnson staring at me through the salon window for hours. I asked the salon owner about it, and she said there were already issues with her.
Judge: You call yourself a celebrity hairstylist. What does that mean?
Defendant: I work with celebrities, have been published in magazines, and have done hair for people on *Housewives* and *Love & Hip Hop*.
Judge: Who is the woman sitting with you?
Defendant: My mother. She has witnessed much of what has happened.
Judge: So what is your side of this?
Defendant: I ignore her, but she calls the police on me constantly, stopping me from working. The police never find any loud music.
Judge: How often has she called the police on you?
Defendant: Countless times. The police advised the salon owner to get a restraining order against her.
Judge: Why do you believe you’re entitled to $10,000 from her?
Defendant: She’s affected my business. In the past two months, I’ve been filming a reality show at my salon. Because of her police calls on October 25th and November 3rd, I had to move filming to other locations, costing me money.
Judge: Miss Johnson, this video of you filming her car—why were you doing that?
Plaintiff: I saw her leave and come back an hour later. I didn’t understand why, so I recorded it.
Judge: Miss Hassan, you’re in your car, and she’s filming you. What do you think?
Defendant: I don’t know why she does this. It feels like harassment.
Judge: Miss Johnson, why are you rolling up on her car and taking video?
Plaintiff: I wanted to document what was happening.
Judge: Do either of you sleep? According to you, Miss Johnson, you closed your business because of this drama?
Plaintiff: Yes, after I was physically assaulted by her and the prior owner’s daughter. I was injured—my wrist and knee. My patrons stopped coming because of the drama.
Judge: Did you have them arrested?
Plaintiff: The officer didn’t arrest them because Miss Hassan claimed she saw the fight, but video footage showed she wasn’t even there.
Judge: You both have stacks of letters from people taking sides. How do you expect me to determine who is at fault?
Judge: I believe in open access to the courts, but neither of you has presented clear proof. You have conflicting videos, police reports that led nowhere, and letters from neighbors picking sides. This is not something I can resolve in a court of law. I rule *zero* for you, Miss Johnson, and *zero* for you, Miss Hassan.
Judge: I suggest you both move forward and stop this feud. Wouldn’t it be great to focus on your businesses instead? Wipe the slate clean and let it go.
Defendant: I’m happy with the ruling and just want peace.
Plaintiff: I want peace too. We had peace before she moved in. I hope we can have it again.
Judge: Good luck to both of you. Case dismissed.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff, Miss Johnson, is suing Miss Hassan for noise violations, property damage, and defamation. She claims that Miss Hassan moved into the storefront next to her in December 2017 and began causing disruptions, such as playing loud music, making noise, and having arguments, which affected the peaceful community they previously had. The Plaintiff alleges that Miss Hassan has also parked illegally, put trash in front of her store, and filmed her storefront.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant, Miss Hassan, is a hairstylist who operates a business called Main Experience, which is located next door to the Plaintiff's business, Retro Girls Hawaiian Ice Shack. The Plaintiff lives upstairs from her storefront. The Defendant claims that the problems between them started before she moved in, with the previous owner of the hair salon in the spring of 2016.

Dialogue:
Judge: There's no argument that the final hearing they received here was a kangaroo court or was in any way insufficient.
Judge: But could those claims—do you acknowledge there might be claims like that to be had?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: There may be, Your Honor. If you're requiring someone to reach a decision between 3 and 5:00, that's not a very good reason under the legal framework for delay.
Judge: No, but let's say it happens really quickly, but it's a kangaroo court, an unfair adjudication. You and I would agree that that was wholly and grossly unfair?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yes, Your Honor, but I think we're far removed from that scenario.
Judge: Of course, we are. In your case, of course, you're going to say that.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Right.
Judge: But your argument would seem to strip the courts of tools to deal with those kinds of cases.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I don't think so, Your Honor. Keep in mind—
Judge: Help me write it so that we don’t do that.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Well, because in past cases, the final hearing was going to be by a federal judge. You can trust that they are going to uphold the Constitution, they're going to do justice. We shouldn’t craft a test that suggests otherwise.
Judge: So let me see if you’re comfortable with this: So long as the processes that are ultimately given are of the sort that are traditionally used for forfeiture and are reasonably fair and comport with traditional due process principles? Something like that?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yes, Your Honor.
Judge: Something like that?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Absolutely.
Judge: But maybe that’s not enough. I mean, I’m sympathetic to your point that this is about how long it takes until someone can get an adjudication and their property back. That’s why people want a retention hearing—because it takes too long, even under the current framework, to have the final adjudication.
Judge: We, in fact, have not decided this precise question. We could say that, despite similarities to past cases, this remains open to decide whether there ought to be a retention hearing in addition to the final adjudication.
Judge: And we know more now than we did when past cases were decided—about how civil forfeiture is being used in some states, the abuses it's subject to, and the incentives operating on law enforcement officers that tend toward those abuses.
Judge: So if we look around and see real problems that would be solved with a quick probable cause determination, why shouldn’t we do that?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Well, Your Honor, I would advise staying within the record of the case. There was ample process provided here. They could have posted a bond at any time to get the vehicles back.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: If we rush the final hearing, it will cause serious problems. Speed sacrifices accuracy. The stakes in civil forfeiture are high—the government has a strong interest in full forfeiture and ensuring that crime doesn’t pay.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: If you have a less accurate retention hearing—essentially a mini-trial that’s faster but less precise—more property will be released to criminals, potentially misused again, and crime will increase.
Judge: Why? First, I doubt that criminal defendants from whom cars have been taken will seek a retention hearing because whatever they say could be used against them in a criminal case.
Judge: These cases matter most to one group—innocent owners. Many involve parents whose teenage children were involved in drug activity. Others involve spouses or friends.
Judge: The government can present evidence ex parte if they suspect the owner is not innocent. But in the majority of cases, statistics show over 60 percent of innocent owners win, and they only get their cars back months or years later.
Judge: So where does the legal framework account for those interests? It doesn’t.
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff's statements suggest that they are involved in a case involving civil forfeiture, where property has been seized by the government. The Plaintiff argues that the current legal framework for final hearings, which may result in a quick decision, is sufficient and does not require additional hearings such as a retention hearing. The Plaintiff's goal or motive behind their words is to maintain the status quo and prevent the courts from stripping themselves of tools to deal with potentially unfair adjudications.

The Plaintiff believes that the current process, which allows for a final hearing by a federal judge, is adequate to uphold the Constitution and ensure justice.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant's lawyer's statements suggest that they are advocating for a fair and reasonable process in civil forfeiture cases, emphasizing the importance of due process and the potential risks of rushing the final hearing. The Defendant's lawyer argues that a quick probable cause determination could lead to inaccuracies, releasing potentially misused property to criminals and increasing crime. They also highlight the potential harm to innocent owners, particularly those whose children, spouses, or friends are involved in drug-related activities.

In order to get global content, we need access to the Irish videos.
They need access to the U.S. videos.

Lawyer of Defendant: Well, Your Honor, I think it would be extraordinarily difficult.

Judge: Mr. Francisco, can I ask you a question about the relevant speech here? So it strikes me that this is a little different than your Bezos example because, there, it's clearly content discrimination because we're talking about the ability to post particular articles versus other articles.

A lot of your examples talk about, including the Bezos one --
Lawyer of Defendant: Mm-hmm.
Judge: -- the right of an American citizen to repeat what a foreign entity says or say, you know, I'm hitching my wagon to China; I want to say everything China does.
Here, the concern is about the covert content manipulation piece of the algorithm.
Lawyer of Defendant: Mm-hmm.
Judge: That is something that ByteDance wants to speak, right?
Lawyer of Defendant: Well, Your Honor, I think that ultimately it's TikTok's choice whether to put it on the platform.
And --
Judge: And you don't want that? Are you --is your client disclaiming any --
Lawyer of Defendant: We --we -- we absolutely resist any kind of content manipulation by China at all, but what I do want to focus in on is what -- their asserted interests here.
They do talk about covertness. But it can't possibly be that all they're concerned about is mere covertness.
If all you were concerned about was the covertness untethered from the underlying content, that's something that could be easily addressed through a risk disclosure.
Judge: But that goes to scrutiny, the level of --
Lawyer of Defendant: Yes, Your Honor.
Judge: -- the application. I'm trying to -- I mean, let's say that I agree with you the First Amendment is implicated, and I'm trying to figure out what level of scrutiny applies.
Lawyer of Defendant: Sure.
Judge: And I'm trying to figure out what content, if any, discrimination is going on here.
You know, there's a disproportionate burden.
I --
Lawyer of Defendant: Right.
Judge: Let's say that I agree with you about that.
No one is preventing you -- I mean, you're seeking access to a particular source code engineering the recommendation feature. It's --it's the technology that you want. You're not trying to repeat, as in the Bezos example --if we take the speech that the government's concerned about to be the covert -- the covert content manipulation rationale, you're not seeking to utter that speech.
Lawyer of Defendant: Well, what we're -- that's correct, Your Honor.

Second, a qualified divestiture would divorce the U.S.

Dialogue:
Judge: All parties, please rise. Are you ready?
Judge: Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket: The plaintiff was hired to help renovate the defendant’s body shop and got stiffed, but the defendant says the plaintiff was hammering him for way too much, even demanding money at a funeral. It’s the case of "I won’t rest in peace until you pay me."
Judge: Thank you, Douglas. Andrew Toll & Toll Renovations, you are suing Albert Bini for $1,800 that you say he owes you for work that was done and completed but that he never paid you for. Tell me.
Plaintiff: Yes, Your Honor. I was contracted by Al to do some work at a body shop that he owned. He was going to have his nephew renovate to work in the body shop. He had me do work at my hourly rate with another employee of mine.
Judge: How did he have you do the work? Did you guys have a contract?
Plaintiff: Verbal contract.
Judge: Okay. And verbally, what did he say he would pay you?
Plaintiff: He would pay me $35 an hour, and my employee would be $20 an hour.
Judge: And then what was the work he was asking you to do?
Plaintiff: Well, what we did was we power washed the body shop completely. We started to paint the body shop.
Judge: You started to paint. How far along did you get?
Plaintiff: 90% of it.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: There was a leak in the roof under the air conditioning condenser. I fixed all of the roof except for that.
Judge: When you say fixed the roof, what does that mean?
Plaintiff: I put down a torch-down rubber-modified roof.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: Underneath the air conditioning condenser was another source of the leak that I couldn’t fix until the air conditioning condenser was removed.
Judge: Okay. So Al found someone to rent the property and canceled all the work. Did you—now tell me everything that you’d done. Anything else to tell me about?
Plaintiff: No, that was it. I tore out a rug that Al said he didn’t want torn out. He had someone come in to rent the space and—
Judge: You tore out a rug that he didn’t want torn out?
Plaintiff: It was an old rug in the office. He thought it was a good rug. Then someone came in that was going to run the body shop for him. He wanted the rug torn out because he was going to move into that area.
Judge: So, he asked for you to rip it out?
Plaintiff: Al said whatever he wanted me to do, to take care of.
Judge: And then later got mad that the rug got discarded?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: How much did he pay you?
Plaintiff: About $4,000 or so.
Judge: And how did he pay that $4,000?
Plaintiff: Over several checks.
Judge: How many checks?
Plaintiff: About five.
Judge: About five. So, how is it that you would get paid? What was the custom?
Plaintiff: I would submit my hours to Al, and he would pay me.
Judge: And then he stopped?
Plaintiff: He paid me less than I had on the estimate or for the invoice, and then finally he just stopped paying.
Judge: Mr. Bini, why is it, according to him, you still owe him $1,800?
Defendant: Your Honor, I met Mr. Toll through my nephew. He was a personal friend of my nephew. I had a tenant move out, and all I wanted was the building painted inside and outside and some leak repairs done to the roof. I told him not to do any demolition work to the rugs in the main office because I could shampoo them and make them work for the next person to come in.
Judge: Tell me what happens. Why did you stop paying him?
Defendant: The work was substandard.
Judge: How so?
Defendant: The paint—he was billing me hourly. I didn’t know that.
Judge: What did you think he was going to bill you?
Defendant: The five checks I wrote him were just like that. But he never defined it. I kept asking him for an invoice.
Judge: Well, then why do you keep paying him?
Defendant: I stopped paying him. That’s why I stopped.
Judge: No, why did you pay him five times if that didn’t work for you?
Defendant: I had to get it rented. I can’t feed my family without the income.
Judge: So you kept having him work for you under this manner for five full checks?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: So you did do business like this. Now, apparently, the last check you gave him was for how much?
Plaintiff: Your Honor, the last check that he gave me was for $1,800. In the notation section, he wrote $2,100 minus $300—$1,800 balance.
Judge: Let me see that. And you cashed that check?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: And that was how long ago?
Plaintiff: February of last year.
Judge: Okay, well, here’s my ruling. You clearly paid him five times for work he did, so you felt five times that he earned his keep. If it was good enough for you, it's good enough for me. I find that you owe him $1,800, just like you said you did, and I’m ordering you to pay the plaintiff $1,800 plus his court costs.
Judge: And listen to me—try to comport yourself like a grown man as opposed to a child, okay? Pretend your daughter is watching you at every turn. She is. Keep an eye on him.
Court adjourned.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff, Andrew Toll & Toll Renovations, is suing Albert Bini for $1,800 that he claims Bini owes him for work done on a body shop renovation project. The work included power washing, painting, and roof repairs, with the Plaintiff and his employee working at an agreed-upon hourly rate. The Plaintiff states that he completed 90% of the work, but Bini canceled the project after renting the property.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant, Albert Bini, hired the Plaintiff, Andrew Toll & Toll Renovations, to renovate his body shop. The work included power washing, painting, and roof repairs. The Plaintiff claims that the work was completed at 90%, but the Defendant found someone to rent the property and canceled the work.

Dialogue:
Judge: Okay, Mr. Plav, you are suing Mrs. Cernic for $623.41, that you say she owes you for different jobs that you did for her husband. According to you, her husband never paid. Now, your husband is deceased, correct?
Plaintiff: Yeah.
Judge: How long ago?
Plaintiff: 2010, 2011, January 2010.
Judge: Okay, so about a year and a half ago?
Plaintiff: Yeah.
Judge: I’ll keep my voice up. Alright, so what’s going on?
Plaintiff: In July of 2010, Mr. Cernic contacted me that his van could not be inspected for pollution because the odometer did not work. It was not unusual that he called me, because over the 25 years that I've known him, without exaggeration, I’ve done over 100 jobs for him in that time. I said, "Bring it over, and I’ll see what I can do with it." Well, according to you, you were good friends with him?
Plaintiff: That’s correct.
Plaintiff: I told him to bring the van over, and I would see what I could do about repairing it. He brought it over, and he said that while it was there, he wanted me to replace the tailpipe on the van and change the air cleaner. I said I’d take care of it. Well, this was all in July of 2010, first week of July.
Judge: Okay, go on.
Plaintiff: I repaired the tailpipe and the air cleaner, and I changed the speedometer sensor, but it didn’t fix the problem. I told Mr. Cernic that the dashboard would have to be removed and repaired. The speedometer in the dashboard was the problem. I priced it with Ford, and they told me it would be about $300 to have it repaired, but they couldn’t replace it because it was obsolete and they couldn’t get a new one. I tried to buy a used one in a junkyard, but they only used that speedometer in that one-year van, and it was very rare, so I couldn’t find a used one. I told Mr. Cernic this, and he said to get it repaired.
Plaintiff: I took it to Park City Ford in Bridgeport, and a week later they called me that they had it. I went and picked it up, and I started to put it in. But before I could finish the job, I ran into a bit of a problem—well, rather, a problem ran into me. A car T-boned me on my motorcycle.
Judge: Oh my goodness, that sounds bad.
Plaintiff: It broke my leg, six ribs in multiple places, my clavicle, my shoulder, and I collapsed a lung. I was hospitalized in critical condition in intensive care for three days, and then I was transferred to a nursing home for how long?
Plaintiff: I was in two different nursing homes for three months.
Judge: Okay, let me ask you a question. You started to say something, and I want to make sure I’m following. You said, "I brought it back in before I could work on it." I thought you said that you had taken it somewhere to be worked on?
Plaintiff: I took the odometer out and gave it to them, not the car, just the odometer.
Judge: Okay, so the last piece was that you were going to put it into the car?
Plaintiff: That’s correct.
Judge: And then what happened?
Plaintiff: But I didn’t have a chance to do that because I got into the accident.
Judge: Okay, and during that time, where’s the van?
Plaintiff: In my yard.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: Mr. Cernic called me in the nursing home and asked me if there was anything I could do so he could get his van back. Obviously, I was in no shape to do anything, but I did know a friend of mine, a mechanic friend nearby, that I called, and he agreed to try to fix Mr. Cernic’s van for me and finish it. He did come and finish it, and I called Mr. Cernic back and told him the van was completed and that he could pick it up.
Plaintiff: I didn’t learn this until a couple of weeks later when, by a quirk of fate, Mr. Cernic wound up in the second nursing home that I was in, right across the hall from my room.
Judge: Oh my goodness. Okay, talk about fate. Alright, anyway, while he was in the nursing home, I went to visit him quite frequently, and he informed me that the van did pass the pollution test and everything was okay.
Judge: Okay, why are you suing two years later for this amount? Why didn’t you collect from him then?
Plaintiff: During this time, I’ve had seven major operations on my leg, and the fact that he had died...
Judge: He didn’t die until January?
Plaintiff: Right. I didn’t get out of the nursing homes until the end of October.
Judge: Okay, but you were able to visit people and talk. You could write a bill and you could...?
Plaintiff: It was pretty tough to get around. I was wearing an exoskeleton for six months.
Judge: You didn’t bill him, and then he passes away in January, and then for all this time, you don’t bill anybody, and then all of a sudden, two years later, you remember that somebody owes you money from two years ago?
Plaintiff: It wasn’t that I just remembered. I had seven major operations on my leg during that time frame, and I wasn’t really able to worry about a bill.
Judge: Okay, go on.
Plaintiff: I did go to see Mrs. Cernic in the spring of this year and I told her about the outstanding bill, and she said she’d have to talk to her son about it and she’d get back to me. Weeks went by, she never got back to me. I went there a second time, and her son happened to be mowing the grass that day. She called him in, and he confronted me and was very irate. He said, "Get out of the house immediately, and he’s going to call the police, and he ain’t paying me a nickel."
[Music playing]
Judge: The plaintiff did a bunch of work for the defendant until he almost died in a motorcycle accident. Things got really sidetracked, and now there’s an issue. The plaintiff says the defendant’s husband, who died, owed him for work, and he waited two years to press the claim. Let’s listen.
Defendant: ’s Son: Talk to me real simple. Two months before he allegedly performed work on my father’s car—and for the life of me, I don’t know how my father could have driven that car to his house or picked it up afterwards, because he was completely incapacitated mentally. He could not drive out of the driveway without my mother being with him. Physically, he could not drive that entire year.
Judge: How old was he?
Defendant: ’s Son: 84 years old.
Defendant: ’s Son: He was in rest homes and hospitals for nine months out of the 12 months in 2010.
Judge: But let me get to the point here. In May of that particular year, I heard that my father had asked this man here to cut down his neighbor’s tree. I called him up on the phone. I said, "John, you’re a good friend of my father’s. I said, ‘Please don’t do this. My father is suffering from dementia.’ There’s absolutely nothing wrong with my neighbor’s tree."
Judge: The only reason he wants to cut it down is because he planned on splitting the wood with the neighbor. So what happened?
Defendant: ’s Son: I stayed out of it because my father got involved. He said, "Listen, John’s going to cut the tree down. I don’t care what you say."
Judge: So this man didn’t respect my wishes?
Defendant: ’s Son: No, he didn’t. But karma works in strange ways.
[Music playing]
Defendant: ’s Wife: Mrs. Cernic, did your husband pay his bills right away?
Defendant: ’s Wife: Right away, and in cash.
Judge: Okay, do you remember anything about the odometer?
Defendant: ’s Wife: No, I don’t.
Judge: Okay. Let me ask you about the whole tree situation. When the son gets involved and says to you, "I would really rather you not do this. I feel like you’re taking advantage." Why did you go forward with it?
Defendant: ’s Wife: I just felt it wasn’t his right.
Judge: Okay. If we assume there is a debt, why would it be her responsibility to pay?
Plaintiff: Because she inherited his estate.
Judge: Well, you’re not suing his estate, are you?
Plaintiff: No, I was told I had to sue her, not the estate.
Judge: You were misinformed. But why would any judge look at a situation where someone has been deceased for almost two years and say, "Oh yeah, I’m sure that’s a legitimate debt with all this time that has passed?"
Plaintiff: I didn’t really want to sue.
Judge: Yeah, you did. You know how I know? Because you did.
Judge: You can’t sue the son, and you can’t sue the wife just because she’s the wife. Neither one of them need to pay you.
Judge: Verdict for the defendant.

Dialogue:
Judge: Al Rivers and Kashira?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Okay, you are suing Tyrell Mason Bazarus?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: For one thousand one hundred dollars that you want him to pay you because he made your prom dress and there were some serious wardrobe malfunctions. So it's your big day, it's your prom day, it's a day every girl dreams of. I don't know if the guys dream about it as much, I don't really care but it's a big deal. And what happens? You go out and you find that this is beautiful. How did you design this dress? Did he design it? Did you see it in a magazine? What was that?
Plaintiff: I had designed it in my head and then I met with him and he drew it out for me.
Judge: Okay, and tell me about the dress. Is it a top and a bottom separate?
Plaintiff: Yes, it's one dress and then it's kind of like a cape that comes around to the front that buttons up so it drags on the bottom.
Judge: So it's like a train?
Plaintiff: Yes, that’s detachable.
Judge: Okay. Do you have pictures of you wearing it?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: So what goes wrong and when does it go wrong?
Plaintiff: When I was about to leave, I was going to put my cape on and my mom said, "Turn around," and when I turned around she said, "You have a big hole in your dress."
Judge: Yeah, that one's pretty big. So it's not a hole, it's that the stitching came loose, right?
Plaintiff: Right.
Judge: Okay. And then what happens?
Plaintiff: My mom said, "Just calm down, it's going to be okay. Just put your cape on and just don't take it off."
Judge: Why didn't somebody just take a needle and thread? Are you the mom?
Plaintiff: ’s Mother: Yes.
Judge: Okay, come on up, Mom. I can't hear you if you're not mic'd.
Plaintiff: ’s Mother: I was not so in the clouds, so I couldn't do it.
Judge: Yeah, you look like a sister, I can't believe it. When we saw that, the hole wasn't that big, it was like about this big.
Plaintiff: ’s Mother: No, I see the hole.
Judge: So back here, we're talking about the back hole at first.
Plaintiff: ’s Mother: The first hole, yes. And then by the time she got home, the whole thing had dragged.
Judge: Yeah, this was the first hole when she left.
Plaintiff: ’s Mother: So I was like, "This hole is not..." If it's just...
Judge: Did you hand-stitch this?
Defendant: No, it was machine-stitched.
Judge: So when she left, it was this hole. So I was like, "This hole is fine, it's not that... Just don't take off your cape."
Plaintiff: ’s Mother: That was kind of the idea, that she should be able to take it off.
Judge: Yeah. So she's fine, but then...
Plaintiff: ’s Mother: Then the unthinkable happened. She called and was like, "Mom, my whole dress has come apart."
Judge: Do you have evidence of when that phone call was?
Plaintiff: ’s Mother: No, I don't.
Judge: When did she call you?
Plaintiff: ’s Mother: I don’t even remember the time.
Judge: I'm going to have you two switch now. Okay, and so when does the front hole happen?
Plaintiff: I got there, I called. We were eating, and that’s when I looked down, so it was around like 8. I want to say that's when it started ripping.
Judge: I would have stapled it.
Plaintiff: ’s Mother: You would have stapled it?
Judge: Yeah.
Judge: So what happened, Mr. Bazarus?
Defendant: Well, I made the prom dress. She came over for the fitting, and she paid 600.
Judge: Oh, is that correct?
Plaintiff: ’s Mother: Yes.
Judge: Is that including the fabric or no?
Defendant: That’s including the fabric, yes. Everything, the fabric and all. So I made the dress. She comes over for fitting and it fits, everything works out beautiful. She loved the dress, and they take the dress.
Judge: When do you get the messages that there's a problem?
Defendant: It was the day of the prom. And that’s when they sent me a picture of the dress via text and a video, I believe, also.
Judge: Okay. And then what?
Defendant: That’s when I said, "Okay, I don’t know what happened, what transpired there." I said, "Could it be that maybe the way she put it on, maybe the stitch could have popped?" Because it's a fashion-fitted dress.
Judge: Listen, it's a fitted dress. You know it’s a fitted dress. You know the stitching has to be done in a way that something like this doesn't happen. Because it's not meant to happen. Your last fitting was when?
Plaintiff: The day before prom.
Judge: Yeah. So it’s not like the last fitting was a month earlier and she gained weight. There's no reason for this to happen. You and I both know that.
Judge: So, you’re suing for 600 that you paid for the dress, right? Because you’re never going to wear that dress again. And you want 400 for repairs and 100 for gas. That’s a lot of money.
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: You should be compensated, but let’s be fair. Mr. Bazarus, did you agree to pay 200 and fix the dress?
Defendant: Yes, but I got busy with personal matters.
Judge: You got busy? That’s not a reason to ignore someone. That’s why you got sued. I find in favor of the plaintiffs in the amount of 250 plus court costs, and the defendant must repair the dress. Case closed.
