Dialogue:
Judge: You don't think this kind of eviscerates the kind of exacting control that Congress usually exercises in the appropriations process?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: I don't. And, you know, to the extent that this question is pressing on the annual appropriations—
Judge: Yeah.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: —as a kind of counterpart here, I think that the question becomes, does the Constitution limit Congress with respect to the duration of appropriations? Is there some kind of implicit limit in the text that the Court could divine that means that Congress, in contrast to other laws, which, of course, can remain in effect until a future Congress acts—is Congress limited in how long to leave appropriations in effect? And, there, I think the Army Appropriations Clause does a lot of work because it's not like the Framers weren't aware of this dynamic.
They thought specifically about the fact that appropriations, like all other laws, might be continuing, indefinite until a future Congress acts, and they were concerned about that with respect to a standing Army, but they didn't otherwise seek to limit Congress's authority.
Judge: Counsel, one of the things that struck me as I was reading it—you have a very aggressive view of Congress's authority under the Appropriations Clause.
I'm not saying remotely that that's not correct, but it struck me—you represent the executive branch as well, and it's a very strong power given to Congress. And it struck me that the reason you would want to defend that is because it gives them more power to give away.
And there are obviously—legend has it there have been times when the same party controlled both houses of Congress and the White House, and in that situation, you can see Congress empowering the President in a way that might seem unusual to the Framers.
So keeping in mind that imbalance—in other words, it's kind of paradoxical, the more power you give Congress, I think, the more—and this is, I think, your friend’s argument on the other side—there’s more that it can give away and enhance the authority of the executive. Is that an unpersuasive concern?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, certainly, I don't think it's an unpersuasive concern, but built into your question as I understood it, Your Honor, was the idea that maybe Congress could do something that would be surprising or anomalous to the Framers.
And I guess what I would say is that if—if you're looking at it through that lens, then history should play a powerful role in trying to understand the limits or scope of, you know, how much Congress can give away, when does it become too much.
And, here, the Court doesn't need to articulate any outer limits because we have a very specific type of appropriation that's actually far more constrained than many that Congress has enacted throughout history because Congress provided funding for a single agency and actually capped that amount of funding in an amount not to exceed the cap set by Congress.
Judge: Well, it's pretty unusual to have that agency drawing its—being able to request however much it wants, subject to a limit that it really hasn't gotten very close to over the years, from an entity that is also drawing money from the private sector.
I didn't see any particularly compelling historical analogues to that.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, let me—
Judge: And, again, to the extent that takes you away from the appropriations power, it significantly enhances the power of the executive.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: So I disagree that there is anything unprecedented about this funding arrangement when you look at the relevant constitutional value of protecting Congress's prerogatives.
And I know that there are a lot of different moving parts and pieces to the arguments Respondents have made, but as I understand it, they are attacking four features of the funding statute:
The fact that it's a standing appropriation, so it remains in place and is not time-limited.
That it gives the director of the CFPB some discretion to act within the statutory cap in requesting the funding.
That the CFPB has enforcement and regulatory functions.
That the CFPB’s funding comes from a source that’s not, in their words, constrained by market forces.
But we have numerous examples of agencies that have all four of those relevant characteristics.
I dispute at the outset that we don't actually think the functions or the market forces constraint are relevant, but even taking the argument on its own terms, I can give you founding-era examples.
The Customs Service and the revenue officers were funded with that kind of mechanism.
They had standing appropriations. For the Customs Service, it was uncapped. These were powerful regulatory entities.
The Customs Service could board ships and seize vessels and inspect records and conduct searches and levy penalties and collect fines, and there was no way to avoid that kind of regulation.
So the market constraint theory that the users could just opt out or regulated parties could decide not to fund the operations doesn't apply to those agencies.
And it's still the case with many of the financial regulators today.
The ones I would put on that list are the Federal Reserve Board, the FDIC, the NCUA, the Farm Credit Administration, and the FHFA.
Judge: Counsel, I just want to understand, following up on the previous question, what role the upper limit plays on your theory of the case.
Is that an essential feature to the constitutionality of this provision, or could Congress pass the same law with no upper limit, allowing the executive branch to determine however much it wished to take?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: So we don't think that Congress would have to provide a statutory specified amount, but they would, of course, have to specify the purpose of the funding.
Judge: Okay.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff's lawyer argues that the funding mechanism for a specific agency is not unprecedented and does not exceed the powers granted to Congress by the Constitution. They contend that the agency's funding, which is subject to a statutory cap and can be requested at the agency's discretion, is not unconstitutional. The lawyer uses historical examples of similar agencies, such as the Customs Service and various financial regulators, to support their argument.
DEFENDANT:
The Defendant's lawyer argues that the funding mechanism for a specific agency is not unprecedented and does not exceed the constitutional limits of Congress's appropriations power. They contend that the agency in question has a standing appropriation, which remains in place and is not time-limited, and the director of the agency has some discretion to act within a statutory cap in requesting funding. The Defendant's lawyer also points out that there are numerous historical examples of agencies with similar characteristics, such as the Customs Service and various financial regulators.

The Defendant's goal or motive behind their words is to establish that the funding mechanism for the agency in question is not unconstitutional, as it has historical precedents and is within the bounds of Congress's appropriations power.

Dialogue:
Judge: Mr. Laxon, you're suing Ms. Hoffman for the return of your security deposit, plus you want to be paid for painting a place that you hadn’t even signed a lease for yet, correct?
Plaintiff: Correct.
Judge: Okay. What happened here?
Plaintiff: Well, um, I was actually at home, and my mother happened to call me. She’s like, “Hey, I found a place for you. I want you to go look at it.” So, we go look at it, and I should have known something was wrong then because she never came upstairs to show us the place.
Judge: Who’s “she”?
Plaintiff: The defendant. She actually had me and another person go look at it.
Judge: The other person was someone looking to rent it as well?
Plaintiff: Correct.
Judge: So, she had kind of like an open house at a certain time, just meeting everybody there and renting it to the first person she liked?
Plaintiff: Correct.
Judge: Yeah, so she stood downstairs. And why is that bad? You said, “I should have known something was wrong.”
Plaintiff: Yeah, 'cause usually landlords all walk you through the place. She didn’t. She just stood downstairs and just said, “Go up and look.” So we did.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: We filled out the application.
Judge: Who’s “we”?
Plaintiff: Oh, I did, and another person that was there looking at it.
Judge: There’s a little bit of stress going on there, a little bit of tension, 'cause both of you wanted it?
Plaintiff: Yeah. Yes, yes, we both wanted it. And, um, she called me on a Monday—actually, that following Monday—and was like, “You know, hey, I’m going to give you the place.” And I was excited to move in. I’d been saving forever to get it.
Judge: Is this your first apartment?
Plaintiff: It is. Well, actually, it’s my second apartment.
Judge: How old are you?
Plaintiff: I’m 26.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: So, it’s actually my second, but I was just excited, you know? I was just like, “Okay, it’s mine! I can’t wait.” It’s in a historical area, right?
Judge: Yeah.
Plaintiff: You know, so I was excited about that as well, you know? And I just couldn’t wait to get in.
Judge: Who lived in that area?
Plaintiff: Many people, but most notably, um, Susan B. Anthony.
Judge: That’s really cool. Susan B. Anthony? How did you learn that?
Plaintiff: Not in that particular place, um, but in the neighborhood.
Judge: Yeah, just the neighborhood.
Plaintiff: The whole neighborhood.
Judge: So, anyway, go ahead.
Plaintiff: So, I actually walked through. She called me, and I said, “Okay, well, you know, I would love to have it.” I met with her on the 4th.
Judge: When did you give her the security deposit?
Plaintiff: On the 4th.
Judge: How much was the rent?
Plaintiff: $475.
Judge: Yeah.
Plaintiff: So, I gave her the security deposit. I never signed a lease with her. I gave her the security deposit, and I asked her—
Judge: When did you give her the security deposit? What day?
Plaintiff: On the 4th.
Judge: And then what happens? You asked her what?
Plaintiff: I asked her… Well, I told her, “Hey, I’ll paint if you prorate the rent,” because, you know, she was kind of, you know, up in age, so I didn’t want to put the stress on her. I said, “I can do the work, and you just prorate the rent.”
Judge: How much was she going to prorate the rent?
Plaintiff: She only prorated $70.
Judge: $70?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: And that was the deal you guys struck? She would prorate the rent by $70?
Plaintiff: Yeah.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: And then what happened?
Judge: She wrote— So, I filled out a receipt—
Judge: Can I see what you signed? You said you filled out a receipt. What are you talking about?
Plaintiff: (Hands over receipt)
Judge: Who’s the person next to you who keeps raising their hand?
Plaintiff: ’s Mother: (Raises hand)
Judge: Keep your hand down. I’ll get to you in a minute if you have something to do with the case. He’s 26.
Plaintiff: ’s Mother: (Lowers hand)
Judge: I know, I know the urge, believe me. I clean up after my 22 and 21-year-old all day long.
Judge: (Examines documents) Okay, so she gave you a receipt to acknowledge your security deposit?
Plaintiff: Correct.
Judge: And so what else?
Plaintiff: So, um, I asked her—after I got done painting—she brought the lease over for me to sign. I said, “Well, hey, before I sign it, can we, you know, do a walk-through?”
Judge: And?
Plaintiff: She’s like, “No, sign the lease and then we’ll walk through the place.”
Judge: So, what do you say?
Plaintiff: I said, “I wasn’t going to do it. I’m not going to sign a lease until you come upstairs and look at what I’ve painted.”
Judge: And then?
Plaintiff: She was just so adamant about me signing this lease before she comes up. So, I didn’t do it. The registers on the floor where the heat comes through—there were none there. You could literally fall through.
Judge: Did you note all these things before you handed over money and agreed that you were going to rent it?
Plaintiff: Yeah, under the understanding that she would, you know, at least meet me halfway to take care of it.
Judge: Right. But when you say “under the understanding,” there’s the understanding in between your ears and there’s the understanding between two people. Why is there not a written agreement?     Judge: Okay, so at this point, you’re saying, “I’m not going to sign the lease until we do a walk-through.” What does she do?
Plaintiff: She says, “No, I’m not doing a walk-through. Sign the lease first.”
Judge: And then?
Plaintiff: I told her, “I’m not signing the lease unless you do a walk-through,” so she got mad and said, “You can’t have the apartment anymore.”
Judge: Okay. Did you ask for your security deposit back?
Plaintiff: I did.
Judge: And what did she say?
Plaintiff: She said, “I’m not giving it back to you because you wasted my time.”
Judge: All right. Let’s hear from you, Ms. Hoffman.
Defendant: ’s Response
Defendant: Your Honor, I had told him when he came to look at the apartment that the apartment was not yet ready, that there were a few repairs that had to be made. I was having the registers replaced and some minor things taken care of.
Judge: Did you tell him that before or after he gave you the security deposit?
Defendant: Before. I told him that when he looked at the place. He seemed fine with it and was very eager to move in.
Judge: Okay. What happened when he refused to sign the lease?
Defendant: He kept insisting that we do a walk-through before signing. I explained to him that I always have tenants sign the lease first and then we do a formal walk-through together. That’s just how I’ve always done it.
Judge: Why not just do a quick walk-through first if that’s what he wanted?
Defendant: Because in my experience, tenants use that as an excuse to delay signing or to demand more repairs. I told him the issues were already scheduled to be fixed, but he didn’t believe me.
Judge: So, when he refused to sign, what did you do?
Defendant: I told him that if he wasn’t comfortable signing, then I would have to rent it to someone else.
Judge: And when he asked for his security deposit back?
Defendant: I told him no, because I had taken the apartment off the market for him and turned down other potential renters. That cost me time and money.
Judge: ’s Ruling
Judge: All right. Here’s where I’m at. Ms. Hoffman, I understand that it is frustrating when a tenant backs out after agreeing to rent, but a security deposit is meant to secure a rental agreement. If there is no signed lease, there is no binding rental agreement.
Defendant: But I held the apartment for him!
Judge: That was your choice. If you wanted a firm commitment, you should have had him sign the lease before giving you any money. The fact that he painted the apartment before even signing a lease is unusual, but that was an agreement you both made, and you only agreed to prorate $70, which he accepted.
Plaintiff: Correct.
Judge: Since there was no signed lease, you had no legal grounds to keep his deposit. You can’t hold onto money for an agreement that never existed in writing. You need to return his $475.
Defendant: But he wasted my time!
Judge: Unfortunately, that’s a risk landlords take when they don’t have tenants sign leases upfront. I am ordering you to return the plaintiff’s full security deposit of $475.
Plaintiff: And the money for painting?
Judge: No, because you agreed to do the painting in exchange for a $70 rent reduction. That was the deal, and it stands. You’re getting back your security deposit, but nothing more.
Plaintiff: Okay.
Judge: Judgment in favor of the plaintiff for $475.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff's statements suggest that he was shown an apartment by the Defendant, Ms. Hoffman, without a signed lease. The Plaintiff was excited about the apartment, which was in a historical area, and agreed to paint the place in exchange for a prorated rent.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant's statements suggest that she had an open house for a rental property, and the Plaintiff, who was looking for an apartment, attended and expressed interest in the property. The Plaintiff and another individual both wanted the apartment, and the Defendant chose the Plaintiff. The Plaintiff gave the Defendant a security deposit on the 4th, but there was no signed lease.

Dialogue:
Judge: Alright, Lorna, you've asked us to refer to you only by your first name, and we'll respect that. You are suing Linda Laramore. Am I allowed to use your last name?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: For $295 that you say she owes you because you paid her that to use her car and she didn't let you use her car. Tell me what's going on.
Plaintiff: I met Linda. We became friends. I had moved to Florida for a job and had a car with that job, and it fell through. Basically, she would let me use the car. I would fill the gas tank, give her money for gas because she was financially in certain dire straits, so I would help her.
Judge: So you were doing that just to be a helpful person or because she was giving you rides places?
Plaintiff: No, I was using the car. She wasn't giving me rides. I was actually using the car, and I would also— we would share the use of the car.
Judge: Okay, and were you paying her for that?
Plaintiff: In the beginning, I was not. I was filling her gas tank, and I was using the car.
Judge: That's how you were paying her?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: And I was using the car very minimally, maybe to go to the gym. It wasn't a mile or two.
Plaintiff: Okay, then she came to me April 9th. She was behind two car payments. The third car payment would result in a repossession. I don't believe I— I don't know if she asked, but I said, "Look, I will pay this car payment so you will not be behind three car payments and get your car repossessed." In lieu of that, I can use the car for a month. I will schedule it, let you know when I need it, and we will share the car. I will do this for you so that way you won't be out the money.
Judge: So she wasn't going to pay you back; she was just going to let you use the car?
Plaintiff: Correct, but she was already letting you use the car. Were you letting her use the car?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Okay, because you were close friends?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: And I had surgery in March and had a total shoulder replacement. I couldn't drive anyway for six weeks.
Judge: So was she driving you places?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Some of the time.
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: How long had you two been friends? It's so sad that you're here.
Plaintiff: Four months, three or four months.
Judge: Okay, and did she give you $295? Did she make that payment of $295?
Plaintiff: She gave me the money, yes.
Judge: And when did she do that?
Plaintiff: I don't remember. I have the information. I have the coupon of the car payment that I made her give me just to make sure that that was the amount. And I have the withdrawal from the bank of $300.
Judge: And what date did that occur?
Plaintiff: April 9th. The car coupon where she was behind on the car payment was April 8th.
Judge: Okay, so had she not made that car payment, she would have been behind three car payments. So you were behind two payments. She was paying $295, and you were coming up with the other $295 or it—
Plaintiff: Yeah, I was going to pay. I paid the other one.
Judge: Alright, so according to you, what happens about a week later?
Plaintiff: She had an appointment, and I had a doctor's appointment in Jacksonville. It was an appointment that was longstanding, and you really wanted to make it.
Plaintiff: Yeah. Okay, go ahead.
Plaintiff: On the way, I had a copy. I wanted her to sign something saying that she received that cash, so I brought— I just typed up something simple saying she got the money, and then also my schedule so we could arrange to share the car when I gave her that information in the car.
Plaintiff: It was a rage, and proceeded to be a really terrifying car ride.
Judge: And this was the next day after you gave her the money?
Plaintiff: No, this was April 19th. It was 10 days after I gave her the money.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: Is that accurate? The conversation happens 10 days afterward?
Plaintiff: Yes, her doctor's appointment was 10 days after it.
Judge: I do not know that.
Plaintiff: That was the discussion 10 days later.
Judge: So according to you, it's 10 days later and she says what?
Plaintiff: I am not going to let you use the car. Is what she said.
Plaintiff: Just because you gave her something to sign that says that?
Plaintiff: Yeah, it just went from friendship to full-on rage, and she just started yelling and threatening.
Judge: What do you think was going on?
Plaintiff: I think she was trying to intimidate me into backing off and not wanting to get the car as the agreement was.
Judge: Was there an agreement according to you?
Plaintiff: There was an agreement that she could use the car.
Judge: Alright, what didn't you like about the way she was presenting it to you?
Plaintiff: I was driving in a rainstorm that day, and she kept insisting that I sign an agreement, a written agreement, and I said no.
Judge: When she pays you the $295, is there any agreement attached to the $295 that she'd be able to use the car?
Plaintiff: Okay, yes.
Judge: And then that's what she puts in writing for you to sign. Why are you offended then?
Plaintiff: How was it put in writing for me to sign? What did she put in writing for you to sign?
Plaintiff: She wanted me to sign the fact that she gave the receipt that she had given me the money.
Judge: So then—
Plaintiff: Okay, you need to tell me what's going on then. What led to the problem? Why did you pull out? Why did you say you can't use my car? What's going on?
Plaintiff: Okay, I'll tell you.
Defendant: If it's time, yeah.
Defendant: Yeah, it's past time. So are barter deals a good idea?
Judge: Absolutely not. Why?
Defendant: I'm a hair stylist, and I work hard for my job, so I'm not going to make a deal with you because you just expect it in the end.
Judge: Again, I'm not sure what that means because you still get something of value if the barter works. What do you think?
Defendant: I think if you both agree that what you're giving is worth what you're getting, then it should be fine, but you guys have to be on the same page.
Judge: Yeah, and it's hard being on the same page sometimes.
Defendant: It's a little loose going inside the courtroom.
Plaintiff: She'd been using it all of March, right? She lived down just a few miles from me at one point, which was no big deal. She had the car the majority of the time. I was in the hospital part of March anyway. I came home. I can't drive. I am making arrangements to ride the Council on Aging bus to get me to my appointments because I'm a senior, and I'm low income.
Plaintiff: So you look great.
Defendant: Thank you.
Plaintiff: Anyway, she moved to Volano Beach, which was a lot further away.
Defendant: How much further?
Plaintiff: It was a good 10 miles away, one way.
Defendant: Right, and then she would drive back, pick me up.
Judge: Hold on. When did you move?
Defendant: April 1st.
Judge: And it's four— it's four miles. It's not 10 miles.
Judge: So if she— she moved by the time you got the money for the car payment, which you agree is April 8th.
Defendant: I don't know when she moved.
Judge: You don't know?
Defendant: I don't know.
Defendant: I know she used my car to move, which was okay because she didn't have any other way to do it. But then it started being appointments in Jacksonville. Then she— her dentist was a good ways away from Volano Beach. Then she was coming to pick me up. It was just getting to be way too much for me to handle, especially with major surgery. I just wasn't— I mean, I just couldn't do it. I was riding a bus. I was walking. My car was with her a lot of the time.
Plaintiff: Okay, when she offered you a piece of paper and said, "I want you to sign this," there was already hostility between the two of you guys because you felt like she was acting like it's her car?
Defendant: Yeah.
Judge: And it's your car?
Defendant: Yeah.
Plaintiff: All right.
Defendant: So do you have an argument in the car that day?
Defendant: Yes, and yes, we did because she kept on and on. You've got to sign this, and you've got to do this, and you've got to do that. And I'm like, "I don't have to do anything. I don't even have to take you to Jacksonville," and I turned around to take her back home.
Judge: So you didn't take her to Jacksonville?
Defendant: I did take her.
Judge: Yes?
Defendant: Oh, you did. Wait, so you turned around then?
Defendant: Yes, three times. I didn’t watch. I thought my husband and I were the only ones who did that.
Plaintiff: No.
Defendant: No, this— this was a lot of stray U-turns when there's an argument in the car.
Plaintiff: It was very scary. She wanted me to get out of the car.
Judge: Did she tell you to get out?
Defendant: Yes.
Plaintiff: Really?
Defendant: I did because she wouldn't shut her mouth. She almost hit a truck.
Judge: Well, you're a grown woman. You don't have more anger control than that.
Plaintiff: You know, it sounds funny now, but no, it's not. It's terrifying then. You're on a highway, and you're sick, and you're going to an appointment, and it's raining. It's not funny, and she just kept on and on. I said, "I don't have to take you to this appointment." Okay, and I turned around not to take her.
Defendant: But I remembered it was a very important appointment for her.
Judge: Okay, and then you went ahead and took her?
Defendant: Yes, we went on to Jacksonville.
Judge: Did she ever borrow the car after that date?
Defendant: Never.
Judge: I don't know. You might not have.
Defendant: I don't have any— I don’t write down everything I do.
Judge: So did she try to, and you said no?
Defendant: She did try to, and I said no.
Judge: She did try to, and you said no?
Defendant: I won't loan you the car after that argument in the car and her telling me that she had the right to use the car like it was her car all the time.
Judge: Right.
Defendant: And I was upset because there was sunscreen, white sunscreen all over the inside of the car.
Plaintiff: Because she used it while— no.
Defendant: You left all your belongings in the car. You borrowed it more times than you ever did. You don't even remember how many. You don't remember how much.
Plaintiff: Okay, okay.
Defendant: You're paying for this now, not your good friend anymore. Just don't know.
Plaintiff: Yeah, that's right.
Defendant: You're not going to be somebody I try to get back to then.
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff, Lorna, is suing her friend Linda Laramore for $295 that she claims Linda owes her. Lorna and Linda became friends four months prior, and Lorna was in a situation where she needed a car due to a job-related move and a car issue. Linda allowed Lorna to use her car, and in exchange, Lorna would fill the gas tank.

Lorna helped Linda avoid car repossession by paying a car payment on her behalf, in exchange for continued use of the car for a month.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant's statements suggest that she allowed the Plaintiff to use her car for a period of time, with the Plaintiff paying for gas and other expenses related to the car's maintenance. The Defendant was financially struggling and the Plaintiff was helping her out. The Defendant had a car payment due, and the Plaintiff offered to pay it to prevent the car from being repossessed.
Techniques_Used: playing the victim, emotional appeal, framing the narrative
Techniques_LLM: playing the victim, emotional appeal, framing the narrative

Dialogue:
Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to the People's Court. Next case on the docket: the plaintiff wants the defendant to pay up on a loan, and he doesn't care that they've known each other for 29 years. But the defendant says he doesn't owe the plaintiff anything; he's making it up. It's the case of "Should Old Acquaintance Be Forgot."
Judge: Shannon Grant?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Okay, you are suing Richard Green for $1,325 that you want paid to you for parking tickets, impound fees, transportation, and lost wages as a result of tickets that the defendant got on a car of yours—car of his that you put in your name?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: What are you doing doing that? Why did you do that?
Plaintiff: When you know somebody for over half your life, you grew up with them, you know their mother, father, and of course, when people just get married and have kids, and they ask for your help because of family problems... You know, I was going through family problems at the same time, and I was living on my own. So when he came to me reaching out, I felt deep inside, like, you know what? If my mother wasn't there to help me and I'm busting my behind working like he was at the time, I'd want a friend to do that for me.
Judge: Yeah, but why did he need you to do that for him?
Plaintiff: He had an accident with his brother's van, and it wasn't his fault, he said, or something like that. And there was a conflict within the family business that he worked in with them helping him get a car on the road.
Judge: Why does somebody have to help him get a car? He's a grown man. First things first, just give me the answer. I know the answer is going to be because he has bad credit, right? That's going to be the answer.
Plaintiff: Basically.
Judge: Okay, how do you get bad credit?
Plaintiff: Um... basically not paying your bills.
Judge: Paying bills. I believe it wasn't just bad credit, though. It was worse than that.
Plaintiff: Well, that's enough to not want to put a car in your name that he's driving because if he gets tickets, he's not going to pay them, right?
Plaintiff: You're right.
Judge: All right. So you decide to put a car he's driving in your name, and what happens?
Plaintiff: And of course, like, if he ends up hitting a truckload of orphans, who do you think gets sued?
Judge: You would.
Plaintiff: Okay.
Judge: All right, so what happens?
Plaintiff: What happened was it was supposed to be for a short period of time until he could just get on his feet, about three months, and four months, really. It was a six-month ordeal with the insurance.
Plaintiff: Long story short, within a two-month period, I couldn't find Rich.
Judge: You couldn't find him?
Plaintiff: Yeah.
Judge: What are you talking about? This guy is like your blood brother, your best friend, who you have to... I'm making fun of you right now, so I don't know why you're chuckling. Okay, your best friend who you can't help but help out in his time of need because you've grown up together—how do you not find him?
Plaintiff: I'm trying to avoid it, but uh... it's drugs.
Judge: Who?
Plaintiff: Who’s it was because of drugs?
Judge: Whose drug use?
Plaintiff: It was because of drug use.
Judge: Whose drug use?
Plaintiff: Everybody else understands the question I'm asking, right? Whose drug use?
Plaintiff: He couldn't be contacted at his house or where he was at because his family and nobody had contact with him. He ran away from everybody.
Judge: Okay, because of drug use?
Plaintiff: His, yes.
Judge: Okay, so now you can't find him. Did you know that he had a drug problem?
Plaintiff: No.

Dialogue:
Judge: Valencia Ray, you are suing Cyber Electronics Incorporated, represented here by its owner, Joseph Rivas, for $435, a refund on an LG 4K television, 43-inch. What a great price. Tell me what happened.
Plaintiff: I had went to the store to buy a TV. I usually get my electronics fixed there. I asked them if I could purchase a TV because I was going to a doctor’s appointment and I could come back and pick it up. He said no problem. I came back to pick up the TV. Meanwhile, the guy from the store took the TV out the store, brought it up the hill, and put it in the cab.
Judge: Okay. That was nice. Did you see him drop it?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: All I saw was that he said he put it in the cab already, in the trunk.
Judge: So you didn’t watch him bring the TV?
Plaintiff: No, I just saw him.
Judge: Did you say, “Wait a second, I need to inspect the TV?”
Plaintiff: No, I did not. I did not say that.
Judge: So then what happens? You go—
Plaintiff: Then I get home. My building has security. The cab driver took it out, and I asked security if he could place it in front of the building. My neighbor was coming down. He said, “I’ll help you with the TV.” They took the TV. We put it in the elevator. They took it upstairs.
Judge: Were you in the elevator with them?
Plaintiff: Yes. We get into my apartment. My girlfriend is there because she comes over. I said, “Let’s take the TV and put it together.” When we took it out of the box, I noticed a crack. She said, “No, that’s probably the plastic.” Then when I took off the Styrofoam, I saw it was a big break. Not even an hour later, I called them back and I explained it to them. There’s no damage to the box. There’s no damage to the Styrofoam. But the TV’s broken.
Judge: Let me hear from you. What’s your protocol over there?
Defendant: On May 16, 2017, we received TVs from the manufacturer. One employee visually inspected all of them, the outside boxes, and then proceeded to test all the TVs for damages.
Judge: When the TVs are shipped to you?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: What employee did that? Is Abdullah here?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: All right. So do you actually remember these particular TVs, or is this what you do whenever a shipment comes in?
Employee: That’s what I do anytime we receive a TV in the store.
Judge: Do you record your findings in any way? Like, is there somewhere where you’re supposed to write stuff down, or do you only— Hold on, am I talking to you?
Defendant: Sorry.
Judge: Is there somewhere where you’re supposed to write stuff down, or do you just say something if it’s broken?
Employee: We have a paper that we sign.
Judge: Go ahead. Now you can show them the paper.
Defendant: Show it to your employee, please.
Judge: Is that paper— Is that your writing on the paper?
Employee: Yes.
Judge: And is that your signature?
Employee: Yes.
Judge: Was that paper authored on the same day that you inspected it?
Employee: Yes.
Judge: Which was the day that it came in from the manufacturer?
Employee: Yes.
Judge: And then what do you guys do with it?
Employee: We check all of them. Once we don’t see any broken TVs, we place them to sell.
Judge: Do you put them back in the box, or do you put them on a shelf?
Employee: We put them back in the box and sell them.
Judge: So when somebody buys a TV, what do you guys do? If they don’t ask to inspect it—tough for them, I guess?
Employee: We check the TV twice as a normal procedure. On this particular situation, the TV was only checked once.
Judge: I just— I’m not sure that it falls on you. I think it’s good that you do that, but I’m just not sure that it falls on them to inspect. Like, in other words, when I valet my car, I literally stand at the top right corner of the car when they come, and then I walk around. I don’t want to be obvious about the fact that I’m inspecting the car, but I know that if I drive off, the valet is going to say, “How do we know what you did with it?”
Judge: Can the TV be damaged without the box being damaged?
Defendant: Yes, because if someone dropped the TV, the box would still be fine.
Judge: Well, here’s a TV that’s heavy and lots of people touched it. Maybe you bought it broken, maybe you didn’t. But the problem is, I don’t deal in maybes. You have to be able to prove that they broke it and not that you took off and then your security guy and your friend or you trying to bring it up broke it. How are you going to do that?
Plaintiff: I have a letter from the LG technician who came to the house. He looked at it and inspected the box and the Styrofoam and everything. He said this was a shipping and handling defect.
Judge: Oh, good, then they’ll replace it for you.
Plaintiff: No, they told him—
Judge: I can’t say they broke it. Verdict for the Defendant.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff, Valencia Ray, is suing Cyber Electronics Incorporated for a refund on an LG 4K television that was purchased from their store. The Plaintiff claims that the TV was damaged during the delivery process, as they noticed a crack in the TV upon unboxing it at home. The Plaintiff did not inspect the TV before bringing it home, relying on the store's assurance that the TV was in good condition.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant's statements suggest that they follow a protocol for inspecting TVs upon receiving them from the manufacturer. This includes visually inspecting the outside boxes and testing the TVs for damages. The Defendant claims that the TV in question was only checked once, and not twice as their normal procedure.

Dialogue:
Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to The People's Court. Next case on the docket, the plaintiff went to a steakhouse, valeted her car, and then, when she walked out and left, realized her computer was missing. But the defendant says she's such a scammer she said 35 softball uniforms were stolen, changed to an iPod, and on and on. It's the case of 'I have beef with a valet.'
Judge: Thank you. Jessica Wolf, yes? You are suing the valet company represented here by Andres Bazan. Are you the owner, the manager?
Defendant: I'm supervising Westchester County accounts of the company.
Judge: Okay. And you have the authority to represent the company here?
Defendant: Yes, Your Honor.
Judge: Okay. You are suing for $3,880 in items that you say the company stole from you when they valeted your car. Tell me what's going on.
Plaintiff: I went to Ruth's Chris Steakhouse on Saturday, November 26th. Earlier that day, I was at the library studying.
Judge: What library?
Plaintiff: The Orangeburg Library in Rockland County.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: I had my laptop, a calculator, and study books and materials in a black tote bag. So I studied, got into the car, and put the laptop down. Later that night—
Judge: Where did you put the laptop?
Plaintiff: On the passenger seat of the car.
Judge: Where did you put the rest of the stuff you described?
Plaintiff: It was in the bag. Everything was in the bag, and I put it on the passenger seat.
Plaintiff: Later that night, we decided to go to Ruth’s Chris Steakhouse for dinner. We had reservations around 8 or 8:30.
Judge: Who is “we”?
Plaintiff: Me and my family.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: I picked up my brother, who got into the car. The bag was on the passenger seat. He moved the bag to the back seat of the car.
Judge: Okay. What about the computer?
Plaintiff: Everything was in the bag. It was a black tote bag, and it had everything in it. He moved it to the back seat of the car.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: We got to the restaurant. I was a little hesitant. I saw the valet. I really didn’t want to valet my car—not because I was thinking about stolen items, but because of scratches and things like that.
Judge: What kind of car do you drive?
Plaintiff: It’s a BMW 3 Series.
Judge: What year?
Plaintiff: 2011.
Judge: Okay. So you pulled up, not realizing that you had the laptop in the back of the car?
Plaintiff: Yes. My brother had just moved it when he got in the car.
Judge: Okay, but your memory didn’t last from then?
Plaintiff: No. I handed the valet my keys, and he gave me the ticket. We went into the restaurant. Everything was fine. We came back out. I handed the valet my ticket. He ran and got the car.
Plaintiff: My brother noticed that the car was parked way in the back, in a secluded area near the woods. It was a little strange.
Judge: How did you notice that it was parked in a secluded area far away?
Plaintiff: There were no other cars around the vicinity.
Judge: Did you stay late? Maybe that’s why there were no other cars.
Plaintiff: No, there were still many people left in the restaurant.
Judge: Okay. But their cars weren’t there?
Plaintiff: No. The car was parked in the last row of the parking lot where the valet parks the cars. There were dozens of open spots closer to the restaurant.
Judge: Did that strike you as strange at the time?
Plaintiff: Not immediately. But when we found out what was missing, I put two and two together.
Judge: So you got your car and didn’t check your stuff?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: You didn’t notice anything missing until the next day?
Plaintiff: Correct. I didn’t even think someone would steal my laptop. The car interior is black, the bag is black, and it was late at night. It just didn’t occur to me.
Judge: You’re claiming $4,000 worth of stuff was missing, but you didn’t check before leaving the lot?
Plaintiff: No. I parked my car in the driveway, and it automatically locks. The next morning, when I went to study, I noticed the bag was missing. That’s when I called the restaurant and reported the theft.
Defendant: Her father actually called, not her. We checked with our employees, and they said they didn’t touch anything. We’ve had no prior complaints about them.
Judge: Do you ever do polygraphs?
Defendant: Sometimes, but not in this case. The claim seemed odd—44 items missing. The ticket also clearly states we are not responsible for valuables left in the car.
Judge: Ms. Wolf, you have no direct proof the valet stole your items, and you didn’t check your belongings before leaving. That makes it difficult for me to rule in your favor. My verdict is for the defendant.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff, Jessica Wolf, is suing a valet company for $3,880 in damages due to the alleged theft of her laptop and other items while her car was valeted at Ruth's Chris Steakhouse. The Plaintiff claims that she had placed her laptop on the passenger seat of her BMW 3 Series, along with a black tote bag containing study materials and a calculator. She handed her car keys to the valet, who parked her car in a secluded area of the parking lot.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant, Andres Bazan, is representing a valet company that is being sued by the Plaintiff, Jessica Wolf, for $3,880 in stolen items. The Plaintiff claims that her laptop, calculator, and study materials were stolen from her car while it was valeted at Ruth's Chris Steakhouse. The Defendant, however, maintains that the Plaintiff is a scammer and that the valet company has no prior complaints about them.

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to The People’s Court. The plaintiff says the defendant T-boned her car at a high rate of speed and may have been drunk. The defendant says her car got it way worse. It’s the case of "I got some tea for you." Thank you, Douglas. Okay, Carrie Boylan, you are suing Desiree McGee for $4,885.51 that you say you are out after an accident that she had with your— is this your son?
Plaintiff: Yes, he was driving the car.
Judge: Come front and center. I don’t know why you’re sitting down. Okay, you weren’t in the car?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: What happened?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: I was driving one of our friends home, and I was going past the side street. I had the right of way. I had no stop sign. She came out, she hit me pretty decent, put a hole in my door. I called my mom first, and she said the cops were on the way already.
Judge: When you say a hole in your door, what do you mean? Is there a picture?
Plaintiff: Yeah, these are all the pictures.
Judge: Were you hurt?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: No, I went to the hospital for tightness, a back problem, and they said it was just tight muscles.
Judge: Did you hear screeching of brakes or anything?
Plaintiff: ’s Son: Nope. I didn’t know somebody was actually going to hit me until somebody said, "Oh! Oh!" Yeah.
Judge: What happened?
Defendant: I was driving. I had my phone on one of those clips on the dashboard, and it fell underneath my foot.
Judge: Why was it on a clip on the dashboard?
Defendant: Like, I use it for my GPS and stuff, so I don’t touch it. Like, it doesn’t roam around, but it fell down, and it fell underneath my foot, under the pedal. So, I took my foot off the gas and slowed down to try to grab it. When I looked up, there was a stop sign. I did stop, and we did hit each other.
Judge: It’s not the best time to go grab your phone.
Defendant: Well, if I were to try to, you know, hit the pedal and it was underneath the pedal, I wouldn’t have been able to stop anyway. You know what I mean? Like, if a water bottle had fallen underneath your car, like, your pedal—
Judge: That wouldn’t happen to me.
Defendant: Well—
Judge: You know why it wouldn’t happen to me? Because the phone wouldn’t be that important for me to continue to drive and not look at what’s happening when I’m about to approach a stop sign. You T-boned somebody because your attention was diverted to go pick up the phone you dropped. So it became his problem. You didn’t just crash into a tree and make it only your problem. You made it his problem by crashing into him. That’s if that’s what was happening, as opposed to that you were texting.
Defendant: No.
Judge: No? So, if you grab your phone as you’re driving and you get in an accident, are you automatically responsible for that accident?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: Because? There’s a word for it. What’s the word for it?
Defendant: Be responsible?
Judge: No. What’s the word for it? You are a blank driver.
Defendant: Drunk?
Judge: No.
Defendant: Reckless?
Judge: No. Distracted. Really good.
Judge: Were you hurt?
Defendant: No, I was not hurt.
Judge: Okay, you’re very lucky nobody was killed. Now, insurance pays off the car. You are upside down on the car, right? Which is what happens when you finance a car. So, you’re asking that she— after the insurance has already paid you— her insurance has already paid you the value of the car, correct?
Plaintiff: Correct.
Judge: That’s what you’re entitled to in a court of law. And if it turns out that you owe more than what the value is, that’s just too bad for you. You’re suing so that she has to pay not just the value of what she damaged but also $2,750 because you don’t want to have to put a down payment on a new car.
Plaintiff: Yeah. I had four years left on my loan. I wasn’t looking for a new car.
Judge: Yeah, I know, but you also didn’t have to pay off four years of a loan. The damage was paid off by the insurance company by paying off the car. So, you can’t then say, "Oh, by the way, buy me a new car too." No, that’s not how it works.
Judge: The next thing you’re suing for is lost wages for three days of work. Tell me about that.
Plaintiff: I had to take time off from work to take him to the doctor’s, physical therapy, and to come here today.
Judge: Do you have proof of your wages?
Plaintiff: His pay stub.
Judge: Did the police come to the site?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Anybody get arrested? Nothing like that, right?
Plaintiff: Nope.
Judge: You think she was driving drunk?
Plaintiff: She seemed out of sorts.
Judge: Maybe because it was an accident? Did the officer suspect anything?
Plaintiff: I told the officer. He said he didn’t suspect anything and just left it at that.
Judge: Verdict for the defendant. You need to watch what you’re doing. It’s never that important. I know you think you’re that important, but you’re not.
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff, Carrie Boylan, is suing Desiree McGee for $4,885.51 due to an automobile accident that occurred when Desiree allegedly T-boned Carrie's son's car. The Plaintiff's son was driving the car at the time of the accident and was not injured. The Plaintiff claims that Desiree was driving recklessly, possibly under the influence, and that the accident caused significant damage to the car.

The Plaintiff's goal or motive behind their words appears to be seeking compensation for the damage to their son's car, lost wages due to time taken off work for medical appointments and court proceedings, and additional costs not covered by insurance.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant's statements suggest that they were driving a vehicle and had their phone clipped to the dashboard for GPS purposes. During the drive, the phone fell under their foot, causing them to momentarily take their foot off the gas pedal to retrieve it. In this distraction, they failed to notice a stop sign and T-boned another vehicle driven by the Plaintiff's son.

Dialogue:
Judge: Okay, Bonnie, you're suing Cindy—you've both asked not to mention your last names or the company's name—for $1,400 that you say you loaned Cindy for rent. According to you, that was a business investment. Let me hear from you first, Bonnie. Tell me what happened.
Plaintiff: Yes, I moved into a storage facility, and on the counter were business cards. The business card I picked was for sewing classes—offering sewing classes in your home.
Judge: Had you ever taken sewing lessons before? Did you know how to sew? Were you good at this, or did you just think it would be an interesting thing to do?
Plaintiff: I've never learned how to sew. I don't even know how to thread the machine. I actually needed something to focus on—something therapeutic for me. Learning a new skill, something I can contribute by helping other people who don't know the skill, would be rewarding for me.
Judge: So, you started taking lessons with her?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: And how long were you doing that?
Plaintiff: Well, the first two Sundays were in my apartment. We started at the end of May, and the relationship quickly deteriorated. The last contact I had with her was, I think, in August or September.
Judge: Okay, let's get to that middle part, though. So, she's in your apartment giving you sewing lessons. You're paying how much an hour or how much per session?
Plaintiff: I can't really say because she charged me $145 for the sewing machine.
Judge: Okay, how much was she charging you per lesson?
Plaintiff: $15 an hour.
Judge: So, you're learning, you're liking it, you're enjoying it. And at some point, the two of you discuss no longer doing it at people's homes but instead from a location. Correct?
Plaintiff: We had a discussion during the time we were having classes. I said, "Oh, I wish I could have my own shop; then I could have people coming and going like I want." And she said, "Well, why don't you get it?" And I said, "Well, I can't afford it." And she said, "Well, I could help you. I'd be interested in helping you." And that's kind of how everything escalated.
Judge: So, you tell her that you're interested in helping her. And what is it you do?
Plaintiff: Well, she comes to me for the security deposit. She said she found a place, she had to get it really fast, and she gave me instructions on what to do—to get the money, get it to the landlord, get the lease there, don't ask questions, don't answer questions, give her the cash.
Judge: I'm sorry—don't ask questions of whom?
Plaintiff: Of the landlord.
Judge: Okay. Were you signing the lease, or was she?
Plaintiff: She was signing the lease.
Judge: So, you got the money to the landlord?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: How much money was it?
Plaintiff: $1,400.
Judge: All right. And what was your understanding of your role in all of this? What were you thinking that $1,400 was?
Plaintiff: That was my advance payment on the classes.
Judge: Okay. So, you expected to get that money back in actual class time?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: And now, according to you, what is that money?
Defendant: That money was shared rent on a workspace that we could have together so I could teach her more, and we could have other people come for classes.
Judge: And in return, were you supposed to give her classes?
Defendant: Because of her gift that I was grateful for, I wanted to make sure she had formal class time in the amount that she would pay for rent in return for her gift that she gave me.
Judge: I thought you said that she was getting access and stuff like that?
Defendant: Right, she was sharing rent with me, and I was grateful.
Judge: How is that a gift?
Defendant: Well, I looked at it—I was grateful for it. I was grateful for her wanting to come in.
Judge: So, she gives you something, and you give her something in return?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: Yeah, we call that a contract.
---
[Judge's Ruling]
"This is a contract case. You paid in advance for something, and now you want to get out of it? I'm not going to let you. You agreed to give lessons, and I’m not going to call this a gift that you don’t have to do anything for. These are lessons that are paid for, and they are going to be given. What we are going to do is try to work out a schedule before you two leave this building."
Verdict: The contract must be enforced. The defendant must provide $1,400 worth of lessons.
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff, Bonnie, initially approached Cindy for sewing lessons, which they began to conduct in Bonnie's apartment. During their sessions, Bonnie expressed an interest in opening a sewing shop and suggested that Cindy could help her achieve this goal. In response, Cindy offered to help Bonnie secure a workspace for their joint venture.

Bonnie provided Cindy with $1,400 as an advance payment for the rent of the workspace, expecting to receive this amount back in the form of sewing lessons.
DEFENDANT:
In this courtroom dialogue, the Defendant, Bonnie, initially approached Cindy for sewing lessons as a therapeutic and potentially profitable venture. During their lessons, Bonnie expressed an interest in opening a sewing shop, to which Cindy offered to help by providing financial assistance for the shop's rental deposit. Bonnie agreed, and the $1,400 she provided was used as the security deposit for the shop.

Bonnie's intent was to use the shop as a platform to teach sewing classes, with the expectation that the classes would be a form of payment for Cindy's financial assistance.

Dialogue:
Judge: We will hear argument first this morning in Case 23-370, Erlinger versus United States. Counsel, you may proceed.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Your Honor, robust and clear precedent dictates the outcome here. In case after case, this Court has held that judges applying ACCA may find only what crime with what elements a defendant was previously convicted of—nothing more. The Court has grounded that rule directly in the Sixth Amendment. A judge may not increase a defendant’s sentencing range based on offense-related conduct that the prior jury did not need to find.
ACCA’s occasions clause, as this Court construed it in Wooden, requires exactly that kind of factual inquiry. The whole point of the clause, in fact, is to require something more than just three convictions before an ACCA sentence is imposed. As such, the Apprendi rule directly applies to it.
That leaves amicus’s objection that the Court should eschew that straightforward analysis because applying the Sixth Amendment here would somehow harm defendants. But, as the briefs filed by criminal defense organizations show in this case, that concern is unfounded.
Guilty pleas, waivers, and stipulations make the occasions clause not really an issue that is litigated in most cases. However, in the rare instances where a defendant disputes both the occasions issue and the underlying Section 922(g) charge, bifurcation is a time-honored solution that courts have already shown they can apply to resolve the issue while avoiding prejudice to the defendant.
This Court should endorse that practice and reverse the court of appeals. I’m happy to take the Court’s questions.
Judge: Counsel, wouldn’t it be more straightforward to overrule Almendarez-Torres?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: That is certainly one option the Court could consider if necessary, but—
Judge: Do you think we should?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I believe the Court should at some point, but I don’t believe it’s necessary in this case. Our position is that what the Court has already stated in Mathis and Descamps makes it clear that the occasions clause falls outside of Almendarez-Torres. Our briefing did not focus on overruling that precedent because the Court typically does not consider overturning a case unless it is essential to the decision at hand.
Judge: But don’t you and the government essentially agree on where to draw the line?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I believe we agree on many aspects, Your Honor. We agree that the test is whether the fact at issue is part of the prior conviction. The government describes this as whether the fact is "integral" to the conviction, while we use the term "inherent." We may have some differences on the margins regarding how that test applies, but this case does not present those issues.
Judge: Almendarez-Torres is an established precedent of this Court that has been relied upon and reaffirmed in subsequent cases. If we were to reexamine it, would it then be appropriate to reconsider the entire Apprendi question? Or are you asking us to only revisit the portion that benefits your argument?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I am not asking the Court to do that today, Your Honor.
Judge: But you seem to have taken the suggestion.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I suppose if the Court did revisit that question, it would be fair to go back to first principles. As the Court’s opinions in Apprendi demonstrated, those first principles support the right to a jury trial on all facts necessary to impose a sentence.
PLAINTIFF:

The Plaintiff suggests that the Court should not increase a defendant's sentencing range based on offense-related conduct that the prior jury did not need to find.
