Dialogue:
Judge: All parties, please raise your right hand. Court is now in session.
Judge: Jared Judson, you are suing Samuel Grossman and Alder LLC. You are the owner and you are a principal in the business, and you are representing yourself?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: You are suing for $500 for the removal of rubbish that was his responsibility that he didn't remove. Okay, talk to me. You bought a house from him?
Plaintiff: Bought a house from him. The day before the closing, we did a walk-through, and he wasn't there. I did a walk-through with the listing agent, and there was all this garbage—construction material, metal pipes, bricks, pavers, garbage, regular trash from people eating lunch, garbage bags. There was a ton of garbage there.
Judge: So, you go to the closing.
Plaintiff: I mentioned it to the agent.
Judge: You did not go to the closing? You had your attorney there?
Plaintiff: I was not at the closing, Your Honor. My attorney represented me.
Judge: So, you complained to the attorney, "Look, there's all this garbage, and I want it gone."
Plaintiff: First, I mentioned it to the listing agent who was with me at the final walkthrough, and she said, "We'll take care of it at closing."
Judge: So, at closing, you bring it up, and what happened?
Plaintiff: I brought it up to the attorney, his attorney, and he didn't know anything about it. He left the room. I don't know if he made a phone call to him.
Judge: Did your attorney call you from the closing and tell you that he wants it to be made part of the paperwork?
Defendant: No, Your Honor. I was out of the state.
Judge: Well, your attorney was your representative there, and according to the paperwork in the closing, he agreed on your behalf to have you be responsible to remove the garbage.
Defendant: Indeed.
Judge: So, then what happens? You close?
Plaintiff: At closing, he handwrites just a rider to the closing documents saying that it'll get taken care of. I waited. I called the law office a few days after I moved in or the next week, and they said the defendant was out of the country. I had to wait until he got back. So, I waited till the date that he got back, I don't remember exactly when, and I called again. I called his attorney's office many times. Finally, they said he was going to stop by sometime to take a look.
One day, he rang my Ring doorbell. I was out of town, and I saw him on my phone. He asked me to call him, so I did. He told me, "Look, I'm not taking care of this stuff. You must have persuaded my attorney somehow to write this contract up, but I never agreed to it, and I'm not taking care of it." He said, "If you want, I'll take the white fence that was in the garage. I know about that, I'll take it to the curb for you." I knew the sanitation department doesn’t take building materials, so I would get fined if that was out there. I told him no, don’t do anything. He said, "Well, if you don’t like it, sue me." So, that’s what I did.
Judge: Why don’t you tell me about that visit to his house?
Defendant: In fact, Your Honor, I have visited his location a few times. There was no answer. And apparently, he forgets that in the agreement signed by my lawyer, it needed the final water bill, reestablishing a door that was closed by me to obtain a certificate of occupancy, as well as some other garbage. The word "garbage" was never defined. The bricks he’s mentioning were left there as a nice way to have a seat or whatever.
Judge: What is this here? I guess at some point, this used to be a fire pit?
Plaintiff: I wasn’t concerned with that.
Judge: What is this pile?
Defendant: These are bricks that were on the side of the fence, which matched other parts of the bricks. We left them there in case he had any broken ones so he could use them.
Judge: Do these bricks match pavers that you have somewhere in the house?
Plaintiff: No, they don’t. I don’t know what they’re there for.
Judge: Now we’re looking at garbage. This is the main part. Is this the fence you were talking about?
Plaintiff: Yes, that’s the fence I had to remove. Anything white, vinyl, that’s what I had to remove from the garage.
Judge: Where’s the garbage you talked about?
Plaintiff: You see that bag on the left? If you keep scrolling through the pictures, you'll see more garbage—pieces of wood, metal pipes, trash.
Judge: Can I see the contract that got amended? From both parties, please.
(Reviews contract)
Judge: The rider states, "Seller agrees to obtain and pay the final water meter, clear all rubbish from rear yard, and install a door to basement as was existing at the time of the original listing with the broker." I presume the water meter bill and the door have been fixed?
Plaintiff: The door was never fixed, but I don’t care about that anymore.
Judge: So, you did not remove the rubbish in the rear?
Defendant: I did not remove the rubbish in the rear.
Judge: You called a company to take it away and paid $500?
Plaintiff: Yes. I have an invoice as proof.
Judge: Let me see it.
(Reviews invoice)
Judge: Vinyl, metal, masonry, miscellaneous garbage—$500. Would have been so much easier for you to just take that stuff out. A deal is a deal. Your lawyer made an agreement on your behalf. You should have paid someone $50 or $100 to get it done. Instead, we’re here. Verdict for the plaintiff—$500
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff, Jared Judson, is suing Samuel Grossman and Alder LLC for $500 due to the removal of rubbish that was Samuel's responsibility but was not removed. During the court session, the Plaintiff explains that he bought a house from Samuel and found a significant amount of garbage, including construction materials, on the property during a walk-through the day before the closing. He mentions this to the listing agent, who promises to take care of it at closing.

At the closing, the Plaintiff's attorney brings up the issue, but Samuel's attorney is unaware of it.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant, Jared Judson, is suing Samuel Grossman and Alder LLC for $500 due to the removal of rubbish that was Samuel's responsibility but was not removed. The rubbish included construction materials, garbage, and regular trash. During the closing, the Defendant was not present, and the responsibility for removing the rubbish was agreed upon in the closing documents by the Defendant's attorney.

Dialogue:
Judge: Well, I mean, that's in part because the facilities closed down and you -- the material is left where the facility used to be.
But is there any comparator for a large amount -- I forget how many metric tons we're talking about here -- is transported to a separate private facility for virtually permanent storage?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Well, I guess the -- the GE Morris facility has been in -- is a standalone facility that's been in operation since, you know, I think around 1980 or before. So I -- I don't think the volumes are the same as the ones that ISP contemplates.
But the -- the two things I would say are, first, the -- the volume of waste is not going to -- in the United States, is not going to change depending on whether licenses like these are granted.
Granting license to possess the spent nuclear fuel in a storage facility is not going to increase the volume of nuclear waste.
It's just going to change where in the country it would be stored.
And with respect to permanence versus temporary status, the -- there's no reason to think that if the Court rules against us and the waste has to stay at the site of the decommissioned reactors, it will stay there for any shorter period of time.
It's still going to stay somewhere until a permanent repository is opened up.
And the third thing -- and this goes to the point I was making before about market-based solutions and ISP's motivation -- part of the suboptimal character of continued storage at the decommissioned sites is that you have a bunch of places around the country that now serve no other purpose but to store spent nuclear fuel when once they were operating reactors.
And, clearly, ISP and some of its potential contracting parties think that it would be better to centralize the fuel at one location so that the other locations could be returned to what's been referred to as greenfield status.
Judge: Thank you.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: They can be put to alternative uses.
Judge: Thank you. Justice Alito?
Judge: Is there more -- excuse me. Is there more security around facilities that are owned by the federal government than around these private facilities?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: I -- I don't know the answer to that.
I mean, certainly, the Commission, in determining whether the -- the licensee has met the requirements, wants to be -- wants to verify that there will be what the Commission views as adequate security arrangements.
Judge: Suppose this is allowed and 40 years go by, and then there's an application to renew the license.
Would it be permanent at that time, or what if it's renewed and it's another 40 years?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: It would still not --
Judge: It will never become permanent?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: It would still not be permanent.
And, again, you would have the same dilemma if the waste is left at the decommissioned nuclear reactor sites.
That is, at some point, the storage -- the -- the materials license will expire.
The Commission will have to decide whether a new license should be issued.
We're going to confront that -- until a repository is made available, we're going to confront that dilemma at some locations within the country.
It's just a question of where those locations will be.
And the other point I would make about security at federal versus private, the -- the decommissioned reactor sites I'm referring to are also private sites.
They -- they would be governed by the same arrangements that would govern ISP.
Judge: Which statutory provision -- I know you cite a number of them. Which one do you think provides the strongest support for your argument?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Well, I think -- I would say two things.
The first -- first, I would point the Court to the licensing procedure -- provisions in the Atomic Energy Act, which are 42 U.S.C. 2073(a), which deals with special nuclear material; 2093, which deals with source material; and 2111, which deals with by-product material.
And the Commission, from 1980, has regarded those -- has had published regulations that treat those as authority to license private storage of spent nuclear fuel.
The other thing I would point the Court to in the Policy Act is that the Policy Act was enacted in 1982, two years after the Commission's rules had been promulgated. Congress clearly expressed its approval of private storage, focusing on onsite storage, but it didn't create new licensing mechanisms for that to occur.
And so --
Judge: And -- thank you, Mr. Stewart.
One other -- one final question. 2073(a) refers to special nuclear material, not to spent nuclear waste, and special nuclear material has a -- a specific narrow definition.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Yes, and there is also a provision -- I think it's 22-0 -- 01(h) -- that says various authorizations can be combined in a single license.
And the -- the -- the three constituent parts of spent nuclear fuel that require a license are special nuclear material, source material, and by-product material.
And so the Commission has always believed that a license that covers each of those will be sufficient to cover spent nuclear fuel because there's nothing else that needs to be licensed.
And then the other point I would make is, if that were not true, the Commission would be equally unable to license onsite storage because these are the same provisions it relies on to license storage at the site of a nuclear reactor.
Judge: Justice Sotomayor?
Judge: Counsel, these -- the onsite storage requires security to watch this product -- inert product and make sure that nobody breaks in.
So what we're talking about is that there is a danger to the community by these inactive facilities holding on to the spent nuclear waste because the degree of store -- the -- the cost associated with the storage in terms of security is greater, isn't it?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Yes.
And that was one of the justifications that ISP gave, that it was -- it was more economical to have security for one centralized facility than to have separate security for different facilities around the country.
Judge: 2201 basically authorizes the agency "to establish by rule, regulation, or order such standards and instructions to govern the possession and use of special nuclear material, including all the by-product materials, as the Commission may deem necessary or desirable to promote the common defense and security or to protect health or to minimize danger to life or property."
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Yeah.
And so the Commission did --
Judge: I look at that as the direct authorization to set forth the terms of possession and license, correct?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Yes.
And I -- I think the Commission was on solid ground when it promulgated the rules in 1980, but when Congress stepped in two years later and enacted some new provisions but without disturbing the preexisting licensing scheme, that was an effective ratification.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff's statements suggest that they are arguing for the licensing of a private storage facility for spent nuclear fuel, as opposed to continued storage at decommissioned reactor sites. The Plaintiff argues that this change would not increase the volume of nuclear waste, but would instead centralize it for easier management and potential return of the decommissioned sites to "greenfield status" for alternative uses. The Plaintiff also emphasizes that the security arrangements for the private facility would be similar to those for the decommissioned reactor sites, and that the licensing of the private facility is supported by provisions in the Atomic Energy Act and the Nuclear Waste Policy Act.
DEFENDANT:
The Defendant, represented by the Lawyer of Plaintiff, argues that the storage of spent nuclear fuel in private facilities is not only legal but also economically and security-wise more advantageous than storing it at decommissioned reactor sites. The Defendant's goal is to centralize the storage of spent nuclear fuel in one location, allowing the decommissioned reactor sites to be repurposed.

1. The Defendant believes that centralizing the storage of spent nuclear fuel will be more economical due to the cost-effectiveness of security for one centralized facility compared to separate security for different facilities around the country.
2.

Dialogue:
Judge: All right. Finally, with respect to the previous question, I guess I'm wondering what, if anything, we can do about the oddity of the standard in this context. It's in the statute, and so I don't know whether we can just read the statute to say something other than it says because we think this is odd to have it here.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: I mean, you can—we certainly agree that you can read the statutory language in light of the relevant opinion because—both because the statute—
Judge: The statute tells you you're supposed to do that.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: It did, both by drawing specific language from that opinion and by including a separate citation to it. But I don’t think the Court can get away from the fact that Congress chose this particular formulation as its distillation of that opinion.
Judge: And that's because, as you said in the beginning, "significantly affects" means something, right? Congress could have used another formulation if it just wanted preemption regarding any law that relates to this, right?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: It could have used—
Judge: They say that in another statute, for example, where it says a law is preempted if it relates.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yes.
Judge: And so that's easy to apply, but here, they didn't say that.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yes. And sometimes some preemption provisions say a state can't enforce a law that is different from or in addition to the requirements of federal law, meaning a state can attach additional consequences to conduct that already violates federal law but can't go beyond that. And Congress didn’t choose anything like that here.
Judge: Thank you.
Judge: Thank you, counsel.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Thank you.
Judge: Counsel for the plaintiff.
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer: Thank you, Your Honor, and may it please the Court. State law significantly interferes with the exercise of national banking powers in two respects. First, the law controls the interest rate on mortgage accounts. Second, a patchwork of 50 state laws would unduly burden national banks, destroying their uniform federal character.
Now, the other side posits that "significantly interferes" requires factual proof that a state law would hinder a banking power to some unspecified degree. But "significantly interferes" can be both quantitative and qualitative. A state law that dictates the attributes of a banking product interferes with national banking power in a qualitative sense, just as courts telling prosecutors what charges to bring would significantly interfere with executive power.
The relevant opinion uses the term "significantly interferes" in a qualitative sense. That opinion reasons that state laws are preempted absent any indication that Congress intended to subject the banking power to local conditions. And here, we know Congress intended the opposite.
First, federal law comprehensively regulates state mortgage escrow accounts in order to protect consumers without requiring any interest. And second, Congress speaks expressly when it contemplates state interest laws. It did so for state usury laws, and federal law itself requires interest on certain mortgage escrow loans but not those of the plaintiff.
It is unfathomable that Congress intended the other side’s test. They never told you what interest rate would be too much, what to do when market forces change, and how courts should proceed bank by bank. But national banks need to know their regulatory obligations ahead of time. It would create seismic uncertainty if the laws of 50 states could apply to every banking product and service—not just every feature of a mortgage but everything from interest rates on savings and checking accounts to ATM fees to credit card reward programs.
Congress surely intended a preemption standard that preserves the stability and predictability that undergirds a safe and sound banking system. I welcome questions.
Judge: Counsel, do we treat express banking powers the same as incidental banking powers? It would seem that you would have to somehow have a way to fathom what these incidental powers are.
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer: No. There are enumerated powers in the statute, and incidental powers are defined as necessary powers to the business of banking. The only enumerated ones are basically lending money, taking deposits, and making real estate loans. What interest a bank charges is so fundamental to a banking product and the banking power that it would seem absurd to say a state could dictate the interest rate on something like a savings account just because that’s an incidental power.
Judge: Well, I agree with you on that. In a prior case, I think it was statutory, right? It was express. But what I’m more interested in is the creation of an escrow account, then interest rate on the escrow account, which is not something a bank would normally have to do.
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer: That’s correct. Thirteen state laws require it. Since 1973, we’ve had federal regulations that never required interest. There are thousands of words of regulations and interpretive statements regulating escrow accounts under federal law, and none of it requires interest.
The other side would argue that states could now make amendments to every single one of those requirements, and banks would have to seek declaratory judgments on each and every requirement just for escrows. And when you extend that logic to everything a bank does, it becomes mind-boggling. National banks provide countless products and services.
Judge: There are amici and the logic of the lower court ruling that suggest—and based on your test—that no state consumer law would be permitted. But there’s an express permission for state consumer laws. So which ones do you say are okay?
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer: The definition of a "state consumer financial law" versus the law that’s preempted under our test focuses on what is being controlled. It’s not simply about state regulation. The key question is: what is being controlled? Is it the national banking power, or is it the financial transaction with the consumer?
Judge: I’m sorry, what’s not controlling the financial transaction with the consumer here?
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer: Let me explain. When the state dictates the attribute of the product or service—as opposed to the interaction with the consumer—it is preempted. Under that definition, state laws prohibiting racial discrimination, fraud, or certain banking-specific rules are not preempted.
For example, in a key prior case, the Court ruled that a banking-specific law was not an unlawful encroachment on national banks' rights, nor did it interfere with an authorized function. The law merely changed the identity of the account holder with a lawful right to demand payment, without interfering with the banking power itself.
Other categories of state consumer financial laws that are not preempted regulate the manner and terms of financial transactions with consumers. For instance, every state has laws regarding the age of majority for obtaining a mortgage—usually 18, but in some states, 21. States also have laws governing when the statute of frauds applies to contracts.
Judge: But aren’t national banks interacting with consumers pursuant to their banking powers? Why don’t those two categories collapse?
Plaintiff: ’s Lawyer: They don’t collapse because, since 1870, the Court has maintained that state contract and tort laws still apply to banks. There’s no federal common law of contracts or torts. So, in their daily lives, banks remain subject to state laws more than federal laws because states provide the underlying contract and tort law framework.
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff's statements suggest that they believe the statute in question, which uses the term "significantly interferes" to determine preemption, should be interpreted in a qualitative sense, rather than a quantitative one. The Plaintiff argues that state laws that dictate the attributes of a banking product significantly interfere with national banking powers, even if they do not have a quantifiable impact.

The Plaintiff's goal or motive behind their words is to persuade the court to interpret the statute in a way that allows national banks to operate with stability and predictability, free from the potential application of 50 different state laws to their various products and services. They argue that a more flexible interpretation of the statute is necessary to preserve the uniform federal character of national banks and to avoid creating seismic uncertainty in the banking industry.

In summary, the Plaintiff's intent is to argue for a broad interpretation of the statute's preemption standard, one that allows national banks to operate with stability and predictability by preempting state laws that dictate the attributes of banking products, while still allowing for state regulation of banking interactions with consumers.
DEFENDANT:
The Defendant's lawyer argues that the statute in question, which uses the term "significantly interferes" to determine preemption, should be interpreted in a qualitative sense, not just quantitatively. They contend that a state law that dictates the attributes of a banking product, such as the interest rate on a mortgage account, significantly interferes with national banking powers in a qualitative sense, as it undermines the stability and predictability of the banking system.

The Defendant's lawyer also argues that the term "significantly interferes" is used in a qualitative sense in the relevant opinion, which reasons that state laws are preempted absent any indication that Congress intended to subject the banking power to local conditions. They further argue that Congress intended a preemption standard that preserves the stability and predictability of the banking system.

The Defendant's lawyer also clarifies that the term "state consumer financial law" should be defined by what is being controlled, not just state regulation.

Dialogue:
Judge: Well, Your Honor, in Twitter, you were applying aiding-and-abetting principles that arose in the context of a murder when you were talking about social media platforms.
Judge: I think the whole point of Twitter was that there is a set of general aiding-and-abetting principles, and that is the law that informs what aiding-and-abetting liability is.
Plaintiff: But I thought we were --
Judge: I don't even know how you would do this kind of statute-specific aiding-and-abetting liability outside of the general principles of aiding and abetting.
Plaintiff: Well, I -- I thought we were only looking at aiding and abetting to the extent that Congress mentions that in an example in the statute.
Plaintiff: So what we're really doing is trying to understand what Congress intended with respect to the exception that it put in this statute.
Plaintiff: And so, to the extent that it references aiding-and-abetting liability in one of the examples, that is just to illuminate the meaning of the statutory terms that exist there, right? I mean, it's sort of odd to me that suddenly a common law of aiding-and-abetting liability is coming in to, in your view, be dispositive of how we think about this case.
Judge: Sure.
Judge: Well, I think that it's an example.
Judge: The -- the reference to aiding and abetting in the statute is an example of a violation that then triggers the statutory exception and the proximate cause analysis.
Judge: So you have to understand then what it means to aid and abet a particular crime. Again, I don't --
Plaintiff: As Congress intended it for the purpose of this statute in --
Judge: Well, and -- and -- and I think it's not that plausible to say that Congress had some completely idiosyncratic view of what aiding and abetting was for this statute as opposed to just looking to the principles like this Court looked to in Twitter, which are just the basic aiding-and-abetting principles that are covered in criminal law.
Plaintiff: Can I ask you about the proximate cause because I'm still a little confused about where you start your proximate cause analysis.
Plaintiff: I listened as you discussed with Justice Thomas the steps, the series of steps, from your clients to the alleged ultimate harm, but it seemed to me that the first moment of illegality in the chain, as you articulated it, was the retailers selling to the straw purchasers.
Plaintiff: Am I right about that? You say your clients do things that are legal. They sell to --
Judge: Mm-hmm.
Plaintiff: -- other legal buyers and -- et cetera, et cetera, until we get to that straw purchaser point, right?
Judge: That is the first moment of illegality, but I don't think that's the sole step relevant to a general proximate cause analysis.
Plaintiff: Right. But we don't have a general proximate cause analysis.
Plaintiff: We have a statute.
Plaintiff: And the statute makes clear that we're starting with an action in which a manufacturer or seller of a qualified product knowingly violated a state or federal statute applicable to the sale or marketing of the product.
Plaintiff: So it seems to me that the first step, given this statute, is the moment of violation, of illegality, as opposed to some theoretical original point.
Judge: And my answer to that question is no, but it doesn't matter.
Plaintiff: No --
Judge: No --
Plaintiff: I understand you think you make it anyway --
Judge: Yeah.
Plaintiff: -- but I'm just trying to understand why you --
Judge: And it's because what the --
Plaintiff: -- why you're insisting --
Judge: Yeah.
Plaintiff: -- that it's way back here.
Judge: Because what the statute says is there has to be a proximate cause between the defendant's violation. Our violation is not the illegal sale itself.
Judge: We don't sell to consumers.
Plaintiff: But you say you don't violate at the time the beginning. So I don't know what your violation is unless it's the point of illegality.
Judge: It's the aiding and abetting. That is the whole aiding-and-abetting theory.
Plaintiff: All right.
Judge: Thank you.
Judge: Thank you, counsel.
Judge: Ms. Stetson.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Mexico's complaint pleads that Petitioners aided and abetted violations of specific federal gun laws and that those violations proximately caused Mexico's harm. That satisfies PLCAA's predicate exception.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: First, the complaint details that Petitioners deliberately supplied the illegal Mexican market by selling guns through the small number of dealers that they know sell a large number of crime guns and who repeatedly sell in bulk to the cartel traffickers.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Petitioners' arguments ignore these allegations.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Next, as the Court said in Twitter, an aider and abetter is liable for harms that were a foreseeable risk of that violation.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: That framing, foreseeable risk, is the proximate cause question.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: As this Court put it in Bank of America, does the harm alleged have a sufficiently close connection to the conduct the statute prohibits? The answer is yes.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: The laws broken here are designed to keep guns out of criminals' hands.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Those violations put guns in criminals' hands and those criminals harmed Mexico.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Petitioners' arguments would rewrite PLCAA and proximate cause law far beyond this case.

So, when this -- not to turn this into an ERISA case, but, in ERISA, Congress specifically said -- in Section 502(a)(3), it referred to equitable relief.
And so, in cases like Great West and Sereboff and Montanile, this Court had to delve into these archaic distinctions in equity about what kind of tracing was required in particular circumstances.
That's not what Congress did when it passed this jurisdictional provision in the FSIA which codifies the common law.
The FSIA comes from common law.
We know that from cases like Samantar.
And so Congress wasn't invoking these equitable principles in this --in the -- in the FSIA.
The tracing rules don't provide the answer here.

Judge: Excuse me, may I ask one follow-up question on that? So I understand your answer to these hypotheticals is that we must look to Congress's historical practices. This is a matter of seeing whether the setup that we have before us is consistent with Congress's historical practices. Is that right?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: We draw heavily on historical practices, also text—
Judge: Is that—
Defendant: 's Lawyer: —of course.
Judge: Is that the test?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I don't want to lose sight of that.
Judge: Is that the test? Is it the test?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I think that the test in this context, as in most separation of powers cases, is, yes, text and history. And here again, we have a specific constitutional provision speaking to duration, speaking to particular types of functions, showing that the Framers were concerned about funding the Army with a standing appropriation but didn't have that same concern or effort to restrict Congress's authority with respect to other functions. And then we have an unbroken line of history. There have been agencies funded this way for every year of this nation's history.
Judge: Well, what is your best historical—your single best example of an agency that has all of the features that the opposing party has cited? Your single best example of an agency with that combination of features?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I think our best example historically is the Customs Service. The first Congress created the Customs Service in 1789. It gave the Customs Service a standing, uncapped source of funding from the revenues that the Customs Service collected through things including coercive law enforcement activity, things like levying fines, also from import duties, which could not be avoided if you wanted to engage in trade with the new nation. And the Customs Service was one of the most powerful agencies that was originally created because it was so important to have a stream of funding for the new republic. So I think that if you look through all of the factors they're challenging here, we have the Customs Service and others—the revenue officers; U.S. Attorneys, for a period of time, were funded through conviction fees.
Judge: What is your best example of an agency that draws its money from another agency that, in turn, does not get its money from a congressional appropriation in the normal sense of that term but gets it from the private sector?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I can't give you another example of a source that's precisely like that one, but I would dispute the premise that that could possibly be constitutionally relevant. This is a case about Congress's own prerogatives over the purse—its authority. And if Congress has given away too much of its authority by not providing for a durational limit or providing too much discretion to the agency, then I don't see how it could possibly fix the problem that other fee-funded agencies directly collect their money from the entities they regulate.
Judge: So I take it your answer is that this is not consistent with any historical practice, but you think that to the extent it is unprecedented, it is unprecedented in a way that is not relevant for present purposes? Is that your answer?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yes, primarily. I think it would be unprecedented in the way that you could say this is the only agency that has the acronym CFPB. That's obviously true also, but it doesn't track the constitutional value. But I also just want to make the factual point that I don't understand them to be saying it's significant that it's structured this way in the abstract. They say what it means is that there's not a check on the overall amount of funding the CFPB could get, and there is a check on those other agencies. And that's wrong as a descriptive matter. There is no similar check on the Federal Reserve Board, the FDIC, the NCUA, the FCA, or the FHFA. All of the entities they regulate cannot exit their regulatory sphere just because they disagree with regulations.
Judge: Thank you, counsel.

Dialogue:
Judge: Yeah.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: So long as he doesn't have a defense outside of bankruptcy.
Judge: Yeah.
Judge: Justice Kavanaugh?
Judge: Just one question about what work 106(a) does for 544(b) if --if you prevail.
I mean, your position would be, well, it's still doing work vis-- vis the states, right? And is it odd -- I mean, just kind of walk me through this double layer thing. I mean, as I understand your argument, it's that 544(b) has a nested cause of action in it under applicable law, so you're standing in the shoes of the creditor pursuing someone under state law.
If under state law you could recover that money from a governmental entity that would otherwise have state sovereign immunity, then you have a claim that's been nested by virtue of the under applicable law, but because the vehicle through which the trustee is asserting that cause of action is 544(b), you still need a separate abrogation of the state sovereign immunity to move forward and that's the work that 106(a) is doing?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Yes.
That's right.
Let me try to say it back to you and see if you think that you agree.
Judge: Okay.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: The work that 106(a) is doing as to 544(b) in that situation is the same work it's doing as to the other avoidance provisions that are referenced in 106(a), which is these are federal code provisions.
They would not normally apply to a sovereign absent a waiver, abrogation of immunity, so you would, let's say, take 548, the federal fraudulent transfer provision.
Normally, you could bring that against a private party, but there's no indication you could bring that against the United States or against that state sovereign.
106(a) allows the trustee to assert that cause of action against a sovereign, absolutely, the federal --
Judge: Yeah.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: -- cause of action. You're right to say that the way 544(b) works is by looking to what would have happened under state law, whether there's a viable avoidance action outside state law.
And that's where that state's waiver of sovereign immunity comes into being, which is that that state has exposed itself to fraudulent transfer liability in its own courts.
The trustee can now mirror that inside the bankruptcy.
Judge: But, absent 106(a), under the way that you're viewing this, the trustee could not proceed under 544(b)?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: That's right, and that's the purpose of 106(a)'s waiver as to the 59 provisions.
It's to allow those federal code provisions to be applied, invoked, enforced against sovereign entities.
That's the work it's doing.
Judge: So the other side says, well, that's pretty weird because then you're looking for two waivers of sovereign immunity or an abrogation of sovereign -- two abrogations of waiver and an abrogation that you have to double-team in order to go.
You want to respond to that?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Yes.
Thanks.
I don't think that our position is asking for two waivers.
To the contrary, what our position is resting on the premise of is that when Congress made a provision like 544(b), which turns on liability that exists outside the code, it doesn't mean to affect that by waiving immunity inside the bankruptcy proceeding.
So we disagree with the premise that Congress wanted to accomplish this thing.
But you're right to say that if Congress wanted to accomplish it, the way to do it would be it has to do something about 544(b). And the answer would be to alter -- the most obvious answer would be to alter the way 544(b) operates.
And instead of operating on the basis of an action that's actually viable outside bankruptcy, which, again, is not our interpretation -- it is the uniform understanding for over a hundred years of 544(b) and its predecessor provisions -- would be, instead of requiring that, it would say something like, in 544(b), transfers to the United States are avoidable to the same manner and the same extent as transfers to a private party, similar to what Congress did in the FTCA context.
Judge: Justice Jackson?
Judge: I think the thing I found interesting in the exchange you just had with Justice Barrett is that she approached the analysis by starting with 544 and you started with 106(a).
And I wonder if that might be the sort of -- to the extent there's any disconnect, and I don't think there is, but the framing is slightly different.
You say we need the waiver of sovereign immunity to start to allow for the trustee to bring an action.
And then, to determine whether or not that action can proceed or is successful or whatnot, you go to 544, which says you have to allow -- the trustee can proceed only to the extent that he could -- that an actual creditor could outside of bankruptcy, and sovereign immunity there can do the work to prevent the trustee from proceeding.
Is that how you're viewing this?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: I actually don't think it matters where you start.
I think where --
Judge: Okay.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: --where Justice Barrett started was perfectly fine too.
Judge: Okay.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: This is more just conceptually trying to understand what's going on here as sort of the whole code, what is Congress doing here.
And 106(a) absolutely waives immunity as to these federal code provisions.
It doesn't matter whether you do that at the end of the analysis or at the beginning of the analysis.
But, when it does that, it specifically says it's not altering the substance of those provisions.
So what do you do as a court adjudicating an action brought under one of these provisions? You go look at what are the substantive terms.
For most of them, the substantive terms don't implicate something happening outside of bankruptcy.
But 544(b) works differently, and it's long been understood to work differently.
You have to go look at what's happening outside of bankruptcy.
And nothing in 106(a) suggests that Congress meant to affect what's happening outside of bankruptcy or that requirement that you look to what's happening outside of bankruptcy.
Essentially, what the trustee is asking for here is that when you look at 544(b)'s actual creditor requirement, you close your eyes or you disregard sovereign immunity that hasn't been waived, that continues to exist.
And that's where the theory is wrong and it's why the 544(b) claim fails on the merits.
Judge: Thank you.
Judge: Thank you, counsel.
Lawyer of Defendant: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: 106 waiver with respect to 544 means that trustees can avoid fraudulent transfers inside bankruptcy even though sovereign immunity applies outside bankruptcy. "With respect to," even read very narrowly, means directly relating to 544.
And the waiver that concededly applies to the trustee's claim has the same direct relationship to the incorporated state law elements.
No textual or logical distinction exists between the two.
106(a)(2) also lets courts hear any issue respecting 544's application to governments, so courts can thus hear these claims without regard to sovereign immunity.
Congress waived immunity knowing that 544 has always required trustees to step into creditors' shoes under state law.
By waiv ing immunity, Congress clearly expected trustees to sue governments by relying on state law.
Congress could not have plausibly intended to waive immunity only to see it smuggled in through the back door under the guise of applying state law.
Nor is it plausible that Congress has ever waived immunity but only contingent on a second waiver.
No such statute exists in the U.S. Code, nor does any statute contain a double waiver.
Congress spoke expressly when it wanted to give t he IRS special treatment and to make exceptions for fraudulent transfers, but it did neither for the IRS in 544.
The government's position overrides these choices and allows the I RS to keep assets that every other transferor would have to return.
That result would prevent the trustee from recouping this money and paying it to the bus drivers and the mechanics and the vendors, who certainly gave All Resort more value than the IRS did.
All -- the government's position finally --destroys creditor equality.
Where governments are creditors, like they are here, the government gets to keep the fraudulent transfer and its share of a much smaller pie.
I welcome the Court's questions.

However, they argue that 544(b) requires looking at what would have happened under state law, and that state's waiver of sovereign immunity is necessary for the trustee to proceed with the action.

Judge: Well, now that's interesting.

Judge: Okay.

No good.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: The congressional policy is in the PLRA.
It doesn't -- it does --
Judge: It says exhaustion, but it doesn't talk about juries.

PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff's argument is centered around the Seventh Amendment right to a jury trial in civil cases. The Plaintiff contends that the Seventh Amendment only preserves the jury's role when both equitable and legal claims are fully before the court and the prerequisites for the case to proceed have been met. The Plaintiff argues that if the prerequisites are not met, the case does not proceed, and preclusion does not prevent the jury from performing their fact-finding role.

In the context of the Prison Litigation Reform Act (PLRA), the Plaintiff argues that Congress has the power to set forth what is required to get a claim to a jury, and the PLRA does not explicitly state that a jury trial is not allowed.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant's argument is centered around the interpretation of the Prison Litigation Reform Act (PLRA) in relation to the Seventh Amendment right to a jury trial. The Defendant argues that the PLRA does not violate the Seventh Amendment, even if it results in a prisoner not being able to have a jury trial, because the PLRA sets the requirements for a prisoner to bring a suit, including the exhaustion of remedies before filing a lawsuit. The Defendant also argues that the PLRA does not explicitly state that a judge, rather than a jury, must decide the case, and therefore, the Seventh Amendment right to a jury trial is not violated.

Judge: Right. My daughter comes up to me and says, ‘Listen, this car that’s registered in your name and the insurance in your name, I’m not supposed to drive without you or a licensed driver, but I’m just going to be driving anyway, Mom, so hand me the car.’ I’m going to do that? No.
Judge: That’s my daughter—she’s my flesh and blood. Why would you do that for a neighbor? I don’t care how many years you’ve known him. Why would you put your name on the lawsuit that he has when he runs over 20 orphans at a stoplight?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: That’s going to be you who’s going to be sued because the car is in your name. So what—but what I don’t understand is—
Plaintiff: Well, we were all friends.
Judge: Well, then that’s the time—just like he has a hard face and—what do we call it in Spanish? He’s got the nerve to come up to you and say, ‘I would like for you to put this—can you do that for me?’ You have the same nerve to say, ‘Sweetheart, I love you, I’ve known you 19 years, but I am not putting in my name a car with a man who doesn’t have a license. Go get a license.’ That’s what I would say. Is that so hard?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: Alright. So how’d that work out for you, by the way?
Plaintiff: Not good.
Judge: Because what happened? You put the car in your name, you registered it in your name, you got insurance, and then what happened?
Plaintiff: Over—well, literally overnight. It was August 12th, he got the car. August 13th, I got the insurance online. August 14th, I printed out the insurance.
Judge: And when did he get his first ticket that he didn’t pay?
Plaintiff: Oh, that’s the other thing I forgot. If he gets tickets—parking tickets—the tickets are going to be issued to the car that is in your name. So it’ll go eventually against your license if they’re unpaid. I forgot that part.
Judge: Apparently, you forgot that part. No, you knew that part, because you told him when you had the discussion, didn’t you?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Didn’t you tell him, ‘If anything happens, if you get tickets, you got to pay them’?
Plaintiff: Yes, we discussed that.
Judge: You actually discussed—so you, unlike me, because I don’t really think that I’m going to get a ticket, you actually thought he might get a ticket and said, ‘If you get a ticket, it’s on you.’
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Mr. Smith, as we’ve discussed, there were multiple unpaid tickets on your vehicle. Are you disputing that these tickets were issued to your car?
Defendant: No, Your Honor, but I wasn’t aware of all of them. I moved addresses, and I never received notices for some of these tickets.
Judge: The records show that the city mailed multiple notices to the address registered with the DMV. Did you update your registration when you moved?
Defendant: No, but I wasn’t trying to avoid anything. I just didn’t know I had to update it immediately.
Judge: Unfortunately, the responsibility to keep your registration updated falls on you. The law presumes that notices sent to the last registered address are received.
Plaintiff: Your Honor, our company followed city regulations. The vehicle was flagged for immobilization due to unpaid citations, and after the defendant failed to pay within the required period, we legally towed and impounded it.
Defendant: But I didn’t even know it was towed until days later! I thought it was stolen!
Judge: Did you attempt to contact the parking authority after realizing your car was missing?
Defendant: Yes, but by then, the fees had already gone up. It felt like they were just trying to take advantage of me.
Judge: I understand your frustration, but the tow company acted within its rights. Mr. Smith, I’m ruling in favor of the plaintiff. You are responsible for the towing and impound fees.
Defendant: This is unfair. I want to file a counterclaim for the stress and inconvenience.
Judge: I’ve reviewed your counterclaim, and I do not find sufficient legal grounds to proceed. Your claim is denied.
Defendant: But—
Judge: This case is closed.
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff, Shena White, is suing her long-time neighbor Clarence Lan for $337 in unpaid parking tickets on a car registered in her name, which he obtained and failed to pay. She is also counterclaiming for $1,960 for insurance costs and the value of the car that was towed and lost due to Clarence's negligent driving.

The Plaintiff initially agreed to help Clarence register and insure the car, as they were close friends, but she warned him about the risks involved since he did not have a valid driver's license at the time. Despite her warnings, Clarence obtained the car, and within a few days, he began accumulating unpaid parking tickets.
DEFENDANT:
The Defendant, Shena White, is suing her long-time neighbor Clarence Lan for unpaid parking tickets on a car registered in her name, which he obtained and used without a valid driver's license. She is also counterclaiming for the insurance cost, the car's value, and towing fees due to the car being towed and impounded.

The Defendant's statements suggest that she considered Clarence as a close friend or family member, which led her to agree to help him register and insure the car despite the risks involved. However, she did warn him about the potential consequences, including tickets being issued in her name if he failed to pay them.

The Defendant's goal or motive behind her words appears to be a mix of loyalty towards Clarence and a desire to help a friend in need.

Judge: I understand, but by operation of the law, don't we have basically the same thing here? Because the second contract is not completely silent.
The second contract says forum selection, go to—you know, disputes go to the court.
And I thought that by operation of law, when you don't speak to delegation in a situation like that, the choice is—the delegation is to the court.
So that, in effect, even though we don't have those words in the second contract, we basically achieve the same thing.
Am I wrong about that?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: I think you are—
Judge: Okay.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: —just a little bit off about that.
Judge: Okay.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: And the nuance here, I think, is the exact nuance that this Court identified really going all the way back to Prima Paint but through Rent-A-Center itself, where it talked about the fact you can have validity challenges that occur at these different levels.
So you can argue that there's some sort of invalidity or some sort of supersession that occurs at the level of the arbitration agreement.
You can also have one that occurs at the level of the delegation clause.
And under the severability principle that this Court has consistently applied and that it draws directly from the text of Section 2 of the FAA, the only question here is whether the Respondents' argument addresses the who decides question.
Judge: Can I—keep going.
Judge: No, I'm—it's all right. I'm done.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So the only—only other point I would make that I think is important to understand about this is that a delegation clause by its very nature must reach more broadly than an arbitration agreement, and that's because, when you get to the delegation and you end up in front of the arbitrator, sometimes the answer will be yes, this should be arbitrated; sometimes it will be no, it shouldn't be.
But the force of the delegation clause does not turn on the reach of the arbitration agreement.
Judge: So, from the reply brief, page 9, I located a point of possible agreement between you and Respondent on a remand possibility.
So you say on page 9 in the reply brief,
"Because the Ninth Circuit did not decide whether the sweepstakes official rules displaced the delegation clause, this Court could vacate the judgment below and instruct the Ninth Circuit to apply the FAA's severability rules. See response 58," the response to the red brief.
The red brief said the Court should remand this case to the Ninth Circuit to more thoroughly consider whether the official rules partially modify the user agreement's delegation clauses they say under non-preempted state laws of contract interpretation.
Are you all saying the same thing there? And if you both agree that the Ninth Circuit's analysis is wrong, it should be remanded, are we done?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So I think, Your Honor, that we do both agree that the Ninth Circuit's analysis was wrong.
I think we do both agree that this should be remanded.
I think we have a disagreement when you get beyond that.
Judge: Understood. Can we just remand and say that's for the Ninth Circuit to figure out in the first instance?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: You can. I think it would be important—
Judge: And if we do that, are we done?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So, Your Honor, I think you—I think you could stop there.
Judge: Okay.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: I think there are some reasons not to stop there and to add a little bit of an explanation.
Judge: But we could. I just want to get the "could" and the Respondent.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yes. Yes, Your Honor. Yes.
Judge: And then tell me why we shouldn't, but I just wanted to get the "could."
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Well, I—I think you could stop there, but in saying that you're remanding the case, I think there are three important principles that the Ninth Circuit went astray on that I would recommend that this Court provide as guidance because this is a situation—successive contracts come up not infrequently in all sorts of commercial settings, consumer settings.
The first is that the severability principle applies just as much in a successive contract scenario as it does to a single contract scenario.
That means that the court can only entertain challenges directly to the validity or enforceability of the delegation clause itself.
The second principle that I think would be important to explain is that delegation clauses can and regularly do direct an arbitrator to resolve disputes about whether an arbitration agreement exists and whether it covers a particular dispute.
The third point I think that this Court should make in remanding it is that a court cannot refuse to enforce a delegation clause based on the court's view that a later contract changes the scope of what disputes are arbitrable because, as I said in—in response to your earlier question, the force of a delegation clause does not rise or fall on the scope of—
Judge: These are huge changes.
Judge: Well, you—you do—
Judge: I'm sorry. These are huge changes.
You are now creating a whole set of federal rules on what constitutes a superseding agreement or not.
I don't—we didn't grant cert on that.
We granted cert on a very narrow question, and the narrow question was where parties enter into an arbitration agreement with a delegation clause, should an arbitrator or a court decide whether that agreement is narrowed by a later contract that is silent as to arbitration and delegation.
I don't know whether the silence or not is relevant.
The question is what does state law do.
We didn't ask for briefing on that.
The short answer is the court decides what state law says or doesn't say.
Here, the Ninth Circuit decided that three years into this litigation, two appeals to the Supreme Court that have been accepted.
Why shouldn't we just answer that question? The Court decides it.
State law may guide the Court in whether there's been a superseding agreement, but we shouldn't be creating federal rules for the state to follow or not follow in interpreting contracts.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Your Honor, we are not asking for you to create any federal rules in resolving this case.
The severability rule is something this Court has applied consistently.
It has drawn directly from the text of Sections 2, 3, and 4.
And all we're asking is that in remanding this, you make clear that the severability rule applies here, just as it always does.
Judge: Well—
Judge: I'm sorry.
Judge: I would suppose that your answer could be that it—it depends.
I mean, simply because there's a subsequent contract that is silent doesn't mean that the delegation clause covers all of that, right?
You could have a big enterprise, and you have a delegation clause in a particular arbitration agreement, and there's a contract entered between the same parties somewhere down the line concerning a totally different issue.
And do you think in that situation it still goes to the arbitrator—
Judge: —to decide whether he or she's been delegated the authority to arbitrate that agreement?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Your Honor, I think there is a practical answer to that and there is a doctrinal answer to that.
The practical answer is that a party only invokes a delegation clause when it thinks it has a real claim that the dispute is actually subject to arbitration.
So—for example, in this case, the Respondents have agreed that absent the rules changing it, their claims are subject to arbitration.
So that's the—that's the practical answer.

The Plaintiff believes that this principle applies just as much in a successive contract scenario as it does to a single contract scenario.

The Plaintiff's goal or motive behind their words is to clarify the role of a delegation clause in resolving disputes, particularly in successive contracts. They argue that a delegation clause can direct an arbitrator to resolve disputes about whether an arbitration agreement exists and whether it covers a particular dispute.

DEFENDANT: The Defendant's lawyer argues that the court should remand the case to the Ninth Circuit to reconsider the validity of the delegation clause in light of the successive contracts. The Defendant's goal is to establish that the severability principle applies to successive contracts, just as it does to single contracts. The Defendant also aims to clarify that delegation clauses can direct an arbitrator to resolve disputes about the existence and scope of arbitration agreements.

Judge: I -- I don't think it operates on --on the body in the same way. Take testosterone.
If you give a boy with a deficiency testosterone because he has constitutional delay of puberty, that allows him to go through the -- the --and develop the reproductive organs associated with being a male.
If you give it to a girl, it renders the girl infertile.
So we have 8-to 12-year-olds being asked --
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Oh, I'm sorry.
I thought your reasons for them being different was that you said they were for different purposes.
I had heard --
Judge: Well --
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: -- you say at the beginning the reason those two are different is because one wants them to transition and the other wants them for some medical purpose other than that.
Judge: Well, to go back to my -- my example in the -- in the introduction, I don't think anyone would say using morphine to assist suicide is the same treatment as using morphine to manage pain.
It's the same drug, just like it's the same drug here.
But they're being used for fundamentally different purposes. They have different effects on the body.
And once you take out and you recognize medical reality, then there is no argument that our law differentiates between treatments for males and females.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Can I ask you about one of the purposes of this law? And I note that your brief does not talk a lot about this, but one of the articulated purposes of this law is essentially to engender -- encourage gender conformity and to discourage anything other than gender conformity.
And I'm wondering how you think that plays into the analysis.
Judge: We --I disagree with that characterization of our law.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, "encourage minors to appreciate their sex and ban treatments that might encourage minors to become disdainful of their sex" sounds to me like we want boys to be boys and we want girls to be girls.
Judge: If I could --
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: And that's an important purpose behind the law.
And I understand that sentiment, but it's -- it's a -- it's a fundamentally different sentiment and it's a fundamentally different understanding of what produced this law than the one that you are talking about now.
Judge: Your Honor, if I could make a few points.
First of all, it sounds like the question is rooted in a potential improper purpose-based argument under an Arlington Heights argument, which, as Chief Judge Sutton pointed out below, this -- that argument was never raised until it got to this Court.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, I -- I -- I'm less interested in sort of like the legal box to put this in and more interested in, you know, you're --
Judge: Sure.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: -- you're -- you're -- you're spending a lot of time talking about what exactly the classification is here.
And I think we've talked a good deal about that.
But what produced this classification might be relevant to understanding what the classification is about.
Judge: Absolutely.
And I would love to address --
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: And -- and what seems to have produced this classification is that we want to ban children, treatments that might encourage minors to become disdainful of their sex. So we think that there's something fundamentally wrong, fundamentally bad, about youth who are -- are trying to transition.
And that's the way this purpose seems to me.
Judge: If I could try to unpack both of those, Your Honor, because I think both of those, read in context, do not support the narrative that Tennessee wants boys to live as boys and girls to live as girls.
So the "appreciate their sex" reference in --detailed in legislative findings, that is simply the recognition that given the high desistance rate among minors and given the tragic regret of detransitioners, that there is an interest in making sure that minors have enough time to appreciate their sex before undergoing life-altering changes. So I think that that has to be viewed in the context of the legislative findings, with --which both emphasize the detransitioners and the high rate of desistance.
With respect to "become disdainful of their sex," the -- the challengers have never explained why it would be problematic to prevent interventions that could affirmatively cause minors to become disdainful of their sex and thus at issue for psychiatric conditions.
And, in fact, there are multiple studies, I would point to this Court JA 400, where minors -- actually, their mental health and suicidality got worse after taking these interventions.
Now my friends on the other side may disagree with that research and that assessment of whether -- the findings of that study, but the legislature specifically noted those studies.
So I think that statement was rooted in the notion that, actually, this is causing affirmative harm to minors who are undergoing the interventions, and that's why they were saying we don't want these interventions that will cause minors to become disdainful of their sex.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: At a --
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Go ahead.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: No, go ahead.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: No, go ahead.
I'll be back.
Judge: Justice Kavanaugh?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: At a -- at a --at a big-picture level, I think the argument on the other side, putting aside some of the details, is why not trust parents rather than the state, particularly in a situation, as General Prelogar said, where there's not the kind of direct harm to third parties that you might see in other contexts like sports.
Judge: Yes, Your Honor.
So, as my friends recognize, the parental rights question is not before this Court.
And we can --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: But she explained how it informs, so just take the question --
Judge: Sure.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: -- as best you can.

Dialogue:
Judge: All parties, please raise your right hand. Welcome back to the People’s Court. Next case in the docket: the plaintiff sold a car to the defendant, but the check bounced all over town. The defendant says the car clunked out the day after he bought it. It’s the case of “I got junk in the trunk and the engine.”
Judge: Okay, Carlos Higera, you are suing Eddie Rossi for $1,100, the balance owed to you, you say, on a car that you sold to him, and he bounced a check. Tell me what’s going on.
Plaintiff: On February 2, my employee sold the car, a ‘97 Nissan Quest, for $1,800. He left a deposit of $300 that day.
Judge: Okay. Was he buying the car from you, or do you own a dealership?
Plaintiff: I own the dealership, but the car was not from the dealership. The car was my personal car.
Judge: Personal car. Okay, go ahead.
Plaintiff: Then the gentleman said he liked the car to my salesman. He took the deposit for $300 that day. The next day, he came to pick it up, and he owed $1,500. I asked, “Where’s the balance?” He said, “Oh no, the bank was closed. I’ll give you a check for $1,100, but tomorrow morning, I’ll pay you and bring a coffee for you and your employee.” Tomorrow morning, he gets the car.
Judge: Tomorrow morning, and you still waited three weeks?
Plaintiff: I gave him the car.
Judge: You gave him the car?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Did you give him the title?
Plaintiff: Yes, because of how he was talking. He convinced me.
Judge: Wait, wait, wait, wait, wait. Rewind. I want to see if I got this right. You are a used car salesman?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: For how long?
Plaintiff: 22 years.
Judge: And you sold a car to a guy, gave him the title and everything else because he talked a good game, and now you’re out $1,100?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: You got nailed on a used car transaction?
Plaintiff: He gave me the check for $1,100.
Judge: And what happened with that check?
Plaintiff: It bounced.
Judge: Right, so it’s no good. You needed money, not a bad check, right? What’s going on?
Defendant: Just hearing the plaintiff talk about the check… When I left the place, he gave me all the paperwork.
Judge: Did you give him an $1,100 check?
Defendant: Yes, I did.
Judge: You were surprised that he gave you the title and everything, weren’t you?
Defendant: Well, I was a little surprised. And when I was leaving, he gave me all the papers for the car. I even said, “Don’t you want all your money before you give me the papers?”
Judge: Exactly.
Defendant: I told him, “I think we’re missing the payment.” Then he realized, “Oh yeah, I forgot to get my money.” So I offered him a check to hold while I went to get the money and come back to pay him in cash.
Judge: Why didn’t you go get the money and come back?
Defendant: Because when I went back out with the car, that’s when all the problems started.
Judge: The second you drove off, the car started having problems?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: But you didn’t call him and say, “Give me my money back. I want to return the car”?
Defendant: Oh no, I did call them several times. Not the same day, but within two or three days.
Judge: But you were supposed to pay him in cash the next day. If the problems didn’t start for two or three days, why wasn’t he already paid?
Defendant: Because the car started having problems.
Judge: Why? Because you got the title and the car?
Defendant: No, because the car had issues. I asked him to come to my place of business multiple times, and I mentioned there was an issue with the engine. He told me it wasn’t covered under the warranty.
Judge: When did he come to your business?
Defendant: About three days after the sale.
Judge: So he came looking for his money, and you said, “Oh, the car has issues,” and then you refused to pay. Would you ever give someone the title to a car without getting full payment?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: You’re sure?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: The title makes the car yours, right? Would you ever in a million years do that?
Plaintiff: Definitely not. I would want my money before giving the title.
Judge: And why is that?
Plaintiff: Because I wouldn’t want to give something away without getting anything in return. You lose your leverage.
Judge: Exactly. Going inside the courtroom, you spoke to Mr. Rossi many times?
Plaintiff: Yes. I even went to his work and spoke to his supervisor. The supervisor told me, “Carlos, don’t worry. Take it to court.”
Judge: Unbelievable. And he still has his job?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Do you have a warranty on this 1997 Nissan Quest?
Defendant: Yes, there was a limited warranty on the slip that came with the car.
Judge: Let me see. It’s not even filled out. Was this blank, and then he filled it out?
Defendant: No, that’s how it was when I got it.
Judge: This doesn’t do you any good. And you don’t even pay for a car, but you want a warranty? I’m done. He owes you the money. I rule in favor of the plaintiff for $1,100. Pay the man.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff, Carlos Higera, is a used car salesman with 22 years of experience. He sold his personal 1997 Nissan Quest to the Defendant, Eddie Rossi, for $1,800, with a $300 deposit. Rossi promised to pay the remaining $1,500 the next day, but the check bounced.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant's statements suggest that he purchased a 1997 Nissan Quest from the Plaintiff, who is a used car salesman, for $1,800. The Defendant initially left a deposit of $300 and agreed to pay the remaining $1,500 the next day. However, the Defendant did not have the full payment the next day and instead gave the Plaintiff a check for $1,100, which bounced.

Dialogue:
Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to the People's Court. Next case on the docket: The Defendant borrowed money from the Plaintiff. The dispute is over whether the Plaintiff has to just keep the rock climbing equipment he got as collateral or whether he can sue. It's the case of the antisocial climber.
Judge: Mark O'Brien, you are suing Michael O'Callahan for $7,000. That exceeds—actually, you say he owes you more than that, but that’s your statutory max in Massachusetts: a $2,000 loan that you made to him that you say he hasn’t repaid, and $200 of an advance that you gave to him for work to be done. But you’ve decided to triple that to $2,500 and make it into a $7,000 claim.
Plaintiff: That’s correct.
Judge: Okay. I’m dying to hear your authority for that. Talk to me—tell me what happens between the two of you.
Plaintiff: Well, I had known him previously as a result of his visitations to my cousin's garage. I got to talk to him, know him a little bit, knew what his background was—a tower climber.
Judge: What’s a tower climber?
Plaintiff: Builds cell phone towers, telecommunications.
Judge: Okay. Go on.
Plaintiff: My cousin had a wind tower with a turbine on it back in the '70s, and there were problems with the turbine. Then the company went out of business, so he just left the tower up. The turbine was gone, and he said he’d give me the tower. I talked to Mike, and he said he’d take it down for me for a certain charge, and we agreed to do that.
Judge: Wait, he would take the tower down, and you would put it where?
Plaintiff: In Maine, on my property in Maine.
Judge: Okay, so go on.
Plaintiff: That worked out well. I paid him, he took the tower down, did a good job. Then he still wasn’t working due to an injury he said he had. So, I said to him, "On the property where I’m caretaking in Massachusetts, if you need more work, I need help cutting and splitting wood." We agreed to that, and I paid him for what he did.
Plaintiff: Then he asked me for a loan because he had gained my trust and confidence. So, I said, "Okay, I’ve got the money right now, and I can loan you the cash." We wrote it down, and he gave me collateral to cover the value.
Judge: Can I see what you wrote down?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: I'm sorry—do you mean you took notes, or do you have a contract?
Plaintiff: We have a contract.
Judge: Okay. So, you loaned to him on February 16th of this year, and he was supposed to pay back on March 3rd?
Plaintiff: He said he could.
Judge: Who were the witnesses?
Plaintiff: Michelle Sullivan and Jake Raymond.
Judge: Did you sign this, Mr. O'Callahan?
Defendant: Yes, I did.
Judge: So you don’t deny that you borrowed $2,000?
Defendant: No.
Judge: Do you say to him, "Hey, it’s March 2nd, you haven’t paid me, what’s going on?" What happens there?
Plaintiff: There was some communication and attempts to get repaid.
Judge: Tell me about the $200 advance for work to be done.
Plaintiff: After he took down the tower, I gave him $200 to put the tower back up in Maine.
Judge: And that didn’t happen—why?
Defendant: I just didn’t have the time to do it.
Judge: And now you don’t want to work with him anymore because you don’t trust him?
Plaintiff: That’s correct.
Judge: Tell me your side, Mr. O'Callahan.
Defendant: I don’t deny I borrowed the money. I was supposed to go back to work but didn’t because I had to go back in for another back surgery. I gave him my climbing equipment as collateral. It’s worth almost seven grand brand new.
Judge: You have the climbing equipment, Mr. O'Brien?
Plaintiff: Yes, I do.
Defendant: He was at my house, and I told him to sell it, and it would take care of the loan.
Judge: Why does he have to sell it?
Defendant: That was the agreement we made.
Judge: Show me where it says that he has to sell it instead of you selling it yourself.
Defendant: That’s just what we agreed to.
Judge: Why does he have to go through all the effort?
Defendant: I don’t know. Maybe there’s no buyer.
Judge: Tell me about the work done cutting wood. You claim he deducted labor from the balance?
Defendant: Yes. I worked, and he deducted $400 from what I owed.
Judge: Did that happen, Mr. O'Brien?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: Do you have it in writing, Mr. O'Callahan?
Defendant: No.
Judge: Mr. Raymond, you were there. Talk to me.
Witness: Yes, Mark wrote it down each day. He paid me cash. He deducted from Mike’s balance.
