Dialogue:
Judge: Yeah.
Lawyer of Defendant: -- that the law is burdening our speech.
Then you have to look at whether the law itself is somehow content-based, not just what their motivations are but whether the law is content-based.
And, here, the trigger for this law, the one thing that gets it going, is if you operate a social media platform that has user-generated content, unless that content takes the form of a product, travel, or business review.
Then, within that universe of content, it says there's one speaker we're particularly concerned about, and we're going to hammer home on that one speaker.
And then, just to make the rubble bounce, they come in and tell us that one of the reasons they're targeting that speaker is because they're worried about the future content on that platform, that it could in the future somehow be critical of the United States or undermine democracy, to pull examples from the government's brief.
So I think there's no way to get around the fact that this is a content-based speech restriction and you do have to go directly to what their interests are.
Now their principal interest is --
Judge: Could -- could I -- because I think I'm a little bit surprised by one of the answers that you gave to the other judge.
I had understood that the company's essential complaint here is that they wouldn't be able to use the algorithm that their parent company has invented and that they want to use the algorithm that their parent company has invented.
Lawyer of Defendant: One hundred percent. And if I -- if I was unclear on that, Your Honor, I apologize.
Judge: Okay.
Because I think --
Lawyer of Defendant: That is absolutely the core of the claim.
Judge: -- what the other judge was saying to you is, like, what's the problem here because the parent company is a foreign company.
Or maybe this isn't what the other judge says; it's just what I say.

Judge: The parent company is a foreign company.
And you started off with another judge saying, you know, well, we would be making a different argument.
And, of course, that's true.
I mean, I would think that precedent makes it pretty clear that you have to be making a different argument with respect to a foreign state or a foreign company.
So let's -- let's say that they don't have First Amendment rights.
The only First Amendment rights lie in the company, which does have First Amendment rights.
And I -- I guess my question is, how are those First Amendment rights really being implicated here? This -- this statute says the foreign company has to divest.
Whether or not that's feasible, however long it takes, the company still has the ability to use whatever algorithm it wants, doesn't it?
Lawyer of Defendant: No, Your Honor.
And their rights are implicated at a most basic level.
In 10 days, the company wants to speak.
In 10 days, because this law was passed, the company cannot speak unless the parent company executes a qualified divestiture.
That's not just the parent company's choice. That is a -- that is a condition --
Judge: Well, I realize --
Lawyer of Defendant: -- that's imposed by law.
Judge: -- that it has -- it definitely has effects on the company if the parent company acts in the way that you're assuming it will act.
So -- so this is not to say that the First Amendment isn't involved because the company is going to suffer some pretty severe incidental effects, but they are incidental, aren't they? Because the statute only says to this foreign company divest or else and -- and leaves the company with the ability --
Lawyer of Defendant: Right.
Judge: -- to do what every other actor in the United States can do, which is go find the best available algorithm.
Lawyer of Defendant: Yeah.
I very much disagree that the effects are incidental because the way that this law works is it is only triggered if somebody is engaging in speech based on their content, user-generated content, except for business, product, and travel reviews.
It then singles out a single speaker.
And you have the concession for the government that one of the reasons they've singled out that speaker --
Judge: That puts a lot of emphasis on the idea of just like -- you know, I think what you're basically saying is that all speaker-based restrictions generate strict scrutiny.
I'm not sure that we've ever said anything like that.
You know, let's put aside the facial -- your argument that this is facially content-based.
It seems to me that your stronger argument or at least the one that most interested me was this argument of, look, if the government is doing something specifically for the purpose of changing the content that people see, that has to be subject to strict scrutiny. But I don't see that as -- as affecting the company as opposed to as affecting the parent company, that --
Lawyer of Defendant: Well, no, no, I -- I very much do see it as affecting the company because they choose this algorithm because it reflects the mix of content.
The government's fear is that another country could come in and pressure the company, the company, through the parent company, to alter that mix of content to make it too pro-foreign or too anti-American.
That is very much directly a content-based charge straight at the company.
The other point I would like to --
Judge: I -- I hear you that it might very well have that effect.
I guess what I'm suggesting is that the law is only targeted at this foreign corporation, which doesn't have First Amendment rights.
Whatever effect it has, it has.
You know, maybe the parent company will figure out a way to, like, put this on open source, and then the company will be able to use the algorithm.
Lawyer of Defendant: So, Your Honor, if I could take that on directly, because, to the -- I think the company has First Amendment rights.
To the extent the parent company is speaking in the United States, it, I believe, has First Amendment rights.
If you conclude that neither has First Amendment rights, then, surely, the creators have First Amendment rights.
But, if you take a step back, what their position is is that none of these entities -- this is the universe of entities affected by this law -- none of these entities have the authority to assert First Amendment rights, which means that the government really could come in and say: I'm going to shut down this company because it's too pro-Republican or too pro-Democrat or won't disseminate the speech I want, and that would get no First Amendment scrutiny by anybody. That cannot possibly be the case, yet that is the effect of their position.
Judge: Thank you, counsel.
Lawyer of Defendant: We think that's the minimum that should be done here.
Judge: Thank you, counsel.
We -- we've been talking about connection between the regulation of -- of the company and the burden on expressive conduct. And your basic position is that interfering with the ownership of the company constitutes a direct regulation of the expressive conduct of other -- other people. What -- what is your best example in our precedent of a situation where we've -- a regulation of corporate structure or something else has been treated as a direct regulation of expressive conduct?
Lawyer of Defendant: The regulation of a corporate structure as a --
Judge: Yeah.
Lawyer of Defendant: Your Honor, I -- I -- I don't have a case in my fingertips.
I can consider it when we come back on --
Judge: Well, I don't have one at my fingertips or any other part of my body.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff's statements suggest that they are a company that uses an algorithm developed by their parent company to manage user-generated content on their social media platform. The Plaintiff argues that a law requiring the parent company to divest from the Plaintiff would infringe on the Plaintiff's First Amendment rights, as the algorithm is essential to their speech. The Plaintiff contends that the law is content-based because it targets user-generated content and specifically targets one speaker, and the government's concern is that the parent company could be pressured by a foreign government to alter the content mix on the platform.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant's statements suggest that they are challenging a law that restricts the use of a specific algorithm by a social media platform, which is owned by a foreign parent company. The Defendant argues that this law is a content-based speech restriction because it targets a single speaker (the social media platform) and is concerned about future content on the platform that could be critical of the United States or undermine democracy. The Defendant's goal is to protect the First Amendment rights of the social media company, which they believe are being infringed upon by the law's requirement for the parent company to divest.

Dialogue:
Judge: All parties please enter. Welcome back to The People's Court. Next case on the docket: The plaintiff says his lifelong friend stiffed him on a trip to Miami, backed out, and won’t pay the cancellation fee. But the defendant says the plaintiff jumped the gun before the defendant committed. It’s the case of "Life of Miami Beach." Thank you, Douglas.
Judge: Patrick Hemingway, you're suing Jamal Cooper, your former friend, for $900 in cancellation fees that you incurred as a result of him bailing on you, according to you. Tell me what’s going on.
Plaintiff: All right, so we all planned—me, Jamal, and another friend—to go to Miami for spring break weekend. We all were in a group chat, and I told them that we had a good deal on a hotel and flight, so I had to pay it by a certain day. Both parties knew. Me and the other friend had got the money together, and we were waiting for Jamal to send the money, but we had to book by that day, so I went ahead and booked the trip.
Judge: But did you have his authority to do that?
Plaintiff: Yes, I spoke to him on the phone, and he's also in the group chat. After the trip was booked, I sent the itinerary. After that, he said, "Could I just give you cash?" So he knew it was completely booked, and he was going to give me cash. I told him yeah.
Judge: And then what happened?
Plaintiff: A couple of days went past. He was supposed to give me the money. We spoke on the phone again, and he told me like, he’s not going. He didn’t know I already booked the trip. Then I responded to that message, telling him everything that he said, and he didn’t respond to my texts or calls.
Judge: You’ve never talked to him since then?
Plaintiff: I haven’t talked to him since then.
Judge: Okay, so you and your other friend end up going anyway?
Plaintiff: Yeah, we had to cancel and rebook.
Judge: Right, but you’re going to have to prove to me you paid $900 in cancellation fees.
Plaintiff: I can prove it.
Judge: Okay. All right. So this is from United Vacations, what they sent over saying about the refund. It was $300 per person. United Vacations canceled. How did you get this?
Plaintiff: Oh, they emailed it to me.
Judge: Can I see the email that they sent you that shows that it came from them?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: And while he's looking for that, what’s your story?
Defendant: He knew—everybody knows that I’m a supervisor at my job. I’m salaried, it’s hard to get days off. Him and my other friend both knew that. The other friend knew that, that’s why he ain’t suing me. He knows.
Judge: What did you tell him? Did you tell him, "Go ahead and make the reservation?"
Defendant: No, I did not. He’s saying that. He is telling me he got a nice deal, whatever, whatever. He never told me to—I never said, "Pay for the trip."
Judge: Did he tell you, "Look, today’s the deadline, we’ve got to pay"? And did you say, "Yeah, okay, do it?"
Defendant: No.
Judge: So now you’ve been able to prove to me that you’re out the money. So the next question is: Show me that it’s his fault that you’re out the money. In other words, show me the conversations that you have with him where he says, "Yeah, go for it. I’m in."
Plaintiff: All right. I can go to the texts.
Judge: You need your phone, right?
Plaintiff: Yeah, and set it so it won’t be scrambled.
Judge: Because it’s simple—you either agreed to do this and then left him hanging, or you didn’t agree to do this.
Defendant: Right. I said I wanted to go. I never said, "Go ahead, pay for the trip."
Judge: Let’s see. Let’s see how the language starts on January 22nd. Okay, hand it over.
Judge: I spend my life scrolling. I have arthritis in my right thumb.
Judge: What is the day that you had to pay the money?
Plaintiff: I believe the 22nd of January.
Judge: There’s not a single text from him agreeing to pay? You’re texting him, but he’s not answering?
Plaintiff: We spoke on the phone. I have our call log too.
Judge: All right. But you don’t have texts proving that he authorized you to spend. But after it was booked, he—you can see where he agreed to send the money over several times.
Judge: So then you don’t have a text proving he authorized it beforehand. But he does say, "Can’t I just give you the cash?" Why would he say that if he didn’t think he owed you the cash?
Defendant: That was before my job said I couldn’t go.
Judge: Oh, well, then that’s on you, isn’t it? Then maybe what you should have been saying to him was, "Absolutely not, don’t include me because I have to first check with my job."
Judge: Verdict for the plaintiff. $900.
Defendant: Nobody told him to pay for that.
Judge: Your mothers must be so upset at you. What do they say about you two being here?
Defendant: I ain’t telling my mother.
Judge: Oh, well, I got some bad news for you. Your mother’s a huge fan, and she’s going to see you. Surprise!
Judge: Case dismissed. Pay the man.
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff, Patrick Hemingway, is suing his former friend Jamal Cooper for $900 in cancellation fees due to Jamal backing out of a planned trip to Miami. The Plaintiff claims that he had the authority to book the trip on behalf of the group, as they were all in a group chat and Jamal was aware of the booking. However, the Defendant, Jamal Cooper, denies authorizing Patrick to book the trip and claims that he was unable to attend due to work commitments.

The Plaintiff's goal or motive behind his words appears to be to prove that Jamal agreed to the trip and was responsible for the cancellation fees incurred when the trip was cancelled and rebooked.
DEFENDANT:

The Plaintiff booked the trip before the Defendant could pay, citing a phone conversation and the Defendant's presence in a group chat as authorization. However, the Defendant claims he never explicitly agreed to pay for the trip and that his job made it difficult to get days off.

Dialogue:
Judge: Justice Barrett? Justice Jackson?
Judge: But do you concede that you would have a problem or not depending upon how the statute is worded? I mean, we're in a statutory scheme here. We're not just doing aiding-and-abetting liability as a matter of common law. Don't you agree?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: I do agree with that, Justice Jackson, but I think the fact that we're in a statutory scheme is an important element of centering this case where it is now. That's why I said earlier we are at the beginning of the beginning of this case.
Judge: No, I understand.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: There's a statute --
Judge: I guess I just -- just quickly in response to what Justice Kavanaugh was just exploring with you, the facts that he laid out seem to me to be covered by this particular PLCAA immunity, that Congress looked at that situation and said, you know, the term "qualified civil liability action," which you can't bring in court, means a civil action resulting from the criminal or unlawful misuse of a qualified product by the person of a third party. So, in other words, Congress started with Justice Kavanaugh's example, you know, my product is being used by third parties in criminal ways. And they said immunity. They said immunity. And so --
Lawyer of Plaintiff: They said --
Judge: -- and so to read the exception to that as essentially capturing the same facts, if you know that your product is going to these people, seems odd to me.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Justice Jackson, what Congress said was immunity unless. And the predicate exception that we've been talking about says immunity unless the manufacturer is alleged, among other things, to have aided and abetted violations of federal gun laws.
Judge: Thank you.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: It's that -- that's the important point.
Judge: Thank you, counsel.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Thank you.
Judge: Rebuttal, Mr. Francisco?
Lawyer of Defendant: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice. Just a few points.
First, Justice Thomas, as to one of your questions, if they're right under PLCAA, this would, in fact, revive the exact same lawsuits that PLCAA was meant to prohibit. If you look at Texas's -- Mexico's complaint, the underlying torts alleged, which is what they're seeking relief on, are the exact same torts that were at issue in all of the other cases. The violations only come in in their effort to get around PLCAA and fit with one of its exceptions.
But, if you accept their interpretation of the PLCAA exception, you will have revived exactly the same type of lawsuit that PLCAA was meant to prohibit when they adopted this statute in the first place.
Secondly, my friend talked about some paragraphs in their complaint, but I think that their complaint makes quite clear what their basic aiding-and-abetting theory is. Paragraph 228 -- and this is a allegation that they repeat throughout -- "each defendant's policy is to sell its guns to any and all federal firearms licensees." That is anyone that the federal government says that we can sell to. So their allegation is that we're treating all retailers exactly the same.
We're not treating any one better than any other. We're treating them the same.
That's important because, in Twitter, the Court made quite clear that the reason they didn't get across the aiding-and-abetting line was because there was no allegation that they were treating ISIS, who they knew to a metaphysical certainty was on their platform, they weren't treating ISIS any better or worse than any other customer.
Instead, they go on -- and this is paragraph 110 -- to explain their theory. "A manufacturer of a dangerous product is an accessory or co-conspirator to illicit conduct by downstream actors where it continues to supply, support, or assist the downstream parties and has knowledge, actual or constructive, of the illicit conduct." I think that's the mere knowledge theory that my friend was just referring to, Justice Kavanaugh.
That, however, is the theory that the Court squarely rejected in the Twitter case.
Turning to foreseeability, Mr. Chief Justice, there was an exchange with my friend on the case law, where I think what I heard her say was that an intervening independent act didn't break the chain if it was foreseeable.
Well, I would say that that is completely inconsistent with this Court's repeated statement that foreseeability alone isn't the standard.
It's also completely independent with the Hemi Group case that did not involve a derivative injury. New York City was the only plaintiff that could sue for the lost taxes that New York City suffered. There was absolutely no other plaintiff that would have been able to pursue that remedy and that loss.
Yet this Court held that the intervening independent act did break the chain.
Lexmark is no different. It did not involve an intervening independent act. If I trick consumers into not buying my competitors' products, I've directly harmed my competitor. There is no intervening independent act.
As to the various treaties, I would agree, Justice Sotomayor, with your suggestion that the law in the verbal formulations here are kind of a mess. But, if you actually look at the cases that the treatises cite for their assertion that foreseeability alone is the standard, they don't cite this Court's cases, which is kind of surprising, if what they're supposed to be doing is describing the law rather than making the law.
But the cases that they do cite principally involve special relationships, like a landlord/tenant relationship or a teacher/student relationship. They don't cite any case in which a manufacturer has been held liable because some criminal unlawfully misuses its product to harm somebody else.
My friend, again, emphasized the Washington Post article. I would urge you to read that article from beginning to end. I think it's very helpful in illuminating how even their theory of mere knowledge doesn't make sense. My friend also talked about three pistols sold by Colt with Spanish-named firearms.
The notion that selling a Spanish-named firearm is what gives rise to joint purpose with cartels under the aiding-and-abetting statute is as wrong as it is offensive. There are, after all, millions of perfectly law-abiding Spanish-speaking Americans in this country that find those firearms very attractive.
And making those firearms available cannot possibly cross the line into aiding-and-abetting liability. But, even if it could, the notion that selling three Spanish-named pistols is the proximate cause of cartel violence in Mexico is, frankly, absurd, and I don't think it comes even close to establishing Twombly's plausibility standard.
My final point is just to step back and talk about what PLCAA was really about. At the end of the day, PLCAA is about protecting Second Amendment rights. It's not just about protecting the manufacturers, the distributors, and the retailers, but it's protecting the right of every American to exercise their right under the Second Amendment to possess and bear firearms.
That right is meaningless if there are no manufacturers, retailers, and distributors that provide them in the first place.
We ask that you reverse.
Judge: Thank you, counsel.
The case is submitted.
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff's lawyer's statements suggest that they are arguing for the interpretation of a statute (PLCAA) that provides immunity to manufacturers, distributors, and retailers from civil liability for harm caused by their products being used in criminal or unlawful ways. The Plaintiff's goal is to argue that the immunity provided by the statute does not apply in cases where the manufacturer is alleged to have aided and abetted violations of federal gun laws.

The Plaintiff's lawyer argues that the statute states immunity unless the manufacturer is alleged to have aided and abetted violations of federal gun laws. They also argue that the Plaintiff's case falls under this exception because the manufacturer is accused of treating all retailers the same, which could be seen as aiding and abetting illicit conduct by downstream actors.

The Plaintiff's lawyer also emphasizes that the case is about protecting Second Amendment rights and that the immunity provided by the statute is essential for the exercise of these rights.

The Defendant's statements suggest that they are arguing against the Plaintiff's interpretation of the Protection of Lawful Commerce in Arms Act (PLCAA). The Defendant contends that the Plaintiff's interpretation would revive lawsuits that PLCAA was intended to prohibit, as it would hold the Defendant liable for aiding and abetting third-party criminal misuse of their products.

The Defendant's goal or motive behind their words appears to be to establish that their actions do not meet the criteria for aiding and abetting liability, as defined by the PLCAA, and to argue that the Plaintiff's interpretation of the statute is inconsistent with previous court rulings.

Summarizing the Defendant's intent in three sentences:
1. The Defendant argues that their actions do not meet the criteria for aiding and abetting liability as defined by the Protection of Lawful Commerce in Arms Act (PLCAA).

Judge: Harry Monroe and Sher Johnson Monroe?
Plaintiff: s: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: You’re suing your former landlord, William Sepy, for $3,600, double the security deposit, because he won’t return it to you. And you have a counterclaim against them for about $3,400 for rent and damages, including some excessive electricity bills. Let me hear from you first. How long were you folks living there?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am. We moved into the apartment on January 7, 2017. We lived there until Mr. Sepy gave us a notice to vacate on the 28th of January—January of 2018.
Judge: All right. So you lived there for a year. Did you have any problems during that year?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am. I complained to him about the washer not working. Mr. Sepy called me and my wife down to his apartment at 11:30 at night.
Judge: Did he live in the same building?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am. He stayed on the first floor, and we stayed on the third floor. We went down there, and he accused us—he just jumped up and accused us—of tearing up his machine. And when I was trying to talk to him, Mr. Sepy, for his size, he’s pretty quick. I was sitting down, and he jumped up and got right over my face, telling me to shut up, shut up, hollering at me, spitting on me. It scared the heck out of my wife, and to be totally honest, it scared me.
Judge: I'm just saying—I would not have gone to his apartment at 11:30 at night.
Plaintiff: At first, I thought he was—you know, he seemed to be a fairly nice person when we first met him, when we first talked.
Judge: Right. When he gave you a notice of eviction, did he tell you why?
Plaintiff: No, ma’am, he did not.
Judge: And you didn’t care because you wanted to leave anyway.
Plaintiff: I wanted to leave anyway because of the issues we were having.
Judge: So what other problems were you having that made you not sad that you got a notice of eviction in January?
Plaintiff: The way he hollers and yells at me—
Plaintiff: You just didn’t want to live there.
Plaintiff: I didn’t want to be there.
Judge: I got you. So he tells you that in January. And then February 1st comes. Do you pay the rent?
Plaintiff: No, ma’am. I asked him about the rent for the following month. His specific words were, I’d rather have somebody else living here.
Judge: So he did not—
Plaintiff: Yep, he did not. He did not want to accept the rent. He said he’d rather have somebody else move in.
Judge: Did you have a month-to-month tenancy at that point, or did you have a year lease?
Plaintiff: No, we had a month-to-month lease.
Judge: The whole time you were there?
Plaintiff: The whole time.
Judge: Let me see the notice he gave you.
(Examining document)
Judge: Okay, so what he gave you was a 30-day notice on your month-to-month tenancy, and he told you that you need to remove yourself from said premises by March 1st. All right. And what about that says you don’t have to pay February rent?
Plaintiff: He told me I didn’t have to pay February.
Judge: Did you ever tell him he didn’t have to pay February rent?
Defendant: No, your Honor.
Judge: Because he doesn’t strike me as the kind of guy who would let you float February. Why would he let you? He has to give you a 30-day notice, but that doesn’t mean you don’t have to pay rent. Why would he tell you that?
Plaintiff: Well, that’s what—when I asked him about it, that’s what he told me. He said, I don’t want it. He said, I’d rather have somebody else living here.
Judge: That only makes sense if you are being evicted for non-payment of rent because then the landlord can’t accept rent from you. Because then they have to let go of the eviction. You have a different situation. You have one where you have a month-to-month tenancy, so he’s giving you your 30 days. You’re done. And yes, he can collect rent. So I have no reason to believe that he wouldn’t collect rent.
(Continued courtroom discussion regarding late move-out, excessive heating, and counterclaims)
Judge: Now, what? What do you think is going to happen here? What’s going to happen is he’s entitled to two months’ rent. So that leaves a remaining rent obligation on your part because the security deposit was $1,800, and rent was only $1,425. That means you owe two times $1,425 minus the $1,800 that’s already there, which leaves a deficit in rent in favor of the defendant in the amount of $1,050. I am ordering you guys to pay that.
Judge: As for the rest of the claims, you’re suing for things you can’t prove, and in New York, you don’t get double damages. Judgment in favor of the defendant for $1,050.

Plaintiff: Uh, I've been in my home for more than 20 years. I believe he's been my neighbor for three.
Judge: Okay, now there's a tree in question that you are quite angry about. What happened? What, according to you, did he do?
Plaintiff: Okay, so one evening in February, I drove into my driveway, and I'm sitting in the car talking, and Mr. Coleman approached my vehicle and he said, "Well, the tree on the right is okay. I just want to cut the branches off of it that are hanging over to my property." And then he said, "The tree on the left side is dead." He said, "I had someone come and look at the tree, and they told me it would cost $1,700 to take the tree down, and it would cost $800 to remove the branches."
Plaintiff: And so I said to Mr. Coleman, "I will need to speak to my husband and other people. I'm not sure that tree is dead. I need to take a look at it." He said, "Okay, I'll see you on Monday." Monday came and went. I never saw Mr. Coleman again. He didn't say anything to anybody. I spoke to my husband and my parents, and we all agreed that the tree was not dead.
Judge: How does anybody know without—
Plaintiff: Well, it's wintertime.
Judge: The fact that there are no leaves in wintertime is not how someone determines whether a tree is dead. You scrape and see if there’s green where you scrape, or you have a tree guy tell you. Did you tell him no, I don’t want the tree removed, or you didn’t say anything?
Plaintiff: We didn’t see each other, so I didn’t say anything.
Judge: So what happened?
Plaintiff: So by Friday of that week, it was February 15th, when I came home, it was dark, but I looked in the backyard and I saw that the tree was cut down.
Judge: All right, so what happened?
Defendant: Okay, um, first off, it wasn’t the first time I had gone to Mrs. Martin. Prior to that, I had had a gate removed, and I went to Mrs. Martin as well as some of the other adjacent neighbors and told them I was going to replace the fence.
Judge: Okay.
Defendant: And they all approved it, and they were actually happy. Now, this tree actually stands on the property line.
Judge: On the property line?
Defendant: Yes. And when putting up the new fence, the fence personnel had to actually go around the trees.
Judge: Really? They had to, or they did?
Defendant: They had to avoid them by going around and encroaching back onto my property to avoid the trees. So I had the right at that point to trim the trees as far as their trunks that were obscuring the line. I didn’t do that, just out of courtesy. I left them as is. But upon living there for two and a half years, I noticed that every other day, debris and branches were falling onto my driveway, my side door—where I have to use to access my home. My child accesses the backyard and the side yard. So it’s a danger in terms of property and in terms of people. So that’s when I said, "You know what, I’m going to trim the trees, just what hangs over my line."
Judge: Do you have anything from an arborist saying the tree was dead?
Defendant: Correct, no.
Judge: If you do, then show it to me. Do you have anything from an arborist saying the tree was dead?
Defendant: Not anything in writing, no.
Judge: Well, how are you going to prove that?
Defendant: Well, again, I have the receipt from—
Judge: No, no, that proves you paid X to remove it. That doesn’t prove it was dead and that’s why you removed it. This is the tree?
Plaintiff: That is the tree.
Judge: Do we have a picture of this tree before it became a post?
Plaintiff: I have it in Google Maps.
Judge: Okay, but I can’t—what you see there is a tree that’s on the neighbor’s property that is, in fact, dead, and they removed it. You want to go over there and show me what you’re saying?
Plaintiff: Sure.
Judge: All right, let's take a look at the images. You said you have a photo from Google Maps?
Plaintiff: Yes, I do. Right here.
Judge: All right. Let me see. [Judge examines the image] This image is from when?
Plaintiff: This is from last year.
Judge: And this shows the tree in question?
Plaintiff: Yes, this is the tree that was cut down.
Judge: All right. Now, Mr. Coleman, do you have any photographic evidence showing the tree was dead or hazardous before removal?
Defendant: I don’t have any personal photos, but I do have the invoice from the tree service.
Judge: Yes, but the invoice only shows payment for removal. It does not establish that the tree was dead.
Defendant: Correct, but the tree service worker verbally confirmed to me that the tree was in bad shape.
Judge: Did you get a written statement from them?
Defendant: No, I did not.
Judge: Ms. Martin, do you have any pictures of the tree closer to the time it was removed?
Plaintiff: No, I wasn’t expecting it to be cut down, so I didn’t take any photos.
Judge: Understood. Based on the evidence presented, my verdict in this case is for the defendant.
Plaintiff: But he cut down my tree without permission!
Judge: Yes, but as the tree was on the property line, and there is reasonable belief it posed a danger, I find that the defendant’s actions were not malicious. Case dismissed.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff, Antoinette Martin, is seeking compensation for the unauthorized removal of a tree that was on the property line between her and her neighbor, Mr. Coleman. The Plaintiff claims that Mr.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant, Mr. Coleman, suggests that he had a legitimate concern about the safety of the tree in question, which was located on the property line. He claims that branches from the tree were frequently falling onto his property, posing a danger to his property and people.

Dialogue:
[Judge turns to the plaintiffs.]
Nathaniel Hamilton and Kimberly Hamilton, you are suing Adam Wolak for $2,100, double your security deposit, because, according to you, he is wrongfully keeping it.
You have a counterclaim against him for $874 in damages.
Let me hear from you first.
Plaintiff: So we moved in there in October of 2015, correct?
Judge: Do you happen to have any pictures of what it looked like when you moved in?
Plaintiff: No.

Plaintiff: $1,050.

Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Okay. He sent a check for how much?
Plaintiff: $795.

Plaintiff: No, we didn’t accept it.

Discussion of Alleged Damages
Judge: Okay, so let’s go over originally what you were attempting to withhold.
The first thing on your list is two broken tiles in the bathroom.

[Defendant hands over a photo.]
Okay, so there’s a crack in one, and a crack in another. Two tiles need to be replaced.
How did that happen?
Plaintiff: If you walk in the bathroom, there’s a slope in front of the toilet. He knows that.
But he put the tile there, and after a while, you keep walking on it, it’s going to crack.

Next—damaged window sill. Do you have a better picture of that? The one up here is no good.

Next—vanity mirror.
Plaintiff: , you admit that the mirror broke?
Plaintiff: I asked him about it while we were still in the apartment. I said, “Adam, the mirror broke.”
He said, “Nate, don’t worry about the mirror. I have a piece of glass down at my other apartment, don’t worry about it, I’ll fix it.”
Judge: So why are you nickel and diming him about it now?
Final Judgment
Judge: So, what we’re left with then is $95 that you’re entitled to keep.
You are suing for double your security deposit because, according to you, he wrongfully withheld it.
But his requirement in your state is that he send you a letter and itemize things.
I may find him to be wrong, but you want me to punish him as though he hadn’t sent you the letter.
He did send you the letter, and he did itemize it.

I am allowing him to deduct $95 for a little bit of this and a little bit of that.

PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff, Nathaniel and Kimberly Hamilton, are suing Adam Wolak for double their security deposit of $1,050, claiming he is wrongfully keeping it, amounting to $2,100. They also have a counterclaim against him for $874 in damages.

The Plaintiffs lived in the property for three years and initially got along well with the Defendant. However, they claim that when it was time for them to leave, Adam Wolak presented them with a list of damages for which he claimed they were responsible.
DEFENDANT:
The Defendant's statements suggest that they had a good relationship with the Plaintiffs during their three-year tenancy. However, when it came time for them to leave, the Defendant claimed there were damages that the Plaintiffs were responsible for, including a broken vanity mirror, damaged window sill, and two broken tiles in the bathroom. The Defendant provided evidence to support these claims, such as photographs of the damaged items.

The Defendant's goal or motive behind their words appears to be to justify withholding a portion of the security deposit to cover the costs of the damages.

Dialogue:
Judge: So, with respect to how to identify a facial sex classification in the first place, I don't think there's any relevant difference between the Court's approach in Bostock and what this Court has long done under the Equal Protection Clause.
In both contexts, the Court has made clear that the right to equal treatment is an individual right, so you look at the particular person and see how the law affects them.
And the Court in both contexts has already made clear that sex just needs to be one but-for causal factor, it doesn't have to be the sole reason or the primary reason.
So, for purposes of identifying whether facial sex classification is happening at the outset, we think it's equal protection principles, as much as Bostock, that carries the day, although, of course, Bostock reinforces those principles.
You asked why this case isn't controlled by Geduldig and Dobbs.
The Court's reasoning there was that when you have a statute that doesn't classify based on sex on its face at all, the fact that the medical condition might be something that only one sex can experience isn't a basis to necessarily say that's facial sex discrimination.
But that doesn't apply in any -- in any relevant respect here, first, because, here, we have the facial sex classification.
The statute says no medications that are inconsistent with your sex.
And, second, these aren't drugs that are limited to one sex or another.
Both males and females alike for decades have been prescribed puberty blockers, hormones, testosterone, estrogen.
They produce the same physical characteristics, as I was saying to Justice Thomas, no matter whether your birth sex is male or female.
So this doesn't look anything like pregnancy, where the Court found that the medical condition itself was expressly limited to one sex.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Well, I -- I -- I'm sure -- I'm not sure that's anything more than a play on words.
Suppose the statute said -- let's just talk about puberty blockers.
Suppose the statute said that puberty blockers may not be prescribed or administered to any minor for the purpose of preventing the onset of puberty prior to the time when puberty generally occurs, okay?
That statute makes no reference whatsoever to anybody's sex.
It applies to all minors.
Would you say the same thing about that?
Judge: So, I'm sorry, if I'm understanding the hypothetical correctly, the statute says you can't take puberty blockers before the time when you would ordinarily have puberty, so it's ruling out precocious puberty?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: You cannot -- no, it doesn't rule out precocious puberty.
It rules out the administration of a puberty blocker for the purpose of preventing puberty from occurring at the time when it generally does.
Judge: I see.
So, if you're hypothesizing a statute where, in essence, the legislature is trying to get at the idea of prohibiting access to these medications for gender dysphoria reasons or otherwise, then maybe you would apply an Arlington Heights type of analysis.
But, of course, that kind of law that you're hypothesizing would also prevent people from taking puberty blockers if they have cancer and want to preserve their fertility because it would prevent them from undergoing puberty at the ordinary time.
I think that's why the legislature hasn't tried to try to circumvent a facial sex classification by drafting a law like that.
It would have many other applications that the State might not want to aim at.
That's very different from a law like this, where the State was being clear we only want to prevent the medications when it's inconsistent with sex, and we're doing that because we have an interest in having minors appreciate their sex and not be disdainful of their sex.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Well, let me ask one final question that addresses Geduldig and Dobbs.
Let's take Geduldig first.
One could make the same argument in Geduldig that you've made here.
A man cannot -- which concerned whether a pregnant woman was entitled to disability benefits for -- for time missed at work when a man would be entitled to disability -- to benefits for time missed at work.
So, in that situation, a man cannot work due to a medical condition that prevents him from working.
He gets benefits.
A woman cannot work due to a medical condition, pregnancy, that prevents her from working for a period of time.
She doesn't get benefits.
It's the same argument you're making here.
Or we could do it in Dobbs.
A man who has a medical condition that causes physical and mental distress and pain and limits his daily activities cannot -- can get a corrective medical procedure.
Let's say it's a hip replacement.
But a woman who has a medical condition that produces similar consequences, namely pregnancy, cannot get an abortion.
So you can make exactly the same argument that you make here under Geduldig and under Dobbs, and yet there was no equal protection problem in either of those cases.
Judge: And that's because the Court said that there was no facial sex classification insofar as using pregnancy does not automatically mean that that's a proxy for sex.
But, here, there's a facial sex classification.
No one can take these medications if it would be inconsistent with their sex.
And that's imposing on the face of the statute two parallel rules on classes of people according to their sex: all adolescent males who want to take these medications to feminize their bodies and all adolescent females who want to take these medications for masculinizing purposes.
That's a facial sex classification through and through --
Defendant: 's Lawyer: All right.
Judge: -- and I don't think it's controlled by Dobbs or Geduldig.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Thank you, Your Honor.
Judge: Justice Sotomayor?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: General, just to unpackage some of this argument, your point, I think, is very clear that Bostock is pertinent only to the extent that, whether it's Title VII or the Equal Protection Clause, the first question is, is the legislature using sex as a classification, correct?
Judge: That's right.
So our argument is that when you're looking for whether there's a facial sex classification, under the Equal Protection Clause, it has always been the same but-for causation principles.
And, of course, we agree with the logic of Bostock, but we think that that logic carries over in this context, where the Court has already said it just needs to be one but-for cause, it doesn't need to be the only cause, and one way you look at that is seeing whether the application of the statute changes when you change the person's sex.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Now Bostock is very different than this case because, in Bostock, what we said is, if you use sex at all, unless you have a statutory exemption, you can't do it, correct?
Judge: Exactly.
And I think that's an important --
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: And, here, under the Equal Protection Clause, we recognize there are inherent differences between the sexes.
Judge: And that can sometimes provide a legitimate basis for classification.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: That's the point, isn't it?
Judge: So you're exactly right.
The standards for liability are different.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Now --
Judge: Under Bostock and under Title VII, you can't use sex.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: -- with respect to the discussion about the European countries and the fact that they haven't limited these treatments altogether, the Cass report, as you point out, explicitly says that medical intervention might be necessary for some adolescents, correct?
Judge: That's right.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: And that is recognized by all the European countries, correct?
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff's statements suggest that they are arguing for the application of equal protection principles, as outlined in the Bostock case, to a statute that prohibits medications that are inconsistent with a person's sex. The Plaintiff argues that this statute constitutes a facial sex classification, as it only applies to minors who wish to take medications to alter their physical characteristics in a manner inconsistent with their sex.

The Plaintiff's goal or motive behind their words is to establish that the statute in question is discriminatory based on sex, and therefore unconstitutional under the Equal Protection Clause. They are attempting to differentiate this case from previous rulings such as Geduldig and Dobbs, where the Court found no equal protection problem, by arguing that the statute in this case explicitly classifies individuals based on their sex.

In three sentences, the Plaintiff's intent is:
1.
DEFENDANT:
The Defendant's lawyer is arguing that the statute in question, which prohibits medications that are inconsistent with a person's sex, constitutes a facial sex classification under the Equal Protection Clause. The Defendant's goal is to demonstrate that this statute, unlike the ones in the cases Geduldig and Dobbs, creates a clear distinction based on sex, as it only allows for medications that align with a person's sex at birth.

The Defendant's lawyer is trying to establish that this statute is not controlled by the decisions in Geduldig and Dobbs because, in those cases, the Court found no facial sex classification, as the medical conditions in question were not limited to one sex. In contrast, the statute in question explicitly prohibits medications that are inconsistent with a person's sex, creating a clear distinction between males and females.

Dialogue:
Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to the People's Court. Next case on the docket: the plaintiff says the defendant rented an apartment in his house and skipped out without paying all the rent, but the defendant says he paid in full. It’s the case of "The Rent's Too Damn Inaccurate."
Judge: Wayne Monsky, you are suing Michael Johnson for $1,794.42 in damages, unpaid rent, and other items that you say you were damaged when your tenant, Mr. Johnson, left abruptly without notice and without paying. Tell me what's going on.
Plaintiff: His girlfriend, Pam, moved out at the end of July, beginning of August. I wouldn’t have known, other than I actually went there in the beginning of August when her uncle was moving her stuff out. I was there to bother Michael about mowing the lawn. We had an agreement for him to mow the lawn. Even though Pam moved out in July-August, she paid for most of the rent for August and September—$775 out of $800. The $25 was for the mowing of the lawn.
Judge: So all of the rent? In other words, his rent was $775 because you gave him a $25 discount for mowing the lawn?
Plaintiff: Yes, but he didn’t mow the lawn. That’s part of it. At the end of September, when the payments from Pam ran out, he called me and said he was moving out. At that time, I told him I was going to have to show the apartment, and he said, "No way, you can’t show the apartment." We bickered about it, and he said, "Only if I’m there, and you have to make arrangements through me."
Judge: But that's exactly how you must show the apartment unless he agrees otherwise. The law gives you the right to do it, but you have to make arrangements with him and give him notice.
Plaintiff: Throughout October, we tried multiple times to come to an agreement. He’d say, "Oh, this date," and then at the last second, it would change. In the end, all I got was one day for less than two hours—October 25th.
Judge: Did anyone show interest?
Plaintiff: Two people looked, but the place was trashed because he was still moving out. No one was interested at that time. I had over two dozen people originally from the advertising.
Judge: When did you put the advertisement in?
Plaintiff: Right away at first, then another one in early October.
Judge: You're suing for advertising costs. Do you have the advertising here?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: October wasn’t paid, and then, according to you, you weren’t able to rent it out for November because of his withholding permission to show the place. When did you rent it?
Plaintiff: December 1st.
Judge: Mr. Johnson, what’s going on?
Defendant: Your honor, on January 29th, he was paid $1,054 from Pamela Heckley to close the case between both of us. He also kept an $800 security deposit for supposed damages.
Judge: You were living there with your girlfriend. It didn’t work out?
Defendant: Yes. She lived there until mid-August.
Judge: So she moves out. Rent is paid until September?
Defendant: Yes. Then October wasn’t paid. I moved out.
Judge: When?
Defendant: October 31st.
Judge: According to him, he couldn’t rent it for November because you wouldn’t allow him to show the place. Respond to that.
Defendant: I gave him two months' notice that I was moving out. I also gave him a month's notice that I wasn’t able to pay October's rent.
Judge: But you’re saying, "I hereby declare I have no intention of paying October?"
Defendant: But he was paid for that when Pam made the deal.
Judge: We’ll talk about that. So, in October, how many times did he come to show the apartment?
Defendant: Twice. Once I was there. The second time, I was not. He told me he was going in anyway.
Judge: Were you present during the first showing?
Witness (Pam): Yes. The place had boxes, but it wasn’t bad. Potential tenants could see past that.
Judge: Did you overhear discussions between them?
Witness: Yes. He asked to come when my boyfriend was at work, but Michael wanted to be there.
Judge: The second time he said he was going in anyway, why didn’t you stop him?
Defendant: I didn’t want to argue. I already told him I couldn’t pay rent.
Judge: Did he send you a letter demanding money?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: Let me see it. This is different. This is a breakdown for Pamela’s mother. It includes rent, damages, advertising, court fees, and more.
Plaintiff: Pamela covered the damages. Now I’m pursuing Michael for lost rent.
Judge: You have an $800 security deposit, and Pamela sent $1,054. That’s more than what you’re suing for. Why are you not satisfied?
Plaintiff: I was trying to keep Pam out of this, per their request. She didn’t pay rent, only damages.
Judge: But you already received more than you’re suing for. You cannot collect twice. My verdict is for the defendant.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff, Wayne Monsky, is seeking $1,794.42 from the Defendant, Michael Johnson, for unpaid rent, damages, and other costs related to the rental of an apartment in his house. The Plaintiff claims that Johnson skipped out on paying rent for October and November, and also withheld permission to show the apartment to potential tenants, causing a delay in renting the property. The Plaintiff argues that the Defendant owes him for the lost rent, advertising costs, and damages to the property.
DEFENDANT:
The Defendant, Michael Johnson, is suggesting that he paid all the rent due until September, as his girlfriend, Pam, paid $775 out of the $800 rent for August and September. He also claims that he moved out on October 31st, giving two months' notice that he was moving out and a month's notice that he couldn't pay October's rent.

The Defendant is also stating that he was paid $1,054 by Pamela Heckley on January 29th, which includes the rent for October that he couldn't pay. He argues that the Plaintiff, Wayne Monsky, is not satisfied because Pamela covered the damages, and he is now being pursued for lost rent.

The Defendant's goal or motive behind his words seems to be to prove that he has fulfilled his obligations as a tenant by paying the rent until September and by paying the October rent through Pamela Heckley.

Dialogue:
Judge: This is the plaintiff, Annette Good. She says she was scammed into buying a piece of junk car from the defendant. Now, he refuses to take the car back and return her money. The defendant has ignored her. She needed to get his attention, filed this lawsuit, and is now suing him for the $2,237.12 she's out.
This is the defendant, Christopher James. He says the 2002 Lincoln with 182,000 miles on it was in excellent mechanical condition and drove like a cream puff, so the plaintiff bought it. Once she had it, she started to complain about every little thing, and he told her he was sorry, but there was nothing more he could do for her. Oh, she didn’t like that. She’s been harassing and threatening his family ever since, and that isn’t cool.
He’s accused of unloading a lemon.
The defendant has filed a countersuit for $1,500 for harassment.
All parties, please rise.
Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket: The plaintiff says she bought a piece of junk from the defendant, while the defendant insists the 2002 Lincoln with 182,000 miles was in perfect condition.
It’s the case of Your Lincoln Was Shot.
Thank you, Douglas.
Come in.
Judge: Okay, Ms. Good, you bought a car and you don’t want it?
Plaintiff: Yes, Ma’am.
Judge: Why not? What’s going on?
Plaintiff: Some of everything is wrong with it—mechanical issues.
Judge: Who did you buy the car from?
Plaintiff: The defendant.
Judge: How did you find the car? Where was it advertised?
Plaintiff: On OfferUp.
Judge: Did you go test drive it?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Did you take a mechanic to look at it?
Plaintiff: No, Ma’am.
Judge: Alright, so you bought the car. Is there any paperwork from the sale of the car?
Plaintiff: No, Ma’am.
Judge: No paperwork? Who needs paperwork, right? Until you get to court. Nothing? No bill of sale?
Plaintiff: I had this.
Judge: Okay, let me see. What did you do with the paperwork?
Defendant: I gave it to her.
Judge: You didn’t keep a copy of anything?
Defendant: No, Ma’am.
Judge: Do you do this a lot?
Defendant: No. Well, my uncle owns a shop, so a lot of times customers will come in, and if I can, I’ll buy the car if it’s a nice car. Some people just come in and the repairs are too great.
Judge: Do you work at your uncle’s shop?
Defendant: I do.
Judge: Are you a mechanic?
Defendant: I do the cleaning for his cars.
Judge: Okay, so you sell cars. How many do you sell a year?
Defendant: Maybe three or four.
Judge: What do you do with the ones you keep?
Defendant: I keep them.
Judge: How many cars do you have?
Defendant: Five right now.
Judge: Okay, it’s an addiction. I know because I live with a man with that addiction. Anyway, tell me, what went wrong with the car, and how fast did it go wrong?
Plaintiff: The next day. I had to get it inspected, and it wouldn’t pass inspection. So I had to pay the guy at the inspection station an extra $50 to get it passed.
Judge: What does that mean? You bribed them?
Plaintiff: Yeah, basically.
Judge: Alright, so go on.
Plaintiff: About two hours later, the check engine light came on. I took it to the shop, and a bunch of codes popped up. Then it started smoking.
Judge: Did you call the defendant?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: And what did he say?
Plaintiff: He acted like he didn’t remember selling me the car.
Judge: That sounds crazy because it is crazy.
Defendant: I spoke with her mechanic, and all he said was wrong with the car was that it needed a tune-up. I sent him the money to do the tune-up.
Judge: Why did you do that?
Defendant: Because I’m a nice person.
Judge: Did you think you were legally obligated to do it?
Defendant: No, because the car was sold as is.
Judge: Do you know what as is means?
Plaintiff: Yes, but his advertisement said the car was in great condition.
Judge: Did you bring a mechanic before buying it?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: So why do you think as is doesn’t apply?
Plaintiff: I didn’t know it was going to do this.
Judge: That’s why people take mechanics. Alright, on to the harassment claim. Defendant, why are you counter-suing?
Defendant: She showed up at my house and my workplace.
Judge: Did you do that?
Plaintiff: Yes, but I just wanted my money back.
Judge: That’s not how it works. Based on what I’m hearing, you don’t have a case. Your lawsuit is dismissed.
Plaintiff: Okay, I’ll just get the car fixed.
Judge: Good luck with that.
Defendant: I just tried to help.
Judge: Good for you. Case dismissed.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff, Annette Good, is seeking a refund of $2,237.12 from the Defendant, Christopher James, for a car she purchased from him. She claims the car, a 2002 Lincoln with 182,000 miles, was in poor mechanical condition and has multiple issues, including failing inspection, check engine light, and smoking. She did not take the car to a mechanic before purchasing it and did not have any paperwork from the sale.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant, Christopher James, is a car seller who occasionally sells vehicles, primarily from his uncle's shop. He claims that the 2002 Lincoln he sold to the Plaintiff, Annette Good, was in excellent mechanical condition. However, the Plaintiff alleges that the car had numerous mechanical issues shortly after purchase, including failing an inspection, check engine light issues, and smoking.

Plaintiff: Yes, Your Honor. I am suing 247 Pawn Shop, represented here by Shimle Kapor, for $5,000, the statutory max in our state because they have sold some items that I pawned for $250. I know what I pawned was worth more than $200, but I just needed the money at that time. I pawned three necklaces, two bangles, three bracelets, a ring, and a charm with a cross.
Judge: Have you ever pawned anything before?
Plaintiff: Yes, Your Honor. It's usually the case that the loan is a lot less than the value of the item because they're not in the business of making a bad deal. But anyhow, I pawned it on November 12th, and I had until March 12th, 2012, to redeem my pawn.
Judge: Do you have the paperwork from the pawn?
Plaintiff: Yes, Your Honor, here it is. On February 15, 2012, I called and spoke to this lady and told her I was coming in that Friday to pick up my pawn. She sounded kind of agitated, so I just said, "Listen, I want to pick up my pawn by Friday, here's my ticket number." No problem. But I didn’t go that Friday because I got distracted. Plaintiff: I immediately went to the location, and there was a new owner there. He said the previous owner didn’t leave anyone's property there. Judge: When did you guys move, ma'am?
Defendant: We moved on November 28th, 2011.
Judge: Why didn’t she get a letter until May?
Defendant: I personally gave her the letter and exchanged my phone number with her. I spoke to her. I told her if she wanted to extend the pawn, she would need to come to the new location. I told her about the new address, which was just down the street, and the phone number stayed the same.
Judge: So you personally spoke with her and gave her the new location?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am. I personally spoke to her.
Judge: When did you do that?
Defendant: In January or February.
Judge: What did you do, call all your clients?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am. I personally went to their places and gave them the letters.
Judge: So you personally went to each and every customer’s home?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: Really?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: You went to every single customer's home?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: I find that hard to believe.
Defendant: No, ma’am. I went to each and every customer’s home.
Judge: Are you saying you personally went to the home of each and every customer?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: I find that hard to believe.
Plaintiff: She never called me.
Judge: Do you have phone records that you called her?
Defendant: Verizon doesn’t provide phone records, Your Honor. I asked for the unlimited call details, but they don’t show phone numbers.
Judge: Do you have proof of when you sent the certified letter?
Defendant: Yes, I sent the certified letter in May.
Judge: So you’re saying you personally went to everyone’s home, but you still sent the certified letter in May?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: I want to see proof that you sent the letter and when it was sent.
Defendant: I sent it in May, and I personally visited each and every customer.
Judge: Why did you wait until May to send the letter?
Defendant: Because the pawn expired on different dates. We were waiting for more time before sending the letter.
Judge: But you moved in November. Why are you sending the letter six months later?
Defendant: I was waiting for customers to pick up their pawns.
Judge: So you sent the letter in May, but you moved in November. Why did you wait until May to notify customers?
Defendant: We were waiting for them to pick up the pawn.
Judge: So you’re going to notify her about the move after her pawn expires?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am. She called me, and I gave her all the information.
Judge: And now where is the stuff?
Defendant: It has been melted.
Judge: You mailed the letter in May, but the letter says her pawn expired in March. That’s two months late. How do you explain that?
Defendant: We were waiting to send the letters.
Judge: You can't possibly think you abided by the law and your contract when you mailed the notice two months late.
Defendant: I was waiting for the customers to pick up their stuff.
Judge: I'm going to award you $400, the value of the pawn, and an additional $500 in punitive damages for your fraudulent business practices.
Plaintiff: I should have gotten more for my jewelry.
Judge: I’m awarding you $900 plus court costs.
Judge: The defendant loses the case and has been ordered to pay the plaintiff.

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to The People's Court. Next case on the docket—the plaintiff says the defendants hired her to write and produce a skit but then got stiffed. The defendants say the skit was lame and she wouldn't take notes. It's the case of your skit is against I've been sworn, your honor.
Bailiff: Thank you.
Judge: You're welcome. Okay, Brenda Hill, you are suing Vanel Loyal and Lois Nelson, yes?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: For $914 that you say you are out as a result of them pulling the plug on a skit that you were going to perform. Okay, explain to me what's going on here.
Plaintiff: Each year we have a fundraiser, and we provide funds for kids that are coming out of high school and wanting to go to college.
Judge: So this fundraiser raises funds for college for the kids?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Okay. And I'm here about a breach of an agreement. Okay, all right. Who’s we? Who puts this fundraiser together? I think you folks don’t want me to use the name of the organization. That’s fine, I’ll respect that. But just tell me what kind of organization this is. Who’s we?
Plaintiff: A fraternal organization.
Judge: All right. So go ahead.
Plaintiff: We had rehearsals. I had written it and rehearsals—
Judge: Wrote what?
Plaintiff: I wrote the skit.
Judge: A skit. And what was the skit supposed to be about?
Plaintiff: The skit was supposed to be about a small group of women who had lost their husbands. Now, after years of mourning, they were going to enjoy themselves.
Judge: So you all get together on what date was this supposed to happen or what date did this happen?
Plaintiff: The 30th of March.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: And I had gotten the people together. They came. I was a little late and I called Lois.
Judge: Lois is Lois Nelson?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: And she’s your mom?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: Okay. I called Lois and asked her—what time were you supposed to be there?
Plaintiff: I was supposed to be there about 7:00.
Judge: And you were calling her to tell her you were running late, and you got there what time?
Plaintiff: 7:15.
Judge: Okay. All right. To tell her to ask my guests that I was on my way. I was five minutes away from the restaurant.
Judge: So this fundraiser is taking place at a restaurant?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Okay. Go on.
Plaintiff: When I talked with her, she told me, "Oh, it’s been canceled."
Judge: The event or the skit?
Plaintiff: No, the skit.
Judge: She said the skit was canceled?
Plaintiff: When I asked her to tell my guests that I would be right there.
Judge: And when you arrived, what happened?
Plaintiff: When I did arrive, I said, "Why did you send my guests away?"
Defendant: Wait. She sent your guests away?
Plaintiff: Yes, because they were there to take part in it.
Judge: Right, but your guests decided to leave because it was canceled. She didn’t say, "You got to get out of here," did she?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: Did you ever ask her why the skit was canceled? I’m not understanding the role the skit has in the fundraiser. Is this just one skit that was going to happen?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: How many skits were going to go on?
Plaintiff: Three.
Judge: All right. And then your group—who decided for your group that yours wouldn’t go on?
Plaintiff: I didn’t.
Defendant: Your honor, can I—?

Defendant: As I said before, this is a fraternal organization, and it has two major fundraisers a year. This is one of them. Within this one fraternal organization, there are 133 smaller suborganizations, and each one voluntarily chooses to get together to put on a skit to compete to win.
Judge: Is that every year, or just this year?
Defendant: It’s been for the last two years since my administration.
Judge: And what’s your role in this organization?
Defendant: I’m over the organization for the entire Commonwealth of Pennsylvania.
Judge: So you’re the Big Kahuna. You’re on top of all these little subgroups?
Defendant: Exactly.
[Conversation continues with arguments about organization rules, responsibility for the skit’s cancellation, and financial claims made by the plaintiff. The judge ultimately rules in favor of the defendants, finding that no legally binding promise was broken.]

Dialogue:
Judge: But the in rem and -- oh.
Judge: -- at the end of the day, you're really just asking us to over --to throw out the general rule that sovereigns can't be sued for appropriations of this sort, right? I mean, once you say commingling counts, well, then everything's -- everything's pretty much fair game, except for the rare possibility that's been mentioned that they happen to have an account that's, you know, appropriated funds in a particular instance.
It seems to me that -- is there -- is there anything wrong with it? In other words, we know that from Sabbatino and the second Hickenlooper amendment that Congress had in mind a much narrower exception than that.
So, other than curious, bizarre situations of accounting, like the one when they have a separate account for appropriated property, this is really just throwing out the whole sovereign immunity principles under which the rest of the world operates?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: I don't think that's quite right, Mr. Chief Justice.
Congress passed the expropriation exception over the Executive's opposition, knowing that that was a departure from international law.
It did so in response to Sabbatino, but it wasn't -- the language that it passed wasn't limited to the facts of Sabbatino. Congress enacted broad language.
Sabbatino itself involved fungible property.
The Court there recognized traceability problems. Congress knew that but enacted the broad language anyway.
Judge: Well, but -- sorry to interrupt, but it seems to me you're just agreeing with me that that's what will happen under your theory.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: So no.
I also think there are significant guardrails to our theory. One is the guardrail that this Court put up in Philipps in interpreting the expropriation exception requiring that you're only talking about domestic takings.
And so -- I'm sorry, the domestic takings rule applies.
And so a foreign country can't be sued for takings from its own citizens.
That's already one limitation.
In addition to that, though, plaintiffs actually have to be able to -- to -- to show liquidation and commingling.
Usually, when somebody's property is stolen, at that point, they don't know what happened to it. This is the rare case, the Holocaust is the rare case, where there is extensive documentation of what Hungary's practice was.
And we put that documentation before the district court.
Hungary did not rebut it. That's why the -- the district court relied on three key sources that we put in: the Hungarian Constitutional Court decision from 1993, a manuscript about the Holocaust in Hungary written by one of Hungary's own experts, and archives from the U.S. Holocaust Museum.
All three of those sources established that Hungary had a practice of not just expropriating but then also liquidating the property, melting it down, commingling it with state funds, including in -- in -- in the -- the country's general -- general accounts.
Judge: Well, you think in most --
Lawyer of Plaintiff: And so this is the rare --
Judge: -- you think in most cases that the appropriated property is not commingled with the general funds?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: It would have to be established.
And, again, typically, a plaintiff is not going to know that.
This is a case where -- because the Holocaust is a unique situation, where we actually do have documentation.
A plaintiff can't just go into court and say: My property was taken, I have no idea what happened to it, but I think it was probably commingled, so, therefore, the sovereign -- the sovereign immunity doesn't apply.
Judge: Counsel --
Lawyer of Plaintiff: That --
Judge: The example you gave, Mr. Dvoretzky, you said, you know, unless a country were stupid enough to establish a Holocaust expropriation fund, but I'm wondering whether, even if a country did establish a Holocaust expropriation fund, whether that would be good enough under the Petitioners' theory, because, you know, there were 500,000 victims of the Hungarian Holocaust.
So there, a lot of money, you know, from different people going into that fund.
So that's all commingled.
So, even in that case, it seems the Petitioner would say the country is off the hook.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: So, again, I -- I think that there's no reason to think that -- that Hungary actually had this kind of a Holocaust theft fund.
So I think that --
Judge: Yes, I know.
It's -- it's an example to sort of suggest -- I -- I mean, a country's never going to pay, you know, under the expropriation exception, which Congress passed presumably to do something.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Right.
Judge: Like, even if a country set up an expropriation fund, all that money is commingled too.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Right.
It -- it is -- it's certainly commingled with the country's overall asset -- asset base, sure.
Judge: Even if it's not.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Yeah.
Yeah.
Judge: Counsel, can I ask you -- because I guess I'm still stuck on whether or not the tracing requirement is inherent in the nature of the nexus that is necessary for jurisdiction here, so let me ask you a hypothetical.
Suppose Hungary obtained expropriated art and then it brought it into, you know, its museum somewhere and then lost track of it, lost it.
We just can't find it.
It never sold it. It never exchanged it.
It just disappeared.
Would there be jurisdiction to sue under this statute in your view?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: So I think we have to distinguish under the statute between Hungary and the instrumentality.
For the instrumentality, the nexus requirement does not require that the property be present in the United States.
Judge: Okay.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: The instrumentality has to have the property or property exchanged for it, and it has to engage in commercial activity in the United States.
Judge: Okay.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: In your hypothetical, where we don't know, however, that either the instrumentality or the country still even has the property --
Judge: Right.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: -- if that can't be established, then I think the expropriation exception probably would not be satisfied in that situation.
Judge: And it's because the property, as far as we know in terms of facts on the ground right now, doesn't exist.
It's not present.
It isn't there.
So that suggests to me that this really is about identifiable property that is presently either in the United States or with the foreign instrumentality that is doing work with the United States, right?
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff's goal in this courtroom discussion appears to be to argue for an exception to the sovereign immunity principle, specifically in cases where a sovereign nation has appropriated property and commingled it with general funds. The Plaintiff argues that this exception is justified because of the unique circumstances surrounding the Holocaust, where there is extensive documentation of Hungary's practice of expropriating and liquidating property, including melting it down and commingling it with state funds.

The Plaintiff emphasizes that this is a rare case where such documentation exists, and that in most cases, a plaintiff would not be able to prove commingling due to the nature of property theft. The Plaintiff also argues that there are significant guardrails to their theory, including the requirement for liquidation and commingling, the domestic takings rule, and the interpretation of the expropriation exception requiring that the takings be domestic.

The Plaintiff also clarifies that the exception does not apply to the sovereign nation itself, but rather to an instrumentality of the nation that engages in commercial activity in the United States.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant (Lawyer of Plaintiff) is arguing for a broad interpretation of the expropriation exception to sovereign immunity, which would allow for lawsuits against sovereign entities for appropriations of property, as long as the appropriated property can be shown to be commingled with the sovereign's general funds. The Defendant emphasizes that there are guardrails to this theory, such as the requirement for documentation of the commingling and the domestic takings rule, which prevents foreign countries from being sued for takings from their own citizens. The Defendant also clarifies that the expropriation exception does not require the property to be present in the United States, but rather requires that the foreign instrumentality have the property or property exchanged for it, and engage in commercial activity in the United States.

Dialogue:
Judge: We'll hear argument first this morning in Case 22-1008, Corner Post versus the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Corner Post opened for business in 2018. Since then, it's paid several hundred thousand dollars in debit card fees that it thinks are unlawful. But the government says that Corner Post's clock to challenge those fees actually started in 2011, seven years before Corner Post pumped a single gallon of gas. The government is wrong.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Corner Post's clock started when it swiped its first debit card and paid its first fee. That is the right outcome here for three main reasons.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: First and most importantly, the text. Section 2401's limitations period starts when a claim "first accrues." This Court has said that phrase means the clock starts only once a plaintiff can sue, and this Court has also said that an APA plaintiff can sue only once it's first harmed by regulation.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: We just want the Court to apply those settled principles. By contrast, the government wants a special rule that contradicts how accrual statutes have worked since at least the 1830s. That government-only carveout would convert Section 2401 into a repose-based statute like the Hobbs Act.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: But Congress knows exactly how to craft repose-based statutes when it wants to, and it hasn't done so for APA claims.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Second, with no textual foothold, the government resorts to policy arguments. It says that siding with Corner Post will undermine reliance interests because it will let plaintiffs challenge rules that are older than six years.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: But challenges to those rules already happen all the time in the as-applied context, and the government admits that as-applied challenges have no time limit.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Third, if Congress's textual choice leads to outcomes that the government doesn't like, this Court has said that those concerns should be addressed to Congress, not to this Court.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: This Court's role is simply to enforce the value judgments that Congress has already made. We ask that it do so here. I welcome the Court's questions.
Judge: Do you have any examples of accrual cases or questions where the injury and the unlawful conduct are on different dates?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, that’s really any typical accrual statute. For example, there are torts where a tort is committed and the cause of action is not complete until later, until the harm is felt. So that's a basic. We think there's nothing remarkable about that fact pattern.
Judge: Well, but how many cases are like yours, where the regulation has been adopted, it's final, and you are not yet in business, so it can't apply to you? Are there any cases like yours where the injury is later?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: So --
Judge: -- where the injury occurs long after the rule is adopted?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: So there's the Herr case in the Sixth Circuit, which starts the circuit split in this context. That's one case where it happened.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: But I think the question, unless I'm misunderstanding it, is are there any other statutes of limitations that operate the way we say that 2401 does?
Judge: Yes.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: So we think that 20 -- we're not aware of any --
Judge: Actually, I'm more interested in the fact pattern that we have here. Your business -- you have a rule that's adopted. It's final. It's been challenged. Then you go into business, you begin to operate under these rules, and you claim, of course, that's the beginning of your injury, and then you say that restarts the statute of limitations. That's what I'm interested in.

The Plaintiff's statements suggest that Corner Post, a business that opened in 2018, has been paying debit card fees that it believes are unlawful since 2018. However, the government argues that the clock to challenge these fees started in 2011, seven years before Corner Post began operations. The Plaintiff's lawyer argues that the government's interpretation is incorrect, as the limitations period should start when a claim "first accrues," which, according to previous court rulings, means the clock starts only once a plaintiff can sue, and when a plaintiff is first harmed by regulation.
