Lawyer of Plaintiff: The only publicly available information about the settlement is what we have -- what was attached to the red brief and what -- it's in Westlaw.
It's cited at page 7 of our brief.
What the SEC had in terms of documents is -- is --is not publicly available. I'm not sure whether Mr. Katyal was -- was talking about what his clients gave them.
I -- I just -- I don't know that.
It's not publicly available.
Judge: But whatever he said the documents showed is not anything that you're prepared to say they showed?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: No.
What I --
Judge: Other than that --what you read?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Yes, excuse me.
I -- I -- I completely agree.
So what the SEC's findings are are embodied in this settlement agreement that --
Judge: You answered the Chief by saying there was one purpose, frivolous lawsuits. I thought the --there was a second purpose, which was to ensure that defendants knew what they were defending against.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: That -- that's right.
It is also --so there's -- there's -- I think the -- the --some of the amicus briefs on Respondents' side point out that, in general, particularity requirements are to assist with notice.
And so I think that's there. And -- and maybe just, Chief -- Mr. Chief Justice, to return to your question, if what we're talking about is the frivolous, that it's more than --this is more -- this is doing more than Rule 11, I would certainly agree with you there.
Judge: Counsel, you've ended answering me, right?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Oh, sure.
Judge: Yeah.
Judge: If the dissent had been the majority, what problems would have been created going forward from your perspective?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: If the dissent had been the majority, I -- I think, honestly, what we have is a -- is a disagreement on the facts that I -- I --I think --
Judge: So that goes to your answer to Justice Barrett's question it sounds like then?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Yes.
I mean, I -- I -- I think that it would have a profound effect on this case, but I'm not sure that it would set bad law going forward. I think that -- just to -- to touch on a few things very briefly that had already come up, in terms of how to handle expert reports, Justice Alito, I think you said that it may be difficult for district courts to know what to do with these very technical sets of facts that you'll find in expert reports.
That's going to be the same problem if they're not in expert reports, and they might even be more confusing because I think, if you have plaintiffs' lawyers trying to articulate a bunch of highly technical facts, it might come out a little bit more garbled.
So I don't think that's a particular reason to adopt a special --a special rule, and the PSLRA does not. Justice Kavanaugh, I think you asked about the Ninth Circuit applying different rules, and I really don't think that they are.
And the best evidence perhaps of this is that Anderson, the case that Petitioners cite and rely on as doing it right, is repeatedly relying on Ninth Circuit precedent. And I'd also note that the district court decision, which, again, Petitioners are relying on, that also is repeatedly relying on Ninth Circuit decisions.
So I don't think this is a case where the court of appeals has gone rogue and is doing anything different than the other -- the other courts of appeals here. And in terms of the slide, the main thing that I want to say there is that the -- the Ninth Circuit did address the slide.
I think that, you know, this Court is not a court of factual analysis, but if you're concerned that the Ninth Circuit just wasn't doing things, the -- there's many pages of analysis from 48a to 55a explaining that they were examining that.
And -- and, similarly, there's many pages of analysis examining the detailed factual allegations underlying the expert report.
That's 20a to 22a. So unless there are further questions?
Judge: Anything further? Thank you, counsel. Rebuttal, Mr. Katyal.
Lawyer of Defendant: Thank you, Your Honor.
Five points. First, Mr. Gupta said that particularity requires detail.
We quite agree.
Yet the Ninth Circuit said that this complaint had enough detail. This is why you need to reverse.
It's dangerous to say this amount of detail is enough for a complaint. That's not a change in our position.
They're the ones -- I'll explain that in a moment -- but they're the ones changing their position.
They are running away from the Ninth Circuit. The Ninth Circuit's decision said that the CEO "would have reflected their expert opinion" and does not engage in any sort of comparative analysis. 42a is not a comparative analysis.
They -- the Ninth Circuit never even bothered answering our competing hypothesis, which was the pricing explanation.
You have to reverse that.
And as the amici say, otherwise, you're letting circuit courts let decisions --let litigants provide these cases and state a claim without a comparative inquiry. And you certainly have to reject their new idea that these employees are enough because they don't indicate when the CEO knew something.
Was it after or before that watershed SKU event? Nothing said that they -- they do say that the CEO actually saw the data at the time.
The best they have is a video that was taken from 2012, well before the class period. And Mr. -- and Judge Sanchez explains at 62a why that slide is so devastating.
And he also explains at 82a why not a single employee ever said the CEO saw anything damaging during the class period. You can't let this stand for the reasons the amici say.
Congress in the Reform Act, as the Chief Justice indicated, wanted to stop lawsuits like this, lawsuits that allow fraud by -- fraud by hindsight.
Judge: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff's lawyer is arguing in this courtroom conversation that the Plaintiff's brief was honest about the settlement with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and that the only publicly available information about the settlement is what was attached to the red brief and is cited at page 7 of their brief. The Plaintiff's lawyer also suggests that if the dissent had been the majority, it would have a profound effect on this case but may not set bad law going forward. The Plaintiff's goal or motive appears to be to defend their case and argue that the settlement provides sufficient detail to support their claims, while also emphasizing that the Ninth Circuit's decision in the case is not significantly different from other courts of appeals.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant's statements suggest that they are arguing against the Plaintiff's position in the case, particularly in relation to the specificity and detail required in the complaint. The Defendant argues that the Ninth Circuit's decision on the case is incorrect and should be reversed, as it does not require a comparative analysis and allows for lawsuits that allow for fraud by hindsight, which was supposed to be prevented by the Reform Act. The Defendant also argues that the Ninth Circuit's decision is inconsistent with their own previous decisions and is allowing litigants to state a claim without a comparative inquiry.

Douglas (Plaintiff’s Representative):
Okay, Woodberry Auction, represented here by Thomas Schwanky, Schwanky, yes, you’re okay. You are suing?
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
Rosa Aona.
Douglas (Plaintiff’s Representative):
And Amir Carpets. You are the owner of Amir Carpets, yes? All right, for $585, the purchase price of a rug that he bought from you. And according to you, he didn’t sign the check, so you weren’t able to actually deposit the check.
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
Whoops. Okay, what happened?
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
First of all, we’re in the auction business. We sell on commission; we don’t own the goods. We had an auction in May of 2011, which was a real-time auction and an online auction. This rug was in an online auction at the end of May. It was available to be seen and inspected before the auction.
Judge: When was the live auction going to be?
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
The live auction commenced on May 21st.
Judge: So when did he buy this?
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
He bought the carpet May 31st.
Judge: Okay, so it didn’t sell at the live auction?
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
That’s correct.
Judge: That’s correct. And then you kept the items online for auction bids, and he bid?
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
Correct.
Judge: Okay.
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
He registered, left us a message, "I’m interested in buying handmade carpets." I know he’s got the rug; we know he got it. But so tell me about the circumstances where you folks accept a check that’s not signed. Is that what happened?
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
What happened is he came to pick up the rug. He was late. When he came to get the rug, he gave my administrative assistant a check. Apparently, she didn’t look at it. He took the check, which was not signed. We discovered it the next day. My assistant asked me what to do, and I said, "Please give him a call." My assistant went to put—took the check.
Judge: That’s correct?
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
That’s right. She got fired?
Judge: No, she didn’t.
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
Okay, go ahead.
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
She’s still with me, but her mother’s ill, and she couldn’t come today.
Judge: Okay, so I said we’ll give him a call, which she did. He said—she asked him to send another one. He told that he would.
Judge: You have an affidavit from her?
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
I do. Can I see it?
Judge: Well, it’s not sworn.
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
Why wouldn’t you bring her?
Judge: I mean, I heard what you said, but I mean, that’s not the issue.
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
Well, no, I’ll—what the issue is—
Judge: I understand. May I please see whatever you do have that’s not sworn and not—
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
Her. This is the registration.
Judge: Why wasn’t the check signed, sir?
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
I did turn that in when I arrived there. They were rushing me too much.
Judge: You were late?
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
I was late. Yes, I was. You know, it was a one-and-a-half-hour drive for me. I asked directions many times.
Judge: So how late were you?
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
I was just maybe a couple of minutes. I don’t know.
Judge: A couple of minutes?
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
Yes.
Judge: And according to your assistant, he’s—how—
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
Oh, it’s not even signed. Let alone sworn. It’s just a paragraph.
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
The next day, Tom showed me the unsigned check, and I called him to let him know the check was unsigned. He said he would send us a new check by mail, and that you were an hour and a half late, not a few minutes, an hour and a half.
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
No, they close at—I don’t know—5 or 6:00. I was in that town, but all right, so you get there, and what happened?
Judge: They just had the car all wrapped up and just put it in my trunk.
Judge: Are you trying to explain why you gave them a check without inspecting the merchandise? Is that what you’re trying to say?
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
Because they were saying that it was late, and the carpet was all wrapped up and whatever. I said that the—
Judge: So why wouldn’t you just unwrap it first?
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
Oh, it’s too big. Too big. I couldn’t do it myself.
Judge: How long did it take to unwrap it in my courtroom?
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
Seven minutes.
Judge: Okay, so why couldn’t it be unwrapped there?
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
Because it was wrapped with the plastic things, and it was tidy and convenient to just move it on down the road, doggy. That’s all. It’s because it was just like—
Judge: Everybody’s lazy. And then all of a sudden, look at you now. You got time for all this now. Nobody had seven minutes then. But even though—
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
All right, so they didn’t tell me about all the damages that this carpet has.
Judge: Oh, you might have seen it if you unrolled the rug, right?
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
But can I see the online auction pictures? Like, I mean, you bought the rug, okay, from the pictures that were online, right?
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
Yes.
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
And so then you bring that rug back to your place, and what is your place?
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
It’s a place that I buy rugs for myself and sell it for my home. I don’t have a store or anything.
Judge: Yes, okay. But if you look at the back, you can see all the damages and all the—
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
I mean, in the picture, I can see this. There’s some—what is this, by the way?
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
It’s a—uh—I don’t really know. If that’s not my job, we sell these.
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
What was it advertised as?
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
It was advertised as a large-room-sized oriental carpet, Blue Field, from a Virginia collector. That’s it. That’s all it was advertised as. There wasn’t anything more.
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
No, okay.
Judge: And how is it not what that says?
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
If you look, you can see all the patches on the carpet that they didn’t see.
Judge: Patches on the—on the—?
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
I’m not lifting this.
Judge: What patches on the bottom of the carpet?
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
Yes. If you—you know, you’re referring to this.
Judge: That’s correct.
Judge: Okay. I mean, all the—even at eBay or other places, other auctions, they disclose all the problems that the merchandise has. Can you—can you flip just—just if you flip this part? I just want to see how it looks on the other end. The receiving end of such a thing is that—
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
Here. That’s the first patch.
Judge: Yeah. I mean, there’s—you could—I’m looking at the picture, and I can tell that there is some fading in a line right here that is sort of separate and distinct from the kind of stuff I’m looking at, which is patched and holes.
Judge: What say you to that? Do you feel that when you advertise something, you need to say that it’s patched and that it has actual—
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
With all due respect—
Judge: Yeah, that usually—you know what we find as judges when people start a sentence with the phrase "With all due respect," we’re about to get disrespected.
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
That’s okay.
Judge: But I want you to come over here, though, because I do want you to see this first. I’d like to read first. I’d like you to come over here, please, if you don’t mind. I just want you to see what I’m talking about, rather than just hear what I’m talking about.
[Judge walks over to defendant]
Judge: You see that?
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
Yes. You’re on.
Judge: I mean, that—you know, the question is in a breach of contract case because essentially what this man is saying is that it’s a breach of contract because you advertised a rug and you don’t mention—can I—let me just get you up there a second. Come on.
Judge: And then you, of course, can see these that it’s been patched up, where those holes are, right?
Thomas Schwanky (Plaintiff):
Yeah.
Judge: And he feels that you folks should have to disclose if you’re selling something that’s been repaired, has a hole, and whatever else, that it’s a little less than forthright if you don’t, and that therefore it’s a breach of contract.
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
Number one, he registered in our auction, and people who register to bid in our auction are responsible for having read the terms and conditions of our sale.
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
Okay, we didn’t sell this in a store. It doesn’t come with a guarantee. All goods are sold as is, where is, with all faults, and all sales are final. All auction items are available for bidder examination. Bidding signifies that you have examined an item, either personally or through an authorized agent, or that you have elected not to examine an item. Buyer relies solely on his or her own expertise and judgment when bidding.
Judge: Okay. As noted above, all sales—do you have an off button? Press the off button.
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
Hand it to my bay. Hand it over to me.
Judge: Don’t—what were you going to do? Hit my bay? I do not suggest playing kis maacha with Douglas.
Judge: What say you to this?
Rosa Aona (Defendant):
Actually, I did see that the carpet on the computer. But any place that they—they have to say the damages. Any place that you buy anything—I want to see you. You’re online right now, right?
Judge: Like, I know. You just do it.
Judge: Anyway. I’m done with it.
PLAINTIFF: In this courtroom dialogue, Thomas Schwanky, representing Rosa Aona and Amir Carpets, is suing the defendant, Douglas, for $585, the purchase price of a rug that Douglas bought from Amir Carpets. Dialogue:
Judge: All parties, please raise your right hand. Welcome back to The People's Court. Next case on the docket: the plaintiff says that his landlord had it out for him because she didn't like his friend. But the defendant says the plaintiff and his friends smoked lots of pot and damaged the place. It's the case of "Smoking The Joint." Thank you. You're welcome.
Judge: Okay, Ricardo Torres, you are suing Karen Reyes for $650, the amount of your security deposit that you are saying she is wrongfully withholding. What happened?
Plaintiff: Around the month of September, I was looking to acquire a room, and I was hanging out with one of my friends, which so happens to be her neighbor. And, um, I see her bringing out the sign that says "Room for Rent," so I asked my friend if he knew Karen, and he said yes, that he would let her know that I was interested.
Judge: So you're interested, and you decide you're paying $450, and then what happens that you start paying $650?
Plaintiff: Um, I acquired, uh, she had a bigger room in the same apartment.
Judge: So then you're there for how long before the problem begins?
Plaintiff: I was there entirely for about seven months.
Judge: Okay, now at some point you decide to leave. Which is what month?
Plaintiff: March 30th.
Judge: March, okay. Now, when you leave, you tell her when? That look, I'm not staying.
Plaintiff: I told her about two weeks prior to that.
Judge: Because?
Plaintiff: And then she got mad because she felt like it wasn't enough notice.
Judge: Yes. All right. Do you have a lease?
Defendant: My lease is under my name.
Judge: Oh, do you have a lease with him, is what I meant, because you're a landlord to him.
Defendant: Yes. We didn't make a lease because he was just renting a room at that time.
Judge: I understand, but when you say your feeling is he gave me no notice, he gave me... okay, how much notice do you say he has to give you?
Defendant: Thirty days.
Judge: Says who? Says where? You don't have a lease. What are you talking about?
Defendant: One of the agreements when he rented the room.
Judge: Verbal? It was an agreement?
Defendant: Stop and listen. A verbal agreement or a written agreement?
Defendant: A verbal agreement.
Judge: Okay. Did you agree with her verbally that you would give her 30 days' notice before leaving?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: Okay. Now you have a problem with that. See, when there is no lease, written lease, there's still a lease. You can have a verbal lease, but the law fixes what the terms are, not you. If you want terms outside of that, then you have to get them in writing and get both sides to sign it. The law where you're from, in the Bronx, he doesn't have to give you 30 days' notice. I don't get it, but that's how it is. So, yeah, if you want 30 days' notice, it better be in writing somewhere. Is it in writing anywhere? In an email? A text? Anything?
Defendant: No.
Judge: No. All right. But according to you, there's more. When he leaves, he ends up leaving the place damaged. Was he a good tenant while he lived there?
Defendant: No. I actually had to always, um, let him know the rent was due on the first.
Judge: That was one thing that was verbally said, that was agreed on? When was it always due?
Defendant: The first.
Judge: It would be the first. It'll be the morning, and you have to ask him in the afternoon because you have to give the rent to the owner?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: So when you say he would pay late, you mean late on the first?
Defendant: Late on the first. Sometime the second.
Judge: Okay. Were there any other problems?
Defendant: Um, he used to smoke around the house. He used to do spray paint in the house.
Judge: He used to smoke... cigarettes?
Defendant: No.
Judge: Marijuana?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: Okay. Can you tell him to stop?
Defendant: I told him. That was one of the number one rules because I knew he was smoking, and that was one of the number one rules. I told him no smoking allowed in the house because I have a three-year-old asthmatic child. That was one of the rules. He said fine.
Judge: So why didn't you get rid of him when he was violating it? I was waiting. Why would you put your asthmatic child in that?
Defendant: To be completely honest, uh, overall, even though that was that one little problem, he told me he didn't smoke in the house. I was never around the house, to be honest. I was always working and in school.
Judge: What about your kid?
Defendant: He was in school.
Judge: So it was only basically the nights when he would be home?
Defendant: Every night when you're home, your kid can't breathe. No, never. Not every night. He couldn't breathe, obviously. He had his asthma machine. But, yes...
Judge: Where's the machine? Because this guy's smoking in the house. Why don't you just get rid of him?
Defendant: I was trying to tell him, you know, I asked him nicely.
Judge: So now, but according to you, he damaged the place?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: Let me see the pictures of the damage.
Defendant: Everything has been fixed. I don't have any pictures. Everything was fixed.
Judge: So can a landlord get a tenant’s case in 2017 for smoking pot? Yes. Okay. Well, lady, do me a favor. Pay the man his $650 and learn how to be a landlord. Verdict to the plaintiff. Thank you.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff, Ricardo Torres, is suing his landlord, Karen Reyes, for withholding his security deposit of $650. He initially rented a room from her for $450, but later moved into a larger room for $650. The Plaintiff claims that he gave her two weeks' notice before leaving in March, but Karen felt that this was insufficient notice.
DEFENDANT:
The Defendant, Karen Reyes, suggests that the Plaintiff, Ricardo Torres, was a poor tenant who violated the rules of the rental agreement, specifically by smoking marijuana in the house despite being aware of her three-year-old asthmatic child. She claims that Torres also damaged the property and did not give the required 30-day notice before leaving. The Defendant's goal or motive behind these statements seems to be to justify withholding the security deposit and to argue that Torres was a problematic tenant who caused damage to her property.

Dialogue:
JUDGE: All parties, please raise your right hands.
Welcome to The People's Court. This case involves the plaintiffs, who hired the defendant to cater their wedding at a historic museum. They claim the defendant knew the museum was closing but still took their deposit. The defendant, however, argues that the museum was not the only option and that he is still available to provide his services.
Plaintiff: s, you are suing Bernard’s catering business for $2,749.62, the amount of your deposit. You’ve requested that we not use the name of the business or Bernard’s last name.
JUDGE: Did you two get married already?
PLAINTIFF (CLAIRE JACKSON): No, not yet.
JUDGE: Alright. What happened?
PLAINTIFF (CLAIRE JACKSON): We got engaged in April of last year.
JUDGE: Congratulations!
PLAINTIFF (CLAIRE JACKSON): Thank you. When we started looking for a venue, one of the first places we thought of was the Dow Museum in St. Augustine, Florida. We had attended a wedding there six years ago and fell in love with the place. It’s charming, rustic, historic, and outdoors.
JUDGE: St. Augustine is a beautiful and historic city. Do you live there?
PLAINTIFF (IVAN DOE): We live in Jacksonville, about 45 minutes away.
JUDGE: My child is in fourth grade, so she’s taking a trip to St. Augustine soon. It’s part of the Florida history curriculum.
Go on.
PLAINTIFF (CLAIRE JACKSON): We contacted the Dow Museum, and they provided us with Bernard’s information. They said that if we wanted to rent the venue, we had to go through Bernard.
JUDGE: Because you are their exclusive caterer?
DEFENDANT (BERNARD): Yes, ma’am.
JUDGE: Tell me about the Dow Museum.
DEFENDANT (BERNARD): It’s a cluster of old historic houses with courtyards and gardens. The entire property is about a city block. The homes have been preserved, and you can rent the venue for weddings.
JUDGE: How old are the houses?
DEFENDANT (BERNARD): They were built in the 1700s.
JUDGE: Wow.
PLAINTIFF (CLAIRE JACKSON): We visited the museum with Bernard and his wife. They showed us where the ceremony and reception would be.
Two days later, after discussing it, Ivan and I decided to move forward. We asked for an estimate for the venue and catering.
JUDGE: So, you worked out the details, signed a contract stating the event would be at the Dow Museum with Bernard as the caterer, and you put down a deposit of $2,749.62. There’s no dispute about that?
DEFENDANT (BERNARD): That’s correct.
JUDGE: Then what happened?
PLAINTIFF (CLAIRE JACKSON): After New Year’s, we started ramping up our wedding planning. Our wedding date is October 20th of this year.
We went back to the Dow Museum one Saturday to take pictures and plan. We spoke with the ticket taker at the museum, explaining that we were getting married there and wanted to look around.
She got a concerned look on her face and asked who we were working with and when our wedding was.
When we told her, she said, “I don’t know if you’ve heard, but the Dow Museum is in the process of being sold to the Children’s Museum.” She told us that all weddings in May had already been canceled.
JUDGE: At that point, did you think your wedding was safe?
PLAINTIFF (CLAIRE JACKSON): We hoped so. We assumed no news was good news.
JUDGE: Sometimes, no news is just no news.
PLAINTIFF (CLAIRE JACKSON): Yes, we were optimistic. We immediately texted Bernard.
JUDGE: Bernard, in January, when she contacted you, did you know there was going to be a problem?
DEFENDANT (BERNARD): No, I hadn’t heard yet.
JUDGE: So, after she reached out, what did you do?
DEFENDANT (BERNARD): I left several messages for the new owners to find out what was happening. When I finally got a response, they told me they didn’t want any more weddings until after May 15th.
JUDGE: But Claire’s wedding is in October.
DEFENDANT (BERNARD): Yes, so I told them that I had a wedding booked in October and asked if we could work something out.
They said they might be under construction but would do their best to accommodate us.
JUDGE: They couldn’t guarantee there wouldn’t be construction during Claire’s wedding, could they?
DEFENDANT (BERNARD): No, they couldn’t.
JUDGE: So, why am I here?
DEFENDANT (BERNARD): I was trying to see if the wedding could still happen there.
JUDGE: If the museum was the whole reason for the plaintiffs booking with you, do they get their money back?
(To the audience) What do you think?
AUDIENCE MEMBER 1: Yes. They paid for it.
AUDIENCE MEMBER 2: Yes. They didn’t get what they paid for.
JUDGE: But the defendant is still offering his services, just not at the museum.
(To Bernard) You were their exclusive caterer for that venue. If the venue is no longer available, that changes everything, doesn’t it?
DEFENDANT (BERNARD): I’m still available to cater their event. I’ve shown them other venues.
JUDGE: But they chose the Dow Museum for their wedding. You were tied to that location. Since that location is no longer available, you need to return their deposit.
Judgment for the plaintiffs in the amount of $2,749.62.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff, Claire Jackson, and her fiance Ivan Doe, are suing Bernard's catering business for the deposit they paid for their wedding, which was to be held at the Dow Museum in St. Augustine, Florida. They had initially contacted the museum, who provided them with Bernard's information as their exclusive caterer.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant, Bernard, is a caterer who was hired by the Plaintiffs, Claire Jackson and Ivan Doe, to cater their wedding at the Dow Museum in St. Augustine, Florida. The Plaintiffs had chosen the Dow Museum as their venue due to its historical charm and had paid a deposit of $2,749.62 to Bernard.

Dialogue:
Judge: Okay, Electrical Control Systems, represented here by Mr. Prasad. You're the owner, yes?
Plaintiff: Yes, I was the owner.
Judge: The business is no longer in business?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: You are suing Edward Catalinus, your former landlord of 20 years, for $3,000, a statutory maximum that you say you are out because he won't return your $1,500 security deposit, and you want interest on it, plus the value of all the things that you left behind. Is that correct?
Plaintiff: Yes, that’s correct.
Judge: Okay, what happened?
Plaintiff: As stated before, I was a good tenant. I paid my rent on time every month for 20 years.
Judge: 20 years. Okay, what kind of business did you have?
Plaintiff: A computer repair business mostly.
Judge: Okay, the store was just accommodating, doing repairs for the locals.
Plaintiff: Yes, that's correct. I was there for 20 years, paying almost $375,000 during that period of time for rent and property tax adjustments.
Judge: Go on.
Plaintiff: Then it was time to go.
Judge: Why did you break down your business?
Plaintiff: Well, no, I mean, the lease ended, and I didn’t need the space anymore.
Judge: So you informed him in January that when the lease is over in May, it's over, and that you no longer need the space?
Plaintiff: Correct. And everything was fine until it wasn’t.
Judge: When was the first inkling you had that you were going to have a problem?
Plaintiff: Well, one month passed, so I contacted him, and he said, “Well, you know, he didn’t forget me, and he would take care of it." Now three months passed, and I received an email saying that I have no security deposit for you because he had to spend an amount that was more than what the worth of the security deposit was.
Judge: The security deposit was $1,500?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Okay, so once you got that response from him, what did you do?
Plaintiff: I was angry, right, because I felt that I had left the place in good condition, and I shouldn’t have to fund him making it spiffier for the next guy.
Judge: And therefore, you are suing for the $1,500 security deposit?
Plaintiff: Correct, and I want compound interest on it for those years.
Judge: Where did you get the idea that you could sue for interest on a business lease?
Plaintiff: From my attorney.
Judge: Really? That’s inaccurate. Time for a new attorney.
Defendant: All right, Mr. Catalinus, what’s going on?
Defendant: He’s a good tenant, paid on time for 20 years. A 20-year relationship. We have this issue over $1,500.
Judge: You have this problem.
Defendant: Well, we probably could have eliminated it. In one of my emails, I said, “Let’s sit down and talk about it, and I’ll go step-by-step so we could eliminate this.”
Judge: How were you planning to eliminate this?
Defendant: Well, this was a security interest on a commercial lease. You don’t have to give interest on a security deposit. I looked up the statute, and it said only on residential, not commercial. I would have gone step-by-step explaining this.
Judge: What happened?
Defendant: Mr. Prasad was leaving in early April, and his lease expired at the end of May. I called him and told him I wanted to do a walkthrough with him to make sure everything was in accordance with the lease surrender.
Judge: Okay.
Defendant: I went in with a contractor who had done a lot of work for me in that building, and we went through it with Mr. Prasad. We started upstairs, and there were two panels of sheetrock missing. Mr. Prasad said he did that because he removed the panels to get more heat and cooling upstairs. So we had to pay $275 to replace the sheetrock panels.
Judge: You have a receipt for that?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: What else was wrong?
Defendant: Apparently, Mr. Prasad had a client or a company give him some lighting, which he put in the store. There were nice fixtures in the front room, but in the back room, we had a hodgepodge of ceiling lights. One was made for a drop ceiling and was sticking down from the ceiling with exposed wires. Two other light fixtures had round circular fluorescent lights, one of which was held up by a piece of wire.
Judge: Do you have pictures of this?
Defendant: No, because I told him I was going to have to take them out, or he had to do it.
Judge: Why didn’t you just bring the lights back to the way they were?
Defendant: Those fixtures aren’t made anymore, and the ones that were there were 20 years old.
Judge: So why didn’t you just save the ones you took down?
Defendant: Because the fixtures that were there were already 20 years old. They don’t make those fixtures anymore.
Judge: But you could have put in new recess lighting?
Defendant: They were never wired to code. The building was never wired to code. Do you have evidence that it wasn’t wired to code when you were there?
Plaintiff: No, but I can show you one of the light fixtures that were there.
Judge: A picture of a light fixture is not going to tell me that the building wasn’t wired to code. What about the smoke alarm rewiring?
Defendant: Well, the plaintiff wired the smoke alarm into the building. When he left, he took it out, and that caused problems.
Judge: You can’t remove something like that when it’s part of the building.
Defendant: It was part of the building, and it caused issues. The monitoring system needed to be replaced because wires were cut and pieces were missing.
Judge: I agree with you on that. The alarm system became part of the building and needed to be maintained. So what happened when he left?
Defendant: When he left, the alarm system was damaged, and we had to replace it. The new tenant had to deal with it.
Judge: So you're keeping the security deposit because of these repairs?
Defendant: Yes. And because of the changes he made to the lighting and other fixtures.
Judge: Okay. Based on what I’ve heard, I find that the $1,500 security deposit has been used up in bringing the place back to code, and since this is a commercial lease, it doesn’t require compound interest. Therefore, my verdict in this case is for the defendant.
Defendant: Thank you, Your Honor.
Judge: All right, the plaintiff comes out with nothing.
Plaintiff: I think justice is not served. I don’t understand why I have to pay for an alarm system I own, and I have to pay to repair it, even though I left it in good condition.
Judge: Why didn’t you sit down with him to go over everything step by step?
Plaintiff: We tried, but he wasn’t interested. After 20 years, I thought he’d be reasonable.
Judge: If he would have read the lease in the beginning, none of this would have happened.
Plaintiff: Right. If he would have sat down and gone over everything with me, it could have been worked out.
Judge: How come you couldn’t get him to sit down and go over everything with you?
Plaintiff: I guess he didn’t have the time.
Judge: What’s a fixture and what’s not a fixture? In most states, it comes down to intent. Was it the intent of both parties to make something part of the building when the tenant leaves?   Judge: What happened with the smoke alarm rewiring?
Defendant: Because the plaintiff wired the smoke alarm to the building so tightly into the building, can the plaintiff remove the smoke alarm when the plaintiff leaves?
Plaintiff: What do you think?
Judge: If it affects the safety of the rest of the tenants in the building, then no. But if it doesn’t…
Defendant: Well, it clearly does. That’s what a smoke alarm is for.
Judge: But if it's in their—if it’s in their own apartment?
Defendant: Well no, it’s a commercial space.
Judge: So, at a point, does it become a fixture in the building, part of the building?
Defendant: I would think so, yes.
Judge: So tough luck. I mean, you put it in and you can use it for as long as you're there, but when you leave, you can't remove it. Right? That seems reasonable to me. Going inside the courtroom, there was no smoke alarm in the building. There were smoke detectors in the building, but at some later date, the town required a central monitoring system with a dial-up in case of a fire. He did put that in, along with a burglar alarm. But after the new tenant moved in, she went to hook up the monitoring system with a company, and they came in and said it had to be replaced because there was not functioning. Wires were cut, pieces were missing.
Judge: So then why would you be entitled to keep anything more than what it takes to remove it and put one in for your new tenant or tell your tenant to put her on?
Defendant: Well, because I looked at it as an addition to the building. He had to leave when he—and when he left, wires were cut, pieces were missing, and it shorted out the panel and it had to be replaced.
Judge: You have a surrender clause, which is typical in a lease, that says that you'll surrender the premises together with any alterations, additions, and improvements, including all plumbing fixtures and other similar installations which may have been made, except movable furniture, movable trade fixtures, carpeting, cabinetry, lighting fixtures purchased at the sole expense of the tenant.
Judge: Alright. I kind of agree with you. Once you make the alarm part of the place, it’s part of the place, and it needs to be in working order.
Judge: Based on what I have heard, I find that well over the $1,500 security deposit has been taken up with bringing the place back up to code. And this commercial lease doesn’t require compound interest on the security deposit. Therefore, my verdict in this case is for the defendant.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff, Mr. Prasad, is suing his former landlord, Edward Catalinus, for $3,000, which includes the $1,500 security deposit, interest on it, and the value of items left behind. He claims that he was a good tenant for 20 years, paying rent and property tax adjustments totaling almost $375,000.
DEFENDANT:
The Defendant, Edward Catalinus, is the former landlord of Electrical Control Systems, a computer repair business owned by the Plaintiff. The Plaintiff is suing Catalinus for $3,000, which includes the $1,500 security deposit, interest on it, and the value of items left behind. The Plaintiff claims that the Defendant has not returned the security deposit and has not compensated for the value of the items left behind.

The Defendant's goal or motive behind his statements suggests that he believes he is justified in keeping the security deposit due to the repairs and improvements made to the property during the Plaintiff's tenancy.

Dialogue:
Judge: If it's right next door, that's close -- that's close in distance. Maybe it would take a while to build the project.
And so that's a matter of the agency's judgment, is: But what should be taken into account?
Lawyer of Defendant: But that's not remote in time and place, right? I mean, that's -- that's close in place.
So is that --
Judge: Right.
No, I was -- I was responding to the point that sometimes an agency should take account of something that isn't directly -- it's not just the place, it's not just the ski lifts.
It's --
Lawyer of Defendant: I -- I agree.
I agree.
It just seems like that's not -- I mean, so I think, like, the 88 miles, just focusing on the track itself, might be too narrow a focus. But, if it's something kind of alongside either in your hypothetical, it's something that seems to me pretty close in space.
Judge: Right.
No, and that -- that -- that was my point about indirect effects.
Some -- it -- it's not the ski resort -- it's not the ski lifts itself. It's -- it's what -- what indirect consequences that will have by building the ski resort there or maybe a mile away.
So it's a question --
Judge: Right.
I guess what the Lawyer of Defendant is saying is, you know, it depends how you define the term "remote." But I wouldn't think of that as remote.
So I would think of that as passing Mr. Clement's test. Is there -- is there anything that you're worried about that's not, like, just around there but really is further away, that the agency should really take a look at because it could influence their decision-making?
Lawyer of Defendant: Well, I -- I think the Corps of Engineers, in issuing a permit for a deposit, is not just going to look at -- or shouldn't just look at the immediate place where there might be fill put in the river but -- but should be concerned if the -- if whatever pollutant is being added, what -- what's its effect downstream going to be.
Is it -- is it poisonous? Would it affect drinking water downstream?
Judge: That goes to the issue of the nature of what the impact is, correct?
Lawyer of Defendant: Yeah, yeah.
Judge: And some impacts can be more localized, and some impacts might not be.
Lawyer of Defendant: Right.
Judge: A smokestack might be blown because of the winds in a particular area to five states.
Lawyer of Defendant: Right.
Judge: In another, it might be blown to two.
And the same thing -- you just mentioned that if it's affecting the water, you can't just look at the little pond that's there; you have to look at where it travels when it leaves the pond, correct?
Lawyer of Defendant: Right.
Judge: And it could go a very far distance in some situations, correct?
Lawyer of Defendant: Right.
Judge: You can have downhill motion that could take something miles away.
Lawyer of Defendant: Yes.
Judge: So that's why a test that just speaks about the local impact is not enough if that's all you're saying.
Lawyer of Defendant: Right.
No, I think that's right.
And -- and Mr. Clement was concerned about context-specific analysis.
To some extent, that's inevitable, but I mean -- what I think we mean by context-specific is looking at the statute that the agency's acting on, what -- what is it supposed to be focusing on, and enabling the agency to focus on that and the particular decision that is being made. Sometimes that focus will be informed by the fact that another agency is also going to look at this.
Another agency will be concerned about the downstream effects.
Judge: Thank you, counsel. You obviously focused on a lot more -- I don't know whether they were adjectives or nouns, but particularly in your opening, to determine what exactly is available here, and then I think a lot of the questions were pressing along the same direction. In light of all that, I think the most important question is going to be whether or not the agency will entertain motions for extension of the 150-page limit.
Judge: How in the world -- how in the world -- putting aside whether -- the question for the people who have to -- and the agencies who have to fill this in, how in the world is somebody going to know how they should use a very, very limited, in terms of government work, page limit and -- and -- and, at each turn, knowing that failure to address a particular item could result in -- in the project being either delayed or -- or denied? And -- and yet then -- and you sort of gave yourself this safety valve: Well, in this case, it's very clear, and in this case, that. But, in other cases, you've left a lot of uncertainty both for the agency and the people appearing before it.
Lawyer of Defendant: Well, two responses to that, and there may be more. The first is that the page limit, I -- I think, should be a statutory affirmation that the agency shouldn't have to go to the ends of the earth to focus -- or to decide the environmental issue.
It should focus on what are the -- what are the core concerns, identifying what the core concerns are so that the agency will have that in mind, and then decide how deeply it needs to go into that.
So I think -- I think enabling -- as this Court's decisions have sought to do, enabling the agency to focus closely on what it regards as most material to its decision. The second point is -- is the other, I think, problem that comes up in judicial review is the fly-specking, which really isn't present here but is more present in the other aspect of this case in terms of downline effects, where the track is going through another state. Courts review these very intensely and said, oh, you should have done another study or you've got -- you've got this problem over here that you could have talked about more. And I think -- and it's not really present in this case, but I think -- I think the courts need to be reminded -- and, again, this comes in with the reasonableness review and Vermont Yankee and arbitrary and capricious -- need to be reminded that the agency gets to decide in the first instance, with great deference, how deeply it needs to go into something.
And if somebody comes in with a -- saying, well, you know, you didn't -- there's another study you could have looked at, at some point, this just has to be cut off, particularly given the page limits and given the -- what NEPA was about was focusing on the things that are most important, which may include, does include, some indirect effects.
Judge: Thank -- thank you, counsel. Justice Thomas? Justice Alito? Justice Sotomayor?
Judge: What do you understand to be the issue before us? There were a lot of decisions by the court below, at least five that I'm aware of.
One was on sending it back for the railroad to consider the additional oil production in the basin and the other that the railroad would spur oil refining in Texas and Louisiana.
And your brief was limited to those two issues.
Lawyer of Defendant: That's what we focused on because we didn't understand the question presented to cover the -- the downline effects.
Judge: Neither did I when I read the cert petition. But, having said that, because they don't mention the other things, the -- the -- the wildfires, the railroad accidents, or the Colorado River impact, you don't think those issues are before us?
Lawyer of Defendant: I -- I -- I don't think -- no, I don't think that they are before you.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff's statements suggest that they are arguing for a broad interpretation of the term "remote" in relation to the environmental impact of a project. They argue that the indirect effects of a project, such as downstream effects of pollutants or impacts on other agencies, should be taken into account when evaluating the environmental impact of a project. The Plaintiff is concerned about the limited page limit for the environmental impact statement, and they argue that the agency should be allowed to focus on the core concerns and not be required to go to great lengths to address every potential issue.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant's Lawyer is arguing that the agency should consider not only the immediate impact of a project but also its indirect effects, such as downstream pollution and potential impacts on other states. They are advocating for a context-specific analysis that focuses on the core concerns of the project and the statute the agency is acting on. The Lawyer is concerned about the page limit imposed on the agency's environmental impact statement, arguing that it should not require the agency to go to great lengths to address every potential issue, but rather focus on the most material concerns.

Dialogue:
Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket, the plaintiff says the defendant was a provocateur who slammed into her Nissan Maxima. But the defendant says the plaintiff was being overly aggressive because she felt the defendant was simply driving too slow. It’s the case of grody to the maximum. Thank you, Douglas. Delina Claxton?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: You are suing Andrew Taylor for $2,781.91 in damages to your car in a car accident you had with him that you say was his fault, correct?
Plaintiff: Yes, sir.
Judge: What happened? Come on over here to the board and explain it to me. Pick a car, any car.
Plaintiff: Okay, I’m coming up.
Judge: Lexington—no, this is the defendant’s car. Okay, so you’ll be the blue car.
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Now, there are two lanes on Lexington going in one direction?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Okay, so let’s see what’s going on. Go ahead.
Plaintiff: So I got up here, and we were both at the stop sign to see if any traffic was coming from Grand Avenue. There wasn’t, because there was a stop sign. So I proceeded. He said something to me, and I didn’t pay much attention because I was on my way.
Judge: Wait, wait. What did he say to you?
Plaintiff: I really don’t know.
Judge: Had there been a problem? Was there an issue before that between you two?
Plaintiff: Well, not really. He was coming up the block in front of me, and there were people behind the block behind me honking their horns. So I honked my horn as well.
Judge: Ah, okay, so the plot thickens. As you’re driving down Lexington, you’re beeping the horn at him because what? He’s going too slowly?
Plaintiff: Yes. He was with his girlfriend, and they were chatting and taking their time.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: So the person behind me honked their horn, thinking that I was slowing up the traffic. So I honked my horn at him because he was going so slow. When there was any chance for me to pass, I passed him because I knew I—
Judge: So now you’re both at the stop sign. He says something to you, which is probably not nice, but you don’t know what it is?
Plaintiff: I didn’t know what it was.
Judge: And then?
Plaintiff: I waited to see if any traffic was coming. I started to make my turn. When I made my turn here, he came behind, made his turn, and smacked me right here.
Judge: So you hit the front of his car or the back of his car?
Plaintiff: The back of his car.
Judge: Is Grand Avenue one way also?
Plaintiff: Yes, it’s a one-way also.
Judge: Come back to the lectern. Let me hear your version. Come on over.
Defendant: Do you want my whole story?
Judge: Yes, I want your whole story. Start from when you were born.
Defendant: First of all, Lexington is a one-lane street. It’s broad, but there’s no marking down the middle.
Judge: Were cars beeping at you? Agitated cars?
Defendant: The plaintiff was beeping at me.
Judge: Was there another car behind her?
Defendant: I don’t remember. I don’t think so.
Judge: Was there somebody else who passed you angrily?
Defendant: There was someone who turned at the previous intersection.
Judge: Did that person seem frustrated?
Defendant: They seemed like they were in a rush.
Judge: Okay, so what happens?
Defendant: Once we were up at the stop sign, I pulled up first. Ms. Claxton passed me in empty parking spaces and pulled up right after me. I drive a 15-passenger van, and from where she was, she couldn’t see if there was any traffic coming. I stopped at the intersection first. I don’t recall shouting anything out the window.
Judge: Did you say anything?
Defendant: I don’t believe I did.
Judge: Did he say anything?
Plaintiff: The windows were up.
Judge: But sometimes you can say something without making your words heard.
Defendant: I think we were like, “What?” because she was trying to pass in parking spaces.
Judge: Okay, so go on.
Defendant: I pulled up to the stop sign first. I looked to my right and saw that there were no cars coming. I made as wide a turn as I possibly could—again, I’m in a large vehicle. I turned, I was about 80% complete with my turn, and at that point, Ms. Claxton attempted to squeeze between a parked car and me. She couldn’t make it and ended up driving into the left rear wheel well of my car.
Judge: Who has pictures? You want to step back? Okay. Let me ask you a question: after you hit each other, what does everybody say?
Defendant: I got out and attempted to exchange information.
Judge: What’s the first thing you say to each other?
Defendant: I said, "Don’t say anything. Let’s exchange information, call the police, and that’s it."
Plaintiff: He wasn’t doing anything! He didn’t even call the cops!
Judge: I find the accident was your fault. I find in favor of the defendant.
Plaintiff: Ridiculous!
Judge: Case dismissed.
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff, Delina Claxton, is suing Andrew Taylor for $2,781.91 in damages to her Nissan Maxima, alleging that he was a provocateur who intentionally rammed into her car during a car accident. However, her statements suggest that the accident occurred due to a series of events that escalated from a minor traffic dispute.

The Plaintiff claims that she honked her horn at the Defendant because he was driving too slowly and taking too long to make a turn, as he was with his girlfriend. The Defendant, on the other hand, states that he was driving a 15-passenger van and could not see if there was any traffic coming due to his vehicle's size.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant's statements suggest that he was driving a 15-passenger van on a one-lane street, Lexington, and was waiting at a stop sign. The Plaintiff, who was behind him, honked her horn to express frustration over his slow driving. The Defendant claims he stopped at the intersection first, made a turn, and the Plaintiff attempted to squeeze between a parked car and his vehicle, causing an accident.
Techniques_Used: playing the victim, emotional appeal, framing the narrative
Techniques_LLM: playing the victim, emotional appeal, framing the narrative

Dialogue:
JUDGE: Plaintiff, you are suing the defendant for $220, which you claim he still owes you for the return of a car he sold you. Tell me what’s going on.
PLAINTIFF: Well, the defendant and I used to work together. He sold me a car on Friday. As soon as I took the car home, the brakes went out.
JUDGE: What kind of car?
PLAINTIFF: A 1998 Pontiac Bonneville.
JUDGE: A 1998? That car is almost old enough to drink!
PLAINTIFF: Yeah. As soon as I took it home, the car started leaking brake fluid everywhere, and I had no brakes. I called the defendant that night and let him know. I felt like he already knew something was wrong because there was a bottle of brake fluid in the back seat.
JUDGE: Did he ever tell you the car needed brake fluid or had issues?
PLAINTIFF: No.
JUDGE: But did you really think a car that old wouldn’t have some problems? Did you have it inspected by a mechanic?
PLAINTIFF: I didn’t mind fixing some things, but I didn’t expect it to have brake problems.
JUDGE: Why not? A 21-year-old car is bound to have issues. You didn’t even check how much it would cost to fix before demanding your money back?
PLAINTIFF: I didn’t even get a chance! I called him right away.
JUDGE: Defendant, did he call you the same day he bought it?
DEFENDANT: Yes.
JUDGE: And you agreed to take the car back?
DEFENDANT: Yes, I took it back and sold it to another coworker.
JUDGE: Another coworker? Because that worked so well the first time?
DEFENDANT: Well, I offered to fix the brakes for him, but he didn’t want that. So, I sold it to someone else.
JUDGE: How much did you sell it for?
DEFENDANT: $500.
JUDGE: Did that person pay you fully?
DEFENDANT: Yes.
JUDGE: Plaintiff, you claim the defendant did not fully reimburse you?
PLAINTIFF: That’s right. He only paid me $380.
JUDGE: Defendant, what do you say to that?
DEFENDANT: I paid him three different times—$300, $200, and $100. The first payment was made in front of someone. The last $100 was given at work in front of a supervisor.
JUDGE: Do you have that supervisor here to testify?
DEFENDANT: No.
JUDGE: Do you have a written statement from them?
DEFENDANT: No.
JUDGE: Do you have receipts proving you made those payments?
DEFENDANT: No.
JUDGE: Plaintiff, do you have any texts proving he still owed you money?
PLAINTIFF: Yes.
JUDGE: Let me see those texts.
PLAINTIFF: (Hands over phone)
JUDGE: (Reading messages) On September 28, you texted, "Yo, defendant, I’ll see you at work. Since you don’t want to answer, I want my money. Can’t sell a car with no brakes. Have my money, or I’ll have your car." Then on September 30, you wrote, "Defendant, what are you going to do? I don’t have time to play games. If I don’t hear from you today, I’m leaving your car at work." And then on October 1st, "When are you going to start giving me my money? If I don’t have it by Monday, I’m filing a lawsuit."
Finally, on October 19, defendant, you responded, "What money?"
Defendant: , you never texted him back saying, "I already paid you everything." You just ignored him.
DEFENDANT: …
JUDGE: Defendant, the burden is on you to prove payment. You have no receipts, no witness statements, and no proof that you paid the full amount. Judgment for the plaintiff in the amount of $220.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff's statements suggest that they purchased a 1998 Pontiac Bonneville from the Defendant, but the car had immediate brake issues after purchase. The Plaintiff claims the Defendant was aware of the issue as there was a bottle of brake fluid in the back seat. The Plaintiff demanded their money back, but the Defendant only paid them $380 instead of the full amount, which the Plaintiff claims is $220.
DEFENDANT:
The Defendant's statements suggest that he sold a 1998 Pontiac Bonneville to the Plaintiff, who experienced brake problems with the car immediately after purchase. The Defendant acknowledges that he took the car back and sold it to another coworker for $500, but the Defendant claims to have made three separate payments to the Plaintiff totaling $300, $200, and $100. However, the Defendant does not have any concrete evidence (such as receipts, witness statements, or texts) to prove these payments were made.

The Defendant's goal or motive behind their words appears to be to defend themselves against the Plaintiff's claim that they still owe $220 for the car.

Dialogue:
Judge: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: In Robinson, this Court held that the government cannot criminalize status. And Respondent has conceded here today that the City cannot criminalize the status of being homeless. Our narrow submission in this case is that government cannot circumvent the principle of Robinson by making it unlawful for a person to reside in the jurisdiction if he has that status. That is what the ordinances here do. As applied to someone who has nowhere else to sleep, which is an essential human function, the ordinances are the equivalent of making it a crime to be homeless while living in Grants Pass.
Although we think the lower court was right to recognize that the core principle of Robinson is implicated in this case, the Court was wrong to award broad injunctive relief in the circumstances and manner in which it did. The Robinson principle requires an individualized determination, and the lower court's failure to require such a determination and its issuance of much broader injunctive relief has led to the problems at issue that the Petitioner and its amici have raised, not the core principle of Robinson.
And, therefore, we urge the Court to adhere to the core principle of Robinson but to emphasize that cities have flexibility to implement these and, in particular, time, place, and manner restrictions on where someone can sleep are entirely valid if they are reasonable, and, indeed, the state law establishes a state policy that time, manner, and place restrictions are the way to go if they are reasonable.
I welcome the Court's questions.
Judge: Wouldn't you have a better argument if Robinson involved someone being arrested for using drugs, but then the Court said that you were in effect arresting him for the status of a drug user because he had no choice but to use drugs because he's an addict?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: No. Our position is not that the conduct as in Robinson, the drug addict can't stop from using drugs. That is not our position. That's a question of personal culpability on the basis of what the substances make up—
Judge: So what's the difference between that and camping out? What you're saying here, it seems as though you're saying, well, there’s no other choice, so you have to camp out. Therefore, you're really arresting this person for the status of homelessness.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yes, but not because of an involuntary compulsion sense. I think, as another judge pointed out, the nexus here is actually closer than in the addiction situation because sleeping outside is essentially the mirror image or the other side of the coin or the definition—
Judge: Well, I agree that the distinction between status and conduct is a slippery one and that they're often closely related. In Robinson, though, the Court said you cannot make the status of being a drug addict a crime, but you can criminalize the conduct, even if it is involuntary and compulsive. And another case reaffirmed that line very strongly and said we're not going to go further.
And I wonder whether the government is asking us to take that step that the previous case counseled against by saying that it is effectively status, and this is throughout your brief. You use the word "effective" or "essentially" or "tantamount to," those kinds of words. And so I just wanted to get your response to that concern.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: No, we are not asking the Court to take the step that it declined to take in that previous case, which had to do with personal responsibility—
Judge: Okay. If you're—
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: —in this Court's decision.
Judge: —not asking us to do that, then I just want to circle back to what was asked earlier, which is, surely, the government wants to continue to enforce the drug laws and all kinds of other laws that people could make an argument that they had an involuntary need to do, a necessity defense. You don’t want us to wipe out all those laws?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Absolutely not, but what is different here is that the conduct in that previous case would have been based on the person's own separate—
Judge: Well—
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: —antisocial conduct. Here—
Judge: Well, another case made clear that some people are going to be forced to drink in public because they don't have a home.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yes.
Judge: He made this very point.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: No, we don’t—but the point here, it is the government that is prohibiting the alternative. It’s not the individual’s inability to control his own conduct. The government, because the person—because of other circumstances, the lack of money, the lack of a friend to stay with, the lack of shelter space, there is no place—we take as a given in our position that there is no other place for the person to sleep—
Judge: And I think, wouldn’t—
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: —and as the government—
Judge: —couldn’t a drug addict, though, make the exact same argument? "I had no other choice."
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: But that is—that is—that—the other choice would be a matter of—of personal—
Judge: No. Say the record says—
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: —understanding, personal culpability.
Judge: But the record says that there is no other choice. "I had to do it."
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Well, I—I do think that engaging in conduct that is unrelated to—let me take that back. The sleeping outside when you have no other place to go is the definition of homelessness.
Judge: Mr. Lawyer, isn’t the response—
Judge: But Judge—
Judge: —also that those two things are different? I mean, you're sort of saying it’s about individual culpability. But it’s not as though everyone engages in drug use.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Right.
Judge: Right? Certain people do, and maybe they have addiction, and maybe you can’t punish them because of the addiction, but you can still punish them as criminally culpable for engaging in the act. It seems to me we are in a totally different category—
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: We are, yes.
Judge: —when you’re talking about acts that everybody participates in, that no one thinks in and of themselves are criminally culpable. And yet somehow this statute is reaching out to punish certain people who engage in that universal human basic need. That seems to me to be the distinction—
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yes.
Judge: —in these situations.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: That is a critical distinction, and not only is it something that everybody engages in, but it's something that everybody has to engage in to be alive.
So, if you can’t sleep, you can’t live, and, therefore, by prohibiting sleeping, the city is basically saying you cannot live in Grants Pass. It’s the equivalent of banishment, which is something that is unknown to the way—
Judge: Mr. Lawyer—
Judge: Mr. Lawyer—
Judge: Wasn't Grant Pass's first attempt, policy choice, to put homeless people on buses so they would leave the city? I understood that to be the history of Grant Pass. Police officers would buy them a bus ticket, send them out of the city, but that didn’t work because people came back because it had been their home, correct?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: They came back.
Judge: They came back.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: I think they might have been sent back by the—
Judge: So then they passed this law. And didn't the city council president say, "Our intent is to make it so uncomfortable here that they'll move down the road," meaning out of town, correct?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: That statement was made at a public meeting of the city council.
Judge: All right.
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff's argument in this court case revolves around the contention that the City of Grants Pass is circumventing the principle established in the Robinson case by making it unlawful for a person to reside in the jurisdiction if they are homeless. The Plaintiff argues that the City's ordinances, which prohibit sleeping in public places, effectively criminalize homelessness as a status, as there is no other place for some individuals to sleep.

The Plaintiff acknowledges that the distinction between status and conduct can be slippery, but they argue that the nexus in this case is closer to the Robinson case because sleeping outside is the definition of homelessness. They emphasize that the government is prohibiting the alternative for homeless individuals, and it is not the individual's inability to control their own conduct that leads to their homelessness.

The Plaintiff's goal is to persuade the court to adhere to the core principle of the Robinson case, which states that the government cannot criminalize the status of being homeless.
DEFENDANT:
The Defendant's argument in this courtroom conversation revolves around the city's ordinances that make it unlawful for a person to reside in Grants Pass if they are homeless. The Defendant argues that these ordinances, as applied to someone who has nowhere else to sleep, effectively criminalize homelessness by making it a crime to be homeless while living in Grants Pass.

The Defendant's goal is to establish that the city's ordinances violate the core principle of the Robinson case, which states that the government cannot criminalize the status of being homeless. The Defendant contends that the city's ordinances are akin to making it a crime to be homeless while living in Grants Pass, as there is no other place for the homeless person to sleep.

The Defendant's motive is to emphasize that cities have flexibility to implement time, place, and manner restrictions on where someone can sleep, as long as they are reasonable.

Dialogue:
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Well, no --I think that you have to dig a little deeper than that, Justice Thomas.
It's not mere foreign ownership and it's certainly -- the broadcast cases I'll get to in a moment.
But it's foreign ownership because of a particular perspective.
If you boil it down to an essence, the owner of a print media or online media publication is -- is the essence of the viewpoint of that publication.
The current owner of X or the current owner of Fox News or the current owner of MSNBC has a particular perspective.
And working with that particular platform is shot through with the ownership from top to bottom.
Judge: But why couldn't Congress prohibit Americans from associating with certain foreign organizations that have interests that are hostile to the United States? I mean, I thought that's what Holder versus Humanitarian Law Project allowed, so I don't really understand what you mean.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Right.
So I'm glad you're bringing that up.
Judge: Yes.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: So, when it comes to national security, you are right that Congress can prohibit Americans, to use that case as an example, from associating with terrorist organizations or other organizations that pose a clear and present danger to this country. This case, Justice Jackson, is fundamentally different.
What the government tells you in its own brief that it is worried about here are the ideas that might be expressed on TikTok.
We might undermine U.S. leadership. We might sow doubts about democracy.
We might have pro-China views. And so, if you look to whether that is a legitimate interest, my fundamental submission -- and this, I think, goes to the last colloquy you were having with Mr. Francisco -- is that is an impermissible government interest.
And you look throughout our history and tradition, and I think the place I would point you most directly would be the opinions of Justice Brandeis in Whitney and Justice Holmes in Abrams --
Judge: I guess I don't understand how that's distinguishable from what's happening in Holder, and --and so can you just say a little bit more?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: It's --it goes to the nature of the national security threat.
So my position is the government just doesn't get to come in and say national security and the case is over or you don't get to associate.
You have to dig underneath what is the national security claim.
And what Justice Holmes said in his Abrams dissent -- and I know that was a dissent, these are hard issues, but that has been vindicated over time --is that it's not enough to say national security.
You have to say what is the real harm.
Is it -- you know, is it terrorism? Is it where --where our battleships are located?
Judge: But Justice Kavanaugh --
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Is it war?
Judge: -- Justice Kavanaugh presented a number of potential risks, right, with --with foreign adversaries using covert manipulation of the data platforms that are being used by youths today that would then make it more likely that people would turn into spies and do terrible things to the United States. This is a hypothetical, but --
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Yeah.
Judge: -- you know what I'm saying?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: I -- I get it.
So I think, if I understood Justice Kavanaugh correctly, he was talking about the data security arguments.
Let me just pull these apart.
You first have an argument -- and the government itself separates these two arguments in its brief.
The first argument and the one I'm focusing on initially is the content manipulation argument, and that argument is that our national security is implicated if the content on TikTok is anti-democracy, undermines trust in our leaders.
They use --they use various phrases like that in their brief.
So my primary submission is that is an impermissible government interest that taints the entire Act.
Now there's a secondary argument the government makes, and we say you don't even get to that because, once you have an impermissible motive like that, the law is unconstitutional.
But, even if you could get to that, Justice Jackson, I do grant that data security in -- in the way Justice Kavanaugh spelled it out is compelling.
That is compelling.
But that's not the question.
You just don't ask in the air, you know, was Congress worried about data security or could it reasonably worry --be worried about data security? You say, can this Act, the Act before you, be sustained on data security grounds? And our answer to that has to be no. You don't have to look any further than the divest -- the -- the divestiture provision itself, which says that the content recommendation algorithm cannot be used in the future.
Well, that has nothing to do with data security.
So the core feature of the divestiture provision is going at content manipulation, which I say is impermissible.
You can't -- you can't uphold that under data security grounds.
And the rest of the Act, when you look at the covered companies provision, Justice Jackson, if this were primarily a data security law, what you think you'd find is, what kind of data is procured? How is it stored? Is it shared? Those are the things you think you'd find under covered companies.
But you don't find that.

Judge: But I'm just saying, you know, when we're trying to figure out what a statement communicates, I guess -- I mean, I guess I agree with Justice Alito about how communication works.
Judge: If we're asking what a statement communicated, and Williams tells us there has to be some sort of statement, there's an assertion -- if you ask what that statement communicated, you have to have some basic understanding of how people use English.
Judge: That's how the jury is going to decide would a reasonable person have found the statement to be false.
Judge: So, I mean, I agree you don't look at any kind of idiosyncratic understanding, maybe, of the person on the other side of the table, but, I mean, you do have to have some kind of understanding of how normal people would understand this in the context of the situation, correct?
Defendant: Lawyer: Just -- Justice Barrett, I disagree with that.
Defendant: Lawyer: And -- and -- and -- and so did this Court in the Bronston case.
Defendant: Lawyer: In the Bronston case, it was absolutely clear what the petitioner -- what the questioner was driving at.

Defendant: Lawyer: And the answerer said: Well, my company did.
Defendant: Lawyer: And it wasn't pursued.
Defendant: Lawyer: And so the situation is very analogous.
Defendant: Lawyer: If -- if Bronston's right, then we can't look at what the -- the perception of the listener was.
Defendant: Lawyer: We have to look at only the context of the question, the answer, and the objective facts.
Judge: Justice Jackson?
Judge: So I guess I don't understand how, on remand, the Seventh Circuit could make the kinds of determinations that you said that they could make in response to Justice -- to Justice Gorsuch, and the reason is because we had a trial in this case.
Judge: We had a trial in which, presumably, those very same arguments about what, you know, the statement meant to your client, what the bank examiner said, et cetera, et cetera, were evidence that was presented to a jury that was then instructed that they were supposed to make a determination about whether his statement was false, right?
Defendant: Lawyer: That's correct, Your Honor.

Judge: This court is now in session. We are here today to hear the case of the Plaintiff versus the Defendant. Plaintiff, you may begin with your opening statement.
Plaintiff: Your Honor, the Defendant has continuously breached our contract, failing to meet agreed-upon deadlines and disregarding the terms we both signed. This has led to significant financial losses on our end. We seek fair compensation for these damages.
Judge: Defendant, how do you respond to these allegations?
Defendant: Your Honor, I strongly disagree with the Plaintiff's claims. The delays were due to unforeseen circumstances beyond my control. I had communicated these issues, but the Plaintiff refused to cooperate or offer any flexibility.
Judge: Plaintiff, do you have evidence supporting your claim that the Defendant failed to meet the contractual obligations?
Plaintiff: Yes, Your Honor. Here are emails and documented conversations where we repeatedly reminded the Defendant of the deadlines. We also have financial records showing our losses due to these delays.
Judge: Defendant, do you have any evidence to counter the Plaintiff’s claims?
Defendant: Yes, Your Honor. I have records of my communications, including requests for extensions and explanations for delays. Additionally, I have expert testimony attesting to the industry-standard delays that were beyond my control.
Judge: I see. I will review the submitted evidence carefully. Plaintiff, were there any attempts at mediation before filing this lawsuit?
Plaintiff: We attempted to reach a resolution through discussions, but the Defendant was uncooperative and unwilling to compromise.
Defendant: That is not entirely true, Your Honor. I offered alternative solutions, but the Plaintiff insisted on rigid terms.
Judge: Very well. I will now hear closing statements. Plaintiff?
Plaintiff: Your Honor, the Defendant’s negligence has cost us greatly. We upheld our end of the contract and expected the same in return. We respectfully request fair compensation.
Defendant: Your Honor, I have done my best to fulfill the contract under difficult circumstances. I believe the Plaintiff is being unreasonable in their demands, and I ask the court to consider the full context.
Judge: I have heard both sides and reviewed the presented evidence. Based on the contractual terms, the nature of the delays, and the communication between the parties, I rule in favor of the Plaintiff. The Defendant will be required to compensate the Plaintiff for financial damages incurred due to the delays.
Plaintiff: Thank you, Your Honor.
Defendant: I respectfully disagree but will comply with the court’s decision.
Judge: This case is now closed.
