I said, “Yo, you don’t got to lie to me, man. Take your whole vest.”
JUDGE: He was going to take your center patch for burning out your tire?
PLAINTIFF: Yeah. I paid $125 for that tire, and he was telling me not to burn out. So, I just gave him the whole vest.
JUDGE: So, suffice it to say, you gave him the vest and broke away from his club?
PLAINTIFF: Right. He should have just come to me like a man, said, “Why did you talk to my wife like that?” Instead, he tried to put it on me burning out the tire, which was never the case. Anyway, long story short, we made up.
JUDGE: You joined another club?
PLAINTIFF: Right. We made up, became friends again. This was two years later. We had a club meeting, and I told them about having a Halloween party, a Monster Ball.
JUDGE: You wanted to have it at his club?
PLAINTIFF: Yeah. We don’t have a clubhouse. They have a house.
JUDGE: Did you sign anything?
PLAINTIFF: No. I came back in a couple of days with the flyer and showed it to him.
JUDGE: Why didn’t you secure the place first? Did you put a deposit?
PLAINTIFF: His word was our word.
JUDGE: Tell me about the club. Where is it?
DEFENDANT: It’s located in Rochester, New York.
JUDGE: What kind of place is it? Warehouse district? Residential area?
DEFENDANT: Warehouse district.
JUDGE: Do you have a liquor license?
DEFENDANT: No.
JUDGE: So, people just bring what they bring and share?
DEFENDANT: No.
JUDGE: That’s what’s supposed to happen?
DEFENDANT: No.
JUDGE: And this is only used as a clubhouse for motorcycle clubs?
DEFENDANT: Correct.
JUDGE: So, what happened?
PLAINTIFF: I decided to have the Monster Ball and talked to him about it. I gave him $50 when I got the flyers done. I visited every other weekend and partied with them. I also put up flyers to let people know about the event.
JUDGE: Flyers? It sounds like you’re inviting more than just people in your club or people you know. Invitations are for people you know. Flyers attract strangers.
PLAINTIFF: That was the first thing discussed. This had nothing to do with my club. This was all on me. He was in agreement with this.
JUDGE: How much were you going to pay him?
PLAINTIFF: $100.
JUDGE: Why would he allow strangers on his site for just $100? Liability issues, injuries—why take that risk?
PLAINTIFF: He was taking the liquor sales. I was taking the door money.
JUDGE: You’re taking the door, he’s taking the liquor?
PLAINTIFF: Right.
JUDGE: How many people did you expect?
PLAINTIFF: At least a thousand.
JUDGE: Have you ever thrown a party with that many people?
PLAINTIFF: Yes, multiple times. This is what I do.
JUDGE: When did you first learn something had gone wrong?
PLAINTIFF: Four days before the party, he told me the police shut him down. He refused to give my money back and just left.
JUDGE: What money did you expect back?
PLAINTIFF: At least my deposit. I spent a lot on decorations, including a six-foot-high ghost that was supposed to hang inside. I even brought my niece to help decorate.
DEFENDANT: We only talked about the party. No deposit was made, no contract was signed.
JUDGE: Do you have a receipt for the $100?
PLAINTIFF: No, but I paid him $50 on two separate occasions.
DEFENDANT: That’s not true. If it were only $100, I would have returned it, but no money was exchanged.
JUDGE: The police gang unit visited you?
DEFENDANT: Yes. They said I either cancel the party or they shut me down.
JUDGE: You’re saying he spread the word about the party?
DEFENDANT: He posted flyers all over downtown, including the bus station.
PLAINTIFF: No, I only put them up in front of local places.
JUDGE: So, what happened to the party?
PLAINTIFF: It was moved elsewhere, but not to my house. I was at the venue that night waiting to see if they’d open the doors.
JUDGE: You already knew the party was canceled. Why were you there?
PLAINTIFF: To see if they were playing games with me.
JUDGE: Here’s what I think happened. You both knew this was a risk. You knew the club didn’t have a liquor license. You both thought you could fly under the radar. When the police shut it down, you lost your investment, but that was a risk you took. However, I do believe the $100 was paid, so I am awarding the plaintiff $100. Judgment for the plaintiff in that amount.

Dialogue:
Judge: Welcome back to The People's Court. Next case in the docket: the plaintiff bought a car, turns out the defendant, he says, didn’t disclose the car was in a bad accident and had airbag problems. But the defendant says the plaintiff went in with eyes wide open. It’s the case of a crash course in buying cars.
Judge: Okay, John, you are suing Alexander and Mark, and your mom—your wife—who not only doesn’t want her last name mentioned, she doesn’t want to be here and gave you power of attorney. All right, you're suing that side for $3,472.38 that you say you are due from them for repairs for a car they sold you that apparently had more problems than you bargained for. Tell me what happened.
Plaintiff: Correct. I located the ad on eBay. I liked the car. I contacted the seller.
Judge: Who was the seller?
Plaintiff: Uh, Alex.
Judge: Okay. Alex is the son?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Okay, and was your name the only name on the car? Were all three names on the car?
Defendant: It was my dad’s name on the car.
Judge: Just the dad’s name on the car. Okay, go on.
Plaintiff: We discussed the purchase of the car. We discussed the lien amount on the car, and if I liked the car, I'd come down with the check to satisfy the lien and I would drive it home the same day.
Judge: Okay. How much is the lien?
Plaintiff: Fifteen thousand and change.
Judge: Okay, so you sold the car to him for fifteen thousand?
Defendant: Sixteen five, I believe it was.
Judge: That sound right?
Plaintiff: That’s about right.
Judge: Okay, so go on.
Plaintiff: I immediately noticed some things that weren’t mentioned in the eBay ad. Bald tires in the back, the shifter was worn out.
Judge: This is before you bought it?
Plaintiff: Right, before I bought it. So I noticed a couple of things when I looked at the car, test drove it for a short test drive, and I liked the car. So we negotiated a price and we settled on a price.
Judge: Okay, and you buy?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Okay, did you have it seen by a mechanic?
Plaintiff: No, I looked at it myself.
Judge: Okay. Are you a mechanic?
Plaintiff: I’ve been working on cars for a long time.
Judge: Okay. All right, so then what happens?
Plaintiff: Uh, well, during the course of my looking at the car, Alex did tell me that the car was in an accident. It was in the front passenger side. And so I paid specific attention to that and looked, checked it over. The car looked like it was in pretty good shape.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: And so I settled and bought the car. Drove it home.
Judge: So why are you suing them?
Plaintiff: Lots of problems. On the drive home, the check engine light came on. That was the first thing. Well, actually, when I looked at the car, the airbag light was on. I asked Alex what was going on with the airbag light. He told me that he brought it to a shop and they couldn’t tell what was wrong with it. I had to take it to the dealer. And since the car had an extended warranty, I assumed that the car—whatever damage to the airbag system or malfunctioning—would be covered under warranty.
Judge: Did you check that before you bought the car?
Plaintiff: No, I didn’t.
Judge: Okay.
Judge: I have reviewed all the facts of this case, and based on the evidence presented, I find in favor of the defendants. This was an as-is sale, and there is no proof of deliberate deception. The plaintiff assumed certain things about the vehicle and did not conduct a full inspection before purchase. That is not on the defendants.
Plaintiff: But Your Honor, I believe the insurance company should have caught this issue. I tried to reach out to Alex to contact Geico, but I never got a response.
Judge: That may be an issue with your insurance company, but it does not place liability on the defendants. They did not conceal information, and they disclosed the previous accident. You even saw the airbag light on before you bought the car. Verdict for the defendants.

All parties, please raise your right hand.
Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket: the plaintiff bought a car from the defendant, and it was a piece of junk. But the defendant says the plaintiff got swindled by a private inspection center and that the car wouldn’t pass inspection. Baloney!

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: This is after you buy the car?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: Not before you buy the car?

Judge: Do you have the paperwork from the sale?
Plaintiff: Yes, I do.
Judge: Let me see.
Plaintiff: (Hands over paperwork)
Judge: Go on.
Plaintiff: So I called Honda, and when I went there and then they did an inspection on the car, they said the car had so much problem. So I go back there, and I explain to her. She said, “Well, those places always tell you the car has a million issues because they want you to come back and fix it.”

Defendant: Yes, Your Honor. There was a six-month warranty, plus we gave her a free five-year maintenance contract.

Defendant: Three days later.
Judge: Okay. What does she say?
Defendant: She says that there’s all this wrong with the car. She showed us an inspection report from another dealer, just like us. We can do the same thing. It’s not the inspection report from motor vehicles. We are obligated to fix anything that motor vehicle says. But in spite of that, we told her that we would fix whatever needed to be fixed once she had the inspection done at motor vehicle, which she still had time to do while waiting for her plates.
Judge: Okay, so now you bring the car back to them, and you, according to you, say to them, “I’d like you to fix it.” And they say no?
Plaintiff: Because she said she’s not going to fix it because she knows that there’s nothing wrong and the car already passed inspection in New Jersey state.
Judge: Can you stand up if you’re going to talk? Who’s this person?
Defendant: Oh, uh, Lea. She’s the owner of the dealership.

Defendant: Because, um, she speaks—She’s more comfortable in Spanish. That’s her first language.
Judge: Okay, but she either doesn’t speak, or she stands up and speaks. She can whisper in your ear, but she can’t shout from her seat.
Defendant: Yes.

Defendant: No.
Judge: Okay. All right. So she comes in, and she says, “I don’t want the car” at some point?
Defendant: Yes.

Judge: But, counsel, the arbitration agreement, you concede, is still operative, right?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: For -- for disputes unrelated to the sweepstakes, absolutely.
Judge: Yeah. Well -- well -- and it says it applies to everything, all disputes about Coinbase services, right? The answer has to be yes.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yeah. On -- on -- on its -- its isolated terms, that's what the --
Judge: That's what it says, that's what it says, isolated terms, okay. And I guess my question for you is the inverse of the one I asked your friend on the other side. The point in Schein was, if you agree to say the arbitrator gets to direct things, if this were all in one contract, it's possible some things would be arbitrated, agreed to arbitrate and some things would be -- you agree to send to court. And if it were in one contract, there's no doubt that the arbitrator would decide which -- which direction to route things, right? The only question is does the second contract make this different than it being in a single contract. And I struggle to see why that would be the case. And I furthermore struggle to see why you would care because your real argument is that the agreement is to litigate this in court. And the routing, who's going to do the routing? Now we're at the Supreme Court of the United States, years have passed, we still don't know where this case is going to be. You could have had an answer from an arbitrator that you belong in court years ago. Why didn't you? Again, I just struggle to see both sides why we're here.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So, to deal with the first part with the Ninth Circuit and the user agreement, I just want to make clear that the Ninth Circuit didn't just rely on this existence language. After it sort of regurgitated that --
Judge: I'm not asking about the Ninth Circuit because it --
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Well -- well -- well --
Judge: -- seems to me both sides have disavowed the Ninth Circuit's opinion.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: -- it -- it really --
Judge: I'm asking why you are here because you could have had an answer from an arbitrator --
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Sure.
Judge: -- because I think your -- your argument's really quite strong as to where this gets routed.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Well, we've -- we've already won in court. We've already won arbitrability in court. And they haven't challenged that here. So the only way they resurrect the arbitrability dispute at all is by winning the delegation agreement.
Judge: My question is, though, if -- if you -- if you think you can win on -- on where this goes, why didn't you just go to the arbitrator to get a quick answer? Why -- why -- why litigate it all the way to the Supreme Court of the United States? I'll try it one more time.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So -- so, I mean, I guess I'd have to go back and think about why I did it in the trial court, why I did it in the --
Judge: Yeah.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: -- in the Ninth Circuit. But, here, so arbitrability, not delegation, arbitrability is finally resolved by the federal courts because -- unless this Court sends that dispute back to an arbitrator.
Judge: Right. Right.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Or the Ninth Circuit sends it back.
Judge: That's why -- that's why we're here. We're at the -- at the antecedent question.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Well, I don't want them to have a second chance to win arbitrability. They've already lost that.
Judge: Can I ask you about the hypothetical about things being in one contract? Because I think it actually matters with respect to what's going on here. So, if we had one contract that just had one delegation provision and then later underneath it, it had user agreements, sweepstakes, or whatever, it didn't say anything, you would agree that that delegation clause would apply to the whole thing?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: I -- I think that's the best reading of the user agreements in isolation.
Judge: All right. So -- but -- but -- but you're saying that, I guess, if we had one contract that had different parts and there was a delegation agreement that was in one part of it and then the other -- that went to the arbitrator and then, in the other, it was pretty clear the parties were saying this is delegated to the court, you're saying that would be a contract formation, the court would have to decide which one of those parts -- which one of those delegation intents was operable?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: It would definitely be a different question if it -- if the two types of terms were contained in a single agreement between the same two parties.
Judge: Yes.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Here, we have two separate agreements, two separate economic transactions, two separate groups of contracting parties, and that changes the intent analysis under -- under traditional rules of interpretation.
Judge: The -- are you done? The brief, you agree with Coinbase that the Ninth Circuit's opinion did not accurately reflect the parties' contractual disputes here, right, particularly the references to formation and existence?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Only that part, Your Honor, yes.
PLAINTIFF:

Dialogue:
Judge: I have a problem, which is I think I don't have to go as far as my colleague. You could continue what you're doing. We have two prior decisions telling the government a notice to appear is inadequate if it doesn't have the time and place. Despite that, and despite the fact that there's ample proof that it can be done, it hasn't been done. It continues to issue these TBA notices to appear. If we rule in your favor, we're giving you an incentive to continue that practice because you can do it continuously. You don't have to pay any attention to the statute. You can continue doing TBAs and continue your practice. As my colleague said, those people who show up, you give them a compliant one when they show up. Those people who don't, you remove them in absentia, and they can't ever come back and complain about your process. That's really the incentive here.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Your Honor, I have two responses to that. First, I want to talk a little bit about our current practices. We have made very significant progress in the years since the prior rulings in reducing the number of TBD NTAs that are still issued. There are technological and operational reasons why we're not down to zero yet, and I'm happy to talk about those if Your Honor is interested. But I do want to assure the Court that we take very seriously the obligation to comply with this Court's decisions and to comply with the statute. The bigger problem is, assuming we could issue NTAs that had time and date information going forward, it does not do anything about the hundreds of thousands of cases that have already been closed where removal orders were already issued—
Judge: So let's go to the practical problems. You started by saying there's an entitlement to reopen. There isn't. It is the right to make a motion to reopen, but it's still discretionary for the Board of Immigration Appeals to decide whether to reopen. Is that correct?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: That is the way that we read the statute. That's not the way that some courts, in particular, the Ninth Circuit, have read the statute. They have suggested that if a non-citizen files a motion and they can prove that they got the defective NTA—
Judge: Well, that's a separate legal issue. The way the statute is written, it is not mandatory to reopen. They can take into account what happened in one of these three cases where a litigant showed up. And I don't know, did you forfeit that argument in—was that the last case that's before us, the Mendez-Colin case?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: In the Mendez-Colin argument?
Judge: Yes. He showed up a number of times and then left, correct?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: That is correct, and we did not make a specific waiver or forfeiture argument if that's what Your Honor is referring to.
Judge: That—
Defendant: 's Lawyer: We highlighted the facts of his case for two reasons. The first is because it shows—
Judge: But you could have made that argument?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I suppose we could have, Your Honor. But it wouldn't, I think, address some of the problems with the other side's position, in particular—
Judge: Why?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: —the other side's—
Judge: If you had raised that argument, do you have any doubt the Board of Immigration Appeals would have said you forfeited because you had notice of these proceedings and your rights and the time and place?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: So there are two problems with relying on waiver or forfeiture in this context. The first is it still allows these motions to be filed. And if even some significant fraction of the hundreds of thousands of old in absentia orders are injected back into the immigration system, that could have a very significant impact on a system that is already dealing with a backlog of 3 million cases. The second problem is that waiver or forfeiture actually heightens the perverse incentives that the non-citizens’ rule creates. Mendez-Colin would have been better off just never showing up. And so their rule creates a circumstance where non-citizens are encouraged to flout the rules of the removal process by failing to appear.
Judge: But if you comply with the statute, they won't be in that position. This is about past people who have already chosen to abscond after they know about hearings. They can't unring the bell.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: And that's our significant concern, Your Honor. There are a huge number of these past people who have gotten in absentia orders after failing to appear, and we don't have any way to remedy that going forward. And if the Ninth Circuit's rule stands—
Judge: I mean, I appreciate the force of the point, Counsel, but there are so many people because the government was out of compliance with this statute for so long. And so, at a certain point, it's just—you can't sort of ask us to read the statute against what the statute says because we've created a world in which kind of we've long since forgotten what the statute says.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: So, respectfully, Your Honor, that's not what we're asking. We're asking you to apply the statute as it's written, and that is a statute that permits in absentia removal when a non-citizen received notice under either paragraph (1) or paragraph (2). And all the non-citizens here got the paragraph (2) notice. They haven't contested that they received notice of when and where to show up.
Judge: I think that's what most fundamentally I find a little bit discomfiting about your argument, this idea that there are two forms of notice, take your pick. If one is good, who cares about the other? I mean, it seems to me that if you read the statute fairly, it's quite clear—and this is what we said in our two prior cases—you know, (a)(1) is the notice. That's the notice. And what (a)(2)—it tells you what you have to put in it, all the things that you have to do. Here are the charges. Here is your right to counsel. And here is when you're supposed to show up. And what (a)(2) is about is Congress understood that there were going to be times when you were told to show up on March 15th and then it turned out that March 15th was impracticable or impossible for any of a number of reasons, and it was a mechanism to say, okay, we don't mean March 15th; instead, show up on August 15th. But that's what the function of (a)(2) is. It's not some completely distinct form of notice that you can say, hey, look, we did that one.

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to the People's Court. Next case on the docket: The plaintiffs say Drew bought a VW Jetta from the defendant that was supposedly in "Superman condition," and it was anything but. Judge: Dorothy and Drew Brown, you are suing Anthony Dorch for $3,284.67 that you say he owes you for things that have gone wrong with the car you bought from him. Tell me what’s going on.
Plaintiff: Drew: My son answered an ad on September 4, 2011.
Judge: Why don’t you tell me that part since it’s yours? How old are you?
Plaintiff: Drew: I’m 18 now. I was 17 when I answered the ad.
Judge: Is this your first car?
Plaintiff: Drew: Yeah, I just really wanted a car.
Judge: You were buying it with your money?
Plaintiff: Drew: Yeah, it was my money.
Judge: How did you make the money?
Plaintiff: Drew: I worked on a goat farm all summer.
Judge: Slaved on a goat farm? Sounds perfectly medieval.
Plaintiff: Dorothy: He was a slave on a goat farm, and he also worked at a restaurant.
Plaintiff: Drew: I found the ad on Craigslist. I was looking for a really long time for a car, and I saw this ad. It said it had a new timing belt, which was the main reason I was looking at it because that’s a huge cost. It had 156,000 miles, and I thought everything was just good with it. So I called him up that night.
Judge: What year was the car?
Plaintiff: Drew: It’s a 2001.
Judge: Let me see the ad. This is the actual ad you responded to?
Plaintiff: Drew: Yeah.
Judge: (Reading) "2001 Volkswagen Jetta 5-speed, black, 1.8T." What does that mean?
Plaintiff: Drew: It’s a 1.8-liter engine with a turbo.
Judge: Oh, so it’s souped up?
Plaintiff: Drew: It was pretty nice. Black leather, power sunroof, power everything. It said "Super Mint, new timing belt, water pump, tensioner, tune-up, clean title in hand."
Judge: So you go see the car, and you like it?
Plaintiff: Drew: Yeah.
Judge: Did you take it for an inspection to your mechanic?
Plaintiff: Drew: We put a down payment on the car when we went down there. We drove from New Haven that night. We took it for a test drive, and it drove really nicely. So we put down a $100 down payment.
Judge: What was the date?
Plaintiff: Drew: I think it was the 8th or 9th. We went down on the 6th and drove it back to us.
Judge: So what’s the answer to my question? Did you guys have it inspected by a mechanic before buying an 11-year-old car?
Plaintiff: Drew: Not before we purchased it, no.
Judge: Did you have it done afterward?
Plaintiff: Drew: I did.
Judge: Oh, a post-purchase inspection. What’s the point of a post-purchase inspection? That’s like having a headache, waiting until it’s over, and then taking some aspirin.
Plaintiff: Drew: You’re absolutely right. I mean, it’s my first car.
Judge: Not your first car, Mom?
Plaintiff: Dorothy: No, it’s not.
Judge: This is what he relies on you for, though?
Plaintiff: Dorothy: Yes, I know.
Judge: Tell me about the whole timing belt thing. Did you discuss it with him?
Plaintiff: Drew: We assumed it was a new timing belt as stated in the ad.
Judge: Did he give you paperwork?
Plaintiff: Drew: He said he worked at a mechanic shop and did the work himself.
Judge: So you finally took it to a mechanic after a month. What possessed you to do that?
Plaintiff: Drew: I wanted to make sure everything was running right.
Judge: So a month later, you get it inspected, and someone tells you it needs $3,200 worth of work?
Plaintiff: Dorothy: Yes, and there was a piece of wood wedged in to hold the timing belt tensioner.
Judge: Did they take pictures?
Plaintiff: Dorothy: Yes, on a cell phone.
Judge: Let me see that.
(Judge examines the evidence, discusses repair costs, and asks Defendant for proof of work done.)
Judge: Defendant, do you have any proof the timing belt was replaced?
Defendant: Anthony: No, I got it done by a neighborhood mechanic.
Judge: An alley mechanic?
Defendant: Anthony: Yeah, basically.
Judge: You get what you pay for. There was a piece of wood wedged in the timing belt tensioner.
Defendant: Anthony: If I knew that, I wouldn’t have driven it 66 miles.
Judge: The sale of a used car is an "as-is" sale unless stated otherwise. However, your ad said "new timing belt." You can’t advertise something that isn’t true.
(Judge rules in favor of Plaintiff for $1,122.)
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff, Drew Brown, is suing the Defendant, Anthony Dorch, for $3,284.67 due to issues with a 2001 Volkswagen Jetta that he purchased from Dorch. He was attracted to the car due to the new timing belt, as it was a significant cost he was trying to avoid.

Judge: No, but the fact -- excuse me.
The fact that that's true suggests that you're wrong about the statute being read as saying, TikTok, you have to go mute, because TikTok can continue to operate on its own algorithm, on its own terms, as long as it's not associated with ByteDance.
So isn't this really just all about association?
Lawyer of Defendant: Your Honor, I think it is partly about association, but I'm going to take another shot at explaining why it's not just about association.
Judge: Okay.
Well, let me just take you down the association path for a second because, if it is about the association of TikTok with ByteDance, then don't we have cases that seem to undermine your view that Congress can't do this? I mean, I thought we had cases about Congress prohibiting association with terrorist organizations, prohibiting association with foreign adversaries.
And so why doesn't this fall into that kind of group of -- of our jurisprudence?
Lawyer of Defendant: Well -- well, at least as I understand all of those cases, they applied strict scrutiny.
The -- the -- the material support statute most definitely applied strict scrutiny.
Judge: And -- and ultimately upheld the law, so fine.
Lawyer of Defendant: But -- but -- sure. And if -- I think, if we go down the strict scrutiny road here, I don't see that this law can possibly be satisfied under the interests that they assert here.
But I do want to emphasize why this is also about TikTok's speech.
Even under your hypothetical, where, theoretically, they can say something differently than they are say -- saying today, that in and of itself is a direct restriction on TikTok's speech.
They can't engage in the speech they want to engage in.
They have to engage in a different kind of speech, the speech they don't want to engage in.
That is a direct burden on TikTok, Incorporated's speech --
Judge: All right.
Lawyer of Defendant: -- wholly apart from association.
Judge: I think I understand that argument.
Let me ask you a question about your colloquy with Justice Kavanaugh.
Did I understand you to concede that there is a compelling interest and that the problem is really tailoring? I mean, you said: I understand the risks.
I don't hear you suggesting that the risks don't exist.
So it sounds like we've gotten past -- even if we're in strict scrutiny world, we've gotten past the compelling interest part of this.
Lawyer of Defendant: No, Your Honor.
What I was saying is that if all you had, standing alone, were the data security, that would be a different case.
Here, when you have the content manipulation sitting right alongside of the data security, that taints the data security rationale.
If Congress came in and said: We're passing this law for two reasons -- one, we really care about data security, and, two, we hate the speech on TikTok -- the data security wouldn't alone sustain that law.
Under cases like Mt. Pleasant, it would speak in both --
Judge: I understand.
But why -- why -- you're equating we don't want foreign adversaries to be able to manipulate the content on this platform, you're equating that with we hate the content, and I'm just trying to understand why.
Lawyer of Defendant: Be -- be -- sure. Because content manipulation is, by definition, a content-based distinction. Look, everybody manipulates content. There are lots of people who think CNN, Fox News, The Wall Street Journal, The New York Times, are manipulating their content.
That is core protected speech.
That's why they put so much weight on this mere covertness.
But --
Judge: Right, but that's -- that -- but that analysis is just about content-based versus content-neutral and, therefore, whether you apply strict scrutiny.
I'm in the strict scrutiny world.
Lawyer of Defendant: Mm-hmm.
Judge: Okay? I'm assuming that you're right that strict scrutiny applies, and now prong number one in that world is do -- does the government have a compelling interest.
Lawyer of Defendant: And --
Judge: And so I'm trying to understand why the government's argument that we have data manipulation concerns, which I understood you in colloquy with Justice Kavanaugh to say is a risk, and we are concerned, based on what Justice Gorsuch says when he's looking at the facts, you know, that the government contends that there's this real problem with this foreign adversary doing manipulation in other places, are you saying those are not compelling government interests?
Lawyer of Defendant: I am 100 percent saying that content manipulation is not just not a compelling governmental interest, it is an impermissible governmental interest.
You could not go to CNN or Fox News and say we're going to regulate you because you're manipulating the content in the way that we don't like.
That is per se impermissible.
Judge: Okay.
Lawyer of Defendant: That is why --
Judge: Can I just ask you one last thing? You -- you say with respect to the tailoring issue that disclosure, you think, is a possible more narrowly tailored way of handling some of this.
And I guess I'm just wondering whether disclosure under this Court's case law and the law of other lower courts doesn't carry its own First Amendment complications, that don't we have -- wouldn't we have compelled speech problems if disclosure was required in this situation?
Lawyer of Defendant: Sure, Your Honor. Now, look, I might think so because I think that the factual predicate is wrong, but they think the factual predicate is right.
And if the factual predicate is right, then there are no First Amendment problems at all under Zauderer and the cases that you're suggesting.
And that underscores the larger problem here.
Not all disclosures are perfect. I'm not here to argue that they are.
But you've always got to consider what the alternative is. And, here, the alternative is shutting down one of the largest speech platforms in America.
The reason there's no evidence in this record as to disclosures is because Congress never even undertook that balancing in the first place --
Judge: Thank you.
Lawyer of Defendant: -- the bare minimum that has to be done before we take an unprecedented -- unprecedented step of shutting down the voices of 170 million Americans.
Judge: Thank you, counsel.
Plaintiff: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Wholly apart from the companies' legal interests here, the Act directly restricts the rights, the First Amendment rights, of American creators to participate and speak in what the Court a little less than a decade ago called the modern public square and what you might say today is the most vibrant speech forum in the United States of America.
And the Act, therefore, is inescapably subject to strict scrutiny because of the First Amendment implications.
And the Act fails that test and, indeed, any level of scrutiny under this Court's case law because the Act and the reasons behind it defy our history and tradition, as well as precedent.

Judge: When a plaintiff brings a Section 1983 malicious prosecution claim under the Fourth Amendment, the plaintiff must show that the alleged malicious prosecution resulted in the plaintiff's seizure, as this Court held in Thompson. The Sixth Circuit correctly concluded that the plaintiff cannot make that showing because probable cause supported his detention.

In light of that, the presence of a third charge, allegedly lacking probable cause, could not have resulted in the plaintiff's detention.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: This result flows directly from existing Fourth Amendment precedents, including Devenpeck v. Elford and Whren v. United States, which make clear that a seizure does not violate the Fourth Amendment as long as the circumstances viewed objectively justify that action.
Judge: What happened to this radical agreement we heard of?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: It was news to me that there was radical agreement. I think what has happened is that the plaintiff has created this any-crime rule that was, in fact, a rule that the Sixth Circuit had not adopted.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Under this any-crime straw man rule, it would mean that the presence of one charge supported by probable cause automatically justifies any detention. I don't think that's what the Sixth Circuit thought.
Judge: What do I do with the language of the court that says, moreover, we can affirm the district court's decision if probable cause supports one or more of the three charges?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I realize that. I would again point the Court to the paragraph that I referenced because that's where they articulate the standard. And if there's anything that might be somewhat unsatisfactory about the lower court opinion, it's that they are joining together the false arrest, false imprisonment, and malicious prosecution analyses.
Judge: So do you disagree with the Solicitor General that if -- I don't know how we get to this proof, but assuming it could be made -- that absent the felony charge, no arrest warrant would have issued?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Oh, no. I absolutely --
Judge: A summons would have issued? Do you think then that it's an unreasonable seizure to have gotten the warrant?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: No, I disagree. And that’s not the way Fourth Amendment precedents operate in this area. We look at the detention and we ask whether it is objectively justified.
Judge: So what makes -- a seizure can be permitted but the detention unreasonable.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Absolutely. And I think that might arise in circumstances where a particular offense is maybe a fine-only offense, for example, and that's the only charge that can provide probable cause.
Judge: Just to get back to the question that was asked about that language, I understand that you're saying we shouldn’t take that language at face value, but if that language were taken at face value to mean that any crime gets you out of liability, you do disagree with that?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: If it meant an any-crime rule the way the plaintiff defines it, that any probable cause automatically insulates any detention, yes, we agree that would be incorrect.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: If, however, you look at the Sixth Circuit opinion and you disagree and you think that is the standard that the Sixth Circuit applied, then we think you should announce the right rule. And we, I think, largely agree with the United States about what that rule is.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: But we also think you should apply it in this case. There’s ample precedent for doing that in Crawford, Employment Division v. Smith, Terry v. United States, and Katz v. United States, and we think that would be appropriate to do here.

Dialogue:
Judge: What is the right perspective? So, when the previous judge asked you the question, he said how much would you buy the company for, but regardless of how he asked it, I think that would be one way to consider it, like what was the whole value worth.
Or do we ask if you had a stranger to the situation, what would the price of one share be? Is that the right way to think about it? And then just to build on that, do you assume the perspective of someone who would buy one of the shares, or just someone like you could even pretend that you had a third brother named Ralph who only had one share?
Like, what's the right way to think about it?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: It is a hypothetical buyer of the same proportion of shares in the company.
So it's a hypothetical buyer of 77.18 percent of the company’s shares.
Now, I think the reason why we talk about the value of a company here is that under the relevant regulations, we are really focusing on the net worth of the company and then multiplying the relevant percentage.
I think both sides are in agreement that this is the correct approach here.
Now, that will not always be true. There may be circumstances in which, for instance, that block of shares gives you a control premium that needs to be valued.
And when you look at the lower court case law in this area, often the price will then be adjusted up or down.
But we're all in agreement that there is no such adjustment here.
And so, really, the fundamental question here is: What was the net worth of the company?
And to make just two additional points about that, the first is the reason why we're talking about a hypothetical block of 77.18 percent of the shares is precisely because, if we were talking about the actual shares of the deceased, those shares are about to be extinguished.
They're subject to the redemption obligation.
So I think there's really no good conceptual way to do that.
And I think the regulations recognize that when they talk about the fact that you can look to an equivalent asset, a comparable item, when you're making this determination.
And then I think the second thing that I think is important to keep in mind here is, when you're talking about the net worth of the company, I don't really hear the other side disputing the fact that an obligation to redeem shares would be treated ordinarily as a liability like any other.
It's a legally binding obligation. The accounting standards treat it as a liability.
In fact, the accounting standards go so far as to specifically enumerate stock to be redeemed upon the death of the holder as giving rise to a liability.
Judge: So fair enough on that, but let's just see if I've got this right, and tell me where I'm wrong.
You agree that the relevant value is of the corporation as a whole.
And, really, the question is: What do we do with the $3 million in life insurance proceeds?
How should that be dealt with?
And I hear the other side saying a prospective buyer would consider that part of the assets of the corporation, and, therefore, it enhances the value of the company.
And I hear you saying no, you really shouldn't count those insurance proceeds because they're earmarked for the redemption, and so no willing purchaser would account for them in part of their assessment of the value of the company.
Is that a fair assessment of the difference between the two?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: I would word the point slightly differently, but I think this difference is important.
We're not disputing that the life insurance proceeds are an asset.
What we're really debating here is whether or not they are a net asset.
Judge: Whether a willing buyer would consider them part of the value of the company that they’re going to obtain when they're really earmarked for redemption?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: And what a willing buyer would do, I think, is to look at this and say:
Yes, there are $3 million in life insurance proceeds that are going to come into the company, but those proceeds are going to immediately go out again.
They're going to go out in order to fund this offsetting liability, which is already on the books.
And under our approach, which, again, I think accords with a healthy dose of common sense here, when the parties entered into the initial buy-sell agreement, that had the effect of putting an asset and a liability on the books at the same time.
Judge: They offset one another.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: They offset each other at every point.
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff's statements suggest that they are arguing for the value of the company to be determined by calculating the net worth, which is the total assets minus total liabilities. In this case, the Defendant's lawyer argues that a hypothetical buyer would consider a block of 77.18% of the company's shares, focusing on the net worth of the company rather than the value of individual shares.

The Plaintiff's goal or motive behind their words is to establish that the $3 million in life insurance proceeds should not be included in the company's value because they are earmarked for redemption and would immediately be used to offset an existing liability. The Plaintiff believes that a willing buyer would account for these proceeds as an offsetting liability, not as an asset that enhances the value of the company.

In summary, the Plaintiff's intent is to argue that the life insurance proceeds should not be included in the calculation of the company's net worth, as they are not a net asset because they will be used to offset an existing liability.
DEFENDANT:
The Defendant's statements suggest that they are arguing for the value of the company to be determined by calculating the net worth, which is the total assets minus total liabilities. In this case, the Defendant argues that a hypothetical buyer would consider a block of 77.18% of the company's shares, and they would account for a $3 million life insurance policy as an asset that would immediately be used to offset a liability (the redemption obligation). The Defendant's goal or motive behind their words is to argue that the life insurance proceeds should not be included in the company's total assets because they are earmarked for redemption and would not enhance the value of the company for a willing buyer.

Dialogue:
Judge: Some of our cases have suggested that the reason we have a clear statement rule is to, in this area, prevent the waiver of sovereign immunity accidentally. You know, that there has to be—it can't be through inadvertence that Congress has waived sovereign immunity. So you could look at this statute and especially the q problem and so forth and say that the waiver was accidental, it was inadvertent, and that's exactly what the clear statement rule tries to prevent. So what would be your best counter to that?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So I have two counters. One is about statutory interpretation. One is FCRA-specific.
The first one is I don't see how you have a workable principle of statutory interpretation that looks behind clear text to say, did Congress really mean this?
So, for example, if the 1996 Act had reenacted the definition of "person" word for word, if the committee report had said we're doing this because we want to make clear the government is liable, the words of the U.S. Code would be exactly the same. But under a rule that looks behind the text, the outcome would be completely different.
And the FCRA-specific argument is—I mean, here, you have a situation, as I mentioned in my opening, Congress didn't just sort of make a single amendment to the FCRA. It amended the civil liability provision to extend to persons in Section 2412 of the 1996 amendment. On the very next page, in the very next section, 2413, it used the word "person" to extend furnisher obligations onto those who furnish information to consumer reporting agencies.
And I think it's fairly implausible that when Congress turned the page, it somehow, using the same word, intended a different definition of the term to apply.
Judge: Could I ask you a couple of questions about Employees? First, suppose that Employees had decided the very issue that is before us now. Would you say that we should disregard it because it used an outmoded method of statutory interpretation?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Well, if Employees had decided the Fair Credit Reporting Act, I think there was an argument for statutory stare decisis that might still apply to the FLSA, but since that doesn't apply here, I think the Court should and has in the past—in the case of implied causes of action—rejected prior methods of interpretation that had become outmoded.
Judge: And was that a "yes" or a "no"?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: I—
Judge: Or maybe?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: —may have lost the thought on the original question, but I—
Judge: Yeah. If it involved the very question that is before us now—
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yeah.
Judge: —would—do you think we should—we would disregard it, or we should disregard it because we disagree with the method of statutory interpretation?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: If it's the very question in a different statute, my answer would be the same. The Court should not follow Employees and should instead read the statute the way it currently reads statutes.
Judge: Okay. I think you went into my second question. Suppose that the statute is different, but the structure—the wording and the structure in all relevant respects is the same. Do you think we should disregard it because of its method of statutory interpretation?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yes, I think you should. Now, when you say the same, there are a lot of differences between the statute there and here, but just to answer your hypothetical, you shouldn't follow a method of interpretation that you had rejected previously in construing a new statute.
Judge: There are a lot of important decisions from the 1970s and the 1980s that use a method of statutory interpretation that is probably not the one that we would use if those questions came before us today. You think we should just disregard all those? They're all fair game? Are they all fair game?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: I think it's open to litigants to argue that the Court should—the statutes say something different.
Judge: Well, the answer to that question has got to be no, right?
[Laughter.]
I mean, we're not going to throw out all our precedents because we've decided that there's a better way to interpret statutes.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: No. No, that's right. I mean, I'm not talking about an over—
Judge: So you have to be saying that this is a different statute, and you are saying that it has a different structure, right?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: I'm saying both of those things. Maybe I misunderstood the hypothetical.
Judge: I didn't think you said it had a different structure. I thought you said that the methodology used—
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: No, the statute had—
Judge: —a methodology—
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Well, the statute has a different structure because, in Employees, the government could still enforce the FLSA against states. That was an important part of the Employees decision.
Judge: What do you do—you've treated Employees as if it's this one-off outlier, but then, in subsequent cases, like Union Gas, it seems like the same principles, accepted by all nine justices there, were used. In other words, the specific reference to states in the original Act in Union Gas, the Court goes out of its way to say that alone—in the definitional provisions—that alone would not have been good enough to do it and cites Employees.
And then, in College Savings Bank, the Court makes a big point that Employees started the retreat from Parden, which was a much looser standard for waiver of sovereign immunity. So it's not an out—you know, it's not just that this case has never been cited again.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: It hasn't been cited for the statutory interpretation point, I think, until—in any subsequent case since the late '80s. I think, in Union Gas, you're correct, the statute was written differently. There was additional language in the definitions section that was dispositive to the Court's analysis there.
So the Court didn't have to sort of reach out and try to address the question the Court might have to address here, which is how viable is that sort of method of interpretation.
Judge: By method of interpretation—I just want to get back to this because it mentions the text and it mentions this principle that we don't lightly interpret the text to waive sovereign immunity. And then there's nothing else that would suggest—in the history of it—that a waiver was intended either. I don't—that sounds like an opinion you could write now and has been written now.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Well, there's nothing in Employees that grapples with why the statutory definition in that case did not meet the clear statement standard.
Judge: I thought it said because the reference to government, to public employers, was not in the cause of action provision.

Dialogue:
Judge: The purpose of a post-seizure hearing is to determine who keeps custody of the car, correct?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yes.
Judge: And the focus is, therefore, different? The focus in the hearing would be that there might be a disputed issue of fact with respect to ownership. The government might claim it needs discovery. The government might claim it has some facts that would lead to a judgment that it needs to explore. But the court would then weigh whether or not that is sufficient not to give custody to the car owner pending the hearing, correct?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yes, Judge.
Judge: So summary judgment doesn't answer this question or the isolated question of who keeps custody of the car pending the ultimate judgment, correct?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: That's right.
Judge: Now, whether or not summary judgment is adequate given that difference in the focus of the hearings, I'm presuming that's why you're saying that's not the issue before us. The issue before us is, which of the two tests do we apply to determine whether that's enough or not?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: That's right, Judge.
Judge: I mean, if I could ask about that same kind of thing, what the difference is between the retention hearing and the final forfeiture determination—take a case like this, where your client is raising an innocent owner defense, and I would think that the questions about whether she was an innocent owner are pretty much the same in the retention hearing and in the final forfeiture determination, isn't that correct?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I think the substantive question is the same, yes.
Judge: Now, there is a different burden, but if she can prove at the retention hearing under a probable cause standard that she is entitled to the car back, I mean, there's no way the government is going to lose on the final determination, right? I mean, she's proved that she's entitled to the car?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I think that's most likely correct. In theory, by the time of the final determination, there could be some additional discovery or investigation that happens that would change the calculus.
Judge: Yeah, I suppose in an individual case, but most likely—
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Most likely.
Judge: The government probably would just give up at that point, right? Under this very generous standard to the government, they've lost, they're not going to keep on pursuing the thing, so she's gotten her car back and the case is over.
And I guess what this suggests is that in both cases, you're really adjudicating the same thing, which is: Am I entitled to my car back right now? So how is it really different? I understand saying this is interim, this is final, but in the end, it's just: Am I entitled to my car back now?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Judge, I think that the substantive question is the same, but there are a few key differences between the retention hearing and the later merits determination.
For one thing, I don't know that the premise is correct that the government would just give up if it loses at the retention hearing. Again, there's a different procedural standard later. The government has the opportunity to conduct more discovery. The government also has, in Alabama, as well as 25 other states, a financial incentive to keep pursuing the forfeiture proceedings because they get to keep the proceeds.
And so I don't know that it's empirically correct that the government would simply give up if it loses at the retention hearing.
Judge: I guess what I'm asking is, if we had a case that says, you know, the constitutional rule about forcing a determination about who is entitled to the car is the Barker rule, why is it that we can say: Well, we have that rule, but, in fact, there's another constitutional rule which is much more beneficial to the claimant that's meant to address exactly the same question that we held in $8,850 was addressed by Barker?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: So I think they're different—for one thing, I think they are different questions, as the Second and the Sixth Circuit have explained.
In Barker, the only question—it was essentially a case where the claimant was trying to argue a gotcha. There was no argument in Barker—or in $8,850 or Von Neumann—that the government was not ultimately entitled to forfeit the property. There was no innocent owner defense.
The claimant's argument there was: Well, but you waited too long in order to actually complete the proceedings and, therefore, I get my car back. And in that situation, this Court said the Barker test applies.
There's a different argument where you have an innocent owner defense, where you have somebody coming forward and saying: I should be entitled, as a matter of Alabama state law, to the rights that Alabama state law gives me. I should be entitled to keep my car. And the state can't effect a de facto forfeiture of that car for months or years during the pendency of proceedings.
Judge: But, in both cases, the claimant wants the property back in the interim. And the court, I mean, couldn't have been much clearer in its language:
"The forfeiture proceeding without more provides the hearing required by due process to protect Von Neumann's property interest in the car," and then repeated it two pages later, "the right to a forfeiture proceeding meeting the Barker test satisfies any due process right with respect to the car and the money."
And those, to me, didn't seem to be accidental comments. I went back to the oral argument transcript where exactly this question was posed about whether that is all the process that’s due, and the court was very definitive.
So you've referred a few times to a methodological question. Methodologically, how can we get around, from your perspective, that seemingly clear statement? I know you have factual distinctions, but those are broad, clear statements that have guided courts since.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Judge, I think those statements have broad language that has to be understood in context.
With respect to the first sentence that you quoted—"the forfeiture proceeding without more provides the post-seizure hearing that due process requires"—that was in part 2 of Von Neumann.
Part 2 of Von Neumann was the section of that opinion holding that the claimant had no due process interest in the first place. That sentence can't reasonably be understood to say anything about due process where, as here, the substantive Alabama law confers an additional interest that then gives rise to new due process requirements.
With respect to the second sentence that you quoted, first of all, that was just referring back to the first sentence from part 2, which, again, doesn't apply here. In the third part of the Von Neumann opinion where that sentence comes from, the court assumed that a protected due process interest existed.
That assumption doesn't really make a lot of sense doctrinally. You can't have a due process interest in a remission proceeding, which is essentially like a discretionary pardon.
Judge: That was the argument, though, wasn't it?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I'm sorry?
Judge: That was the argument, right?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: The court assumed there that there was a due process interest. But I'm saying the assumption doesn't even really hold because you can't have a due process interest in that kind of a discretionary proceeding.
And beyond that, again, the argument that the Court actually addressed in the Von Neumann opinion was about a final determination. It was about the speed to final determination in a context where there was no substantive right to avoid the forfeiture.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff's lawyer is arguing that there are key differences between a retention hearing and a final forfeiture determination, despite both hearings addressing the question of whether the claimant is entitled to their property back. The lawyer contends that the government may not necessarily give up if it loses at the retention hearing due to procedural differences, financial incentives, and the opportunity for further discovery. The lawyer also argues that the Barker rule, which addresses the question of when a determination about property entitlement is required, does not apply in cases where an innocent owner defense is raised.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant's lawyer argues that while the retention hearing and the final forfeiture determination share similarities in terms of determining whether the Defendant is entitled to their car back, there are key differences between the two. The lawyer suggests that the government may not necessarily give up if it loses at the retention hearing due to procedural differences, financial incentives, and the opportunity for further discovery. The lawyer also argues that the Barker rule, which addresses the question of who is entitled to the car, is not applicable in cases where an innocent owner defense is raised.

Judge: It's much broader than that, Your Honor.

So a case may be pending for two or three years.
The district court might be really familiar with it.
It might be a really straightforward question of state law.
In that situation --
Lawyer of Plaintiff: So how doesn't Rule 15 take care of that? It gives plaintiffs a narrow window to amend and, otherwise, it needs to seek permission.
So why didn't the district court simply deny permission here?
Judge: This case was pending almost two years before the amendment was made, but it was still an amendment as of right in this case.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: It wasn't within the window permitted by the rule.
Judge: It was, Your Honor, because the case went up on appeal and came down.
So there are situations like that.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: I see.
Judge: But there's also -- you know, leave to amend should be freely given under Rule 15.
That's not really the kind of standard that takes into account these judicial efficiencies, comity --
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Oh, it certainly does.
I mean, that's the entire purpose of the freedom -- of the power and discretion to amend. So I just think, as a matter of first principles, it's -- it's really -- you have an amici, the Center for Litigation and Courts, who supports your argument but says don't rely on that.
And I think they make that point for a reason.
It's not your strongest point.
And then I don't understand why we should change all the other rules that respect an amended complaint as the complaint setting forth the claims in an action.
Judge: Your Honor, we think, if you were to rule for the other side, that would be upsetting a hundred years of precedent, every single court of appeals decision.
That would be changing the rules.
All we ask this Court to do here is apply settled law.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Justice Kagan? Justice Gorsuch?
Lawyer of Defendant: We talked a lot about 1367, but I'm not sure we paid much attention yet to 1447.
And I -- I can certainly see the argument that the operative complaint should be the one at the time of removal under the old version of 1447, which suggests that a case should be remanded if it was improvidently removed.
That -- that does seem to focus the Court's attention on the complaint at the time of removal.
And I think a lot of the court of appeals kind of have been operating under that kind of idea of the rule.
But it's been amended, and it now reads that -- that a case should be remanded if at any time it appears that the district court lacks subject matter jurisdiction, which, you know, just reading that, one might -- and I'm sure we're going to hear this argument, so I wanted to give you a chance to respond to it before you sit down -- that that focuses the Court's attention on --on the then-operative complaint.
Thoughts?
Judge: Two responses, Your Honor.
So I think it's important to keep in mind that the 1911 version of the statute, the predecessor of 1447 that was in effect during St. Paul Mercury, had basically the same text as it did today.
Lawyer of Defendant: I grant -- I grant you that.
And then it went to was improvidently removed --
Judge: Yes.
Lawyer of Defendant: -- for a very long time.
And now it's come back to looking more directly at the -- the then-operative complaint, doesn't it?
Judge: So I think it is important that this Court reached the ruling that it did in St. Paul Mercury under the old text.
I think that suggests that it --
Lawyer of Defendant: I grant you that --
Judge: Yes.
Lawyer of Defendant: -- with respect to the amount in controversy.
We've been around that --that tree a few times. So putting aside that point, have you got anything else you want to say about it?
Judge: Certainly. So they didn't make that argument in the red brief because it doesn't answer the question.
The question is whether the federal court has jurisdiction or not.
We think that's answered at the time of removal.
And this Court, in the Wisconsin Department of Corrections case, said that Section 1447 was merely procedural.
It did not affect the district court's jurisdiction.
So I think you would have to revisit that case in order to read 1447 here the way you suggest.
Lawyer of Defendant: Okay.
Thank you.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Justice Kavanaugh?
Defendant: I just want to make sure on the state of the law, and maybe following up on Justice Kagan's question, because you would think when you pick this up, if you were uninitiated, that there would be a standard rule.
Look at the complaint at the time of filing or removal, or look at the complaint at the time of amendment.
But at least as I looked at everything, it's just a mess, right? There's just boxes everywhere where, you know, in the diversity context, destroying diversity almost always compels dismissal or a remand, but reducing the amount in controversy or changing citizenship of a party almost never does. Right? Is that correct?
Judge: That's correct, Your Honor.
There --
Defendant: Like Morgan's Heirs and St. Paul Mercury on the one hand and Owen Equipment on the other, there's no logic connecting those things at least as I see it.
Judge: I --
Defendant: They were just rules out there without connective logic.
There might be --each box has its own little idiosyncratic policy considerations, but there's no connective rule at least as I read it. Correct me if I'm wrong.
Judge: I think you're right.
Defendant: Or the other side can correct me if I'm wrong too.
Yeah.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff's lawyer is arguing that the court should not change the existing rules regarding amendments to complaints, as this would disrupt a century-long precedent and potentially create confusion in the legal system. They emphasize the importance of judicial efficiency and comity, and suggest that the power to amend complaints is designed to facilitate such efficiency. The Plaintiff's lawyer also raises concerns about the application of Rule 1447, arguing that the court should consider the operative complaint at the time of removal, rather than at the time of amendment.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant's statements suggest that they are arguing for the application of settled law, specifically the interpretation of Rule 1447 and the St. Paul Mercury ruling, in a case where the plaintiff has amended their complaint after the initial removal of the case to federal court. The Defendant argues that the federal court's jurisdiction should be determined at the time of removal, not at the time of amendment, and that this interpretation is supported by the text of the amended Rule 1447 and previous court decisions.

Dialogue:
Judge: Daniel Benal, you are suing Homero Zapata for $5,000, the amount you paid for a vehicle that you claim you never got a valid title for. Tell me what happened.
Plaintiff: Your Honor, around June 18th, a mutual friend named Anthony facilitated an exchange. I traded a watch I paid $7,000 for in exchange for the defendant’s car, which he was selling for $5,000.
Judge: Okay.
Plaintiff: At the time, I was living in Massachusetts. It’s impossible to live there without a car—I couldn't get a job, etc.
Judge: What made you move to Massachusetts if you didn’t already have a job?
Plaintiff: I did have a job lined up, but I couldn’t take it without a car.
Judge: Who proposed this trade?
Plaintiff: It was my idea.
Judge: What kind of watch was it?
Plaintiff: A Chopard Grand Prix Del Monaco. Limited edition. There are 500 in the world.
Judge: Why didn’t you just sell the watch, keep two grand, and buy the car?
Plaintiff: The resale value fluctuates depending on whether you have the box and papers. A barter was easier.
Judge: Okay, so the car gets delivered to you how?
Plaintiff: July 2nd. The defendant drove it up to Massachusetts.
Judge: Who did you give the car to?
Defendant: I drove it up and gave it to him directly. I already had the watch before that.
Judge: So he just gave you the watch before even getting the car?
Defendant: Yes. He trusted Anthony, and Anthony trusted me.
Judge: Then what happened?
Plaintiff: I went to register the car at the DMV that week. They told me the title was defective. I have a copy here. The car was registered to someone named Timothy, not the defendant.
Judge: Who is Timothy?
Defendant: I bought the car at an auction. I planned to buy it for a relative.
Judge: Did you ever register the car in your name?
Defendant: No, ma’am.
Judge: So Timothy signed over the title?
Defendant: Yes, but there were cross-outs, and I was misinformed. I thought I could sign it over directly. I didn’t know.
Judge: So, plaintiff, you went to the DMV, and they wouldn’t accept it?
Plaintiff: Correct. Over the next few weeks, I tried to sort it out with the defendant.
Judge: Defendant: , when did the plaintiff first notify you?
Defendant: August 7th.
Judge: Why the delay, plaintiff?
Plaintiff: I had been going back and forth with the DMV.
Judge: I don’t buy that. I think you waited a month. So, what did you do next, defendant?
Defendant: He mailed me the title. I received it on August 13th. The next day, I went to the DMV to fix it. They told me it would take six to eight weeks. I told the plaintiff, and he was okay with it.
Judge: Did the new title come?
Defendant: Yes, on October 11th.
Judge: When did you inform the plaintiff?
Defendant: October 12th. I texted him. He never responded.
Judge: Plaintiff: , when did you file this lawsuit?
Plaintiff: Before I heard from him.
Judge: Where is the car now?
Plaintiff: Still in Massachusetts, parked in Anthony’s backyard.
Judge: So here’s how this works. When you sell a car, you’re supposed to provide a good title. The plaintiff went to the DMV and couldn't register the car. The defendant then cooperated fully and did everything he was supposed to do. He fixed the title issue as soon as he got the paperwork from the plaintiff.
Judge: The delay between August 14th and October 12th was due to the DMV. That’s not the defendant’s fault. You agreed to wait for the new title. By the time he got it, you had changed your mind. But you can’t just undo the deal because of a DMV delay you knew would happen.
Judge: This is your car, plaintiff. Do you have the title, defendant?
Defendant: Yes, ma’am.
Judge: Hand it over. Did you sign it in the right place this time?
Defendant: Not yet.
Judge: Good. I’ll make sure this is transferred correctly.
Verdict for the defendant.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff, Daniel Benal, is suing Defendant Homero Zapata for $5,000, the amount he paid for a vehicle that he claims he never received a valid title for. The Plaintiff's statements suggest that he traded a valuable Chopard Grand Prix Del Monaco watch (worth $7,000) for the defendant's car, which was being sold for $5,000. The Plaintiff needed a car to live in Massachusetts, where he had moved to for a job.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant, Homero Zapata, sold a vehicle to the Plaintiff, Daniel Benal, for $5,000. The exchange was facilitated by a mutual friend, Anthony. The Plaintiff traded a valuable Chopard Grand Prix Del Monaco watch, worth $7,000, for the car.

Dialogue:
Judge: Now, if the jury's making the finding, the Rule of Evidence applies in ways it doesn't to the judge, but all the litigation is going to happen regardless. It's just who's making the fact finding. And I think you -- I want to come back to your other question quickly.
Judge: Yes.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: You asked about are we going to have these borderline Almendarez-Torres cases coming back to you. I don't think that's necessarily the case. I won't say it's impossible, but this is my answer to the previous question. There are very few states that have or -- or in the federal code that have facts beyond the prior conviction itself that trigger enhancements that are -- that are currently in the law found by judges. So I think it's very uncommon. And, of course, there will be further guidance presumably in this opinion for federal and state judges, so I think it's very unlikely you're going to see additional cases just because those laws are so uncommon.
Judge: Thank you.
Judge: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Feigin.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Thank you, Your Honor, and may it please the Court: As I think the discussion so far this morning has demonstrated, as we see it, this case boils down to an unavoidable syllogism, which is that under Wooden, the different occasions finding under the ACCA requires a multi-factored inquiry involving the timing, the proximity of location, and the character and relationship of prior offenses, whereas the Sixth Amendment prohibits, to use the words in Mathis, exploring the manner in which a prior conviction's offense occurred. And we therefore think that because a district judge is disempowered from doing it, the only option left is that the jury has to do it, and so we have acknowledged that a jury would need to do that. I'm happy to take the Court's questions, but I don't think the Court needs to or, frankly, should say much more than that to resolve this case.
Judge: Well, wouldn't it be cleaner, though, to just simply overrule Almendarez-Torres?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: I had a suspicion you might ask me that question, Your Honor.  And as you might anticipate, your suspicion might have been my answer is no, and the reason why is that we don't -- we think Almendarez-Torres is correct, but it's also a precedent this Court has adhered to for 25 years post-Apprendi, always acknowledging this. Nobody's asked the Court to overrule it in this case. There's no need for the Court to overrule it in this case. And we therefore just leave it the way it is.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: To address some of the questioning from the Court, we don't think very many Almendarez-Torres questions are really going to come up in practice. We do think that it means a bit more than I think my colleague thinks it means, but our principal interest here is actually in the type of cases that are currently before the Court in the Brown and Jackson cases, which involve the comparison of state predicates to federal predicates and some timing questions that come up with those. And that situation's already covered by McNeill. It's clear that a court can find what version of the statute was applied to the defendant at the time of the prior conviction, so --
Judge: Do you want to say why you think Almendarez-Torres is correct?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, Your Honor, I -- I don't really want to turn this into relitigation of Almendarez-Torres, which, again, even the Defendant has not asked for, but we think the Court was correct. There are more states that I think support that than my colleague would acknowledge. There are clear precedents from multiple states. And although some of those statutes allow for jury trials on the back end, that was all historical evidence that was in front of the Court in Almendarez-Torres, and the Court found that there was at least enough non-uniformity on the issue to allow legislatures some flexibility on this topic.
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff's lawyer's statements suggest that the case at hand revolves around a syllogism involving the interpretation of the Armed Career Criminal Act (ACCA) and the Sixth Amendment. The Plaintiff argues that the ACCA requires a multi-factored inquiry, while the Sixth Amendment prohibits exploring the manner in which a prior conviction's offense occurred. The Plaintiff believes that, due to this conflict, the jury should be the ones to make the fact-finding decision.

The Plaintiff's goal or motive behind their words is to persuade the judge to allow the jury to make the fact-finding decision in cases that involve the ACCA, rather than the judge.
DEFENDANT:
The Defendant's lawyer suggests that the application of Almendarez-Torres cases, which involve enhancements based on prior convictions, is uncommon and unlikely to increase significantly due to the rarity of laws that trigger such enhancements beyond the prior conviction itself. The Defendant's lawyer also argues that Almendarez-Torres is a long-standing precedent that has been adhered to for 25 years and should not be overruled in this case, as it is not being challenged by the Defendant. The Defendant's goal or motive appears to be to limit the application of Almendarez-Torres cases and maintain the current legal precedent, as they believe it is correct and has been consistently upheld.
