Dialogue:
Judge: This is the case of Chenise Hawkins. She says she put down a deposit on an apartment and was ready to move in when the defendant abruptly changed her mind and rented the place to someone else. Now, the defendant refuses to return her \$500, which the plaintiff believes is unfair. She is here suing for the money.
Plaintiff: On the evening of October 21st, I contacted Glattus to see an apartment she was renting. We liked the place, so we left a cash deposit. She wanted us to bring more information the next day, which I did. I brought my pay stubs and everything. She then told me that a few other couples had come and seen the place as well and were willing to give her \$500 to move in that day until November 1st.
Judge: And you were planning to rent it from November 1st?
Plaintiff: Yes.
Judge: So what did she ask you for?
Plaintiff: She asked me for an additional \$250 to move in that day.
Judge: Or else what?
Plaintiff: She’d rent it to the other people.
Judge: And what did you say when she said that to you?
Plaintiff: At first, I agreed. Then I went home and discussed it with my fiancé, and we decided that November 1st was better because I had already paid rent for October.
Judge: Did you call her back and tell her that?
Plaintiff: I did. I called her back the next morning, and she said she was going to teach me the law and that I could only get \$500 back.
Judge: Was the topic ever discussed that you could just go ahead and move in as planned?
Plaintiff: No, she did not. She wanted the extra \$250.
Defendant: She came on the 21st with her boyfriend. I showed them the apartment, and they loved it. They asked me what the requirements were, and I told them one month’s security and one month’s rent. Once a deposit is made, I cannot rent it to anyone else.
Judge: Did you call them and tell them you had other interested renters?
Defendant: Yes, she came back with her documents, and I told her about the other people.
Judge: Why were you trying to get another \$250 if you couldn't rent it to anyone else?
Defendant: Because it was 10 days from the 21st to the 31st, so I said I would take off five days and she would only pay for five.
Judge: But why did you do that? If she already had the apartment, why were you trying to charge her more?
Defendant: They paid \$1,000, and then she came back, and I told her about the other offer. She went outside and called her fiancé. Then she came back in.
Judge: If your premise is that the \$1,000 deposit secured the apartment, meaning you couldn’t rent it to anyone else, then why did you ask for an additional \$250? That sounds like extortion. She already had the apartment for November 1st, so she didn't have to give you another penny. You changed the deal, not her.
Defendant: I told them the requirements from the beginning.
Judge: Did you tell them after accepting the \$1,000 deposit that they needed to give you another \$250 the next day?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: You can't do that. Once you accepted their deposit, you had an oral agreement. You can’t turn around and demand more money. You breached the contract, not her. And when she backed out, you rented it to someone else the same day. You didn’t lose anything. In fact, you made an extra \$500.
Defendant: But they changed their mind.
Judge: No, she always planned to move in on November 1st. You tried to charge her more after the fact. If you had other renters, why didn’t you just return her deposit and rent to them?
Defendant: I did rent it to them.
Judge: Then you’re out nothing. Keeping the deposit is meant to cover a loss, not to give you extra profit. Pay the plaintiff her \$500. Verdict for the plaintiff.
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff's statements suggest that they initially agreed to pay a deposit for an apartment on October 21st, with the intention of moving in on November 1st. The Plaintiff was asked to pay an additional $250 to move in earlier, which they initially agreed to but later decided against after consulting with their fiancé. The Plaintiff claims that the Defendant then refused to return their initial $500 deposit, stating that they could only get a portion of it back.

The Plaintiff's goal or motive behind their words appears to be to recover the $500 deposit they believe they are owed, as they are suing the Defendant for the money.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant's statements suggest that they had an apartment available for rent and showed it to the Plaintiff on October 21st. The Defendant initially asked for a deposit of one month's rent and security, which the Plaintiff provided. The Defendant then informed the Plaintiff about other interested renters and asked for an additional $250 to move in earlier than the agreed-upon date.

Dialogue:
Judge: I do have one question. So putting aside what n and o mean—we've gone around that tree—your second argument was that Congress needs to be clearer than even that to waive sovereign immunity. Even that wouldn't be enough.
And I guess I wonder why. I certainly understand—this is kind of a first principles question. I certainly understand the clear statement rule this Court has developed to protect the Eleventh Amendment and state sovereign immunity, separate sovereigns.
But, here, we have the sovereign itself speaking, right? It's not waiving someone else's immunity. It's not purporting to strip another entity of its protections under the Constitution. It's Congress, which has control over the federal fisc, itself deciding. And so I wonder why the clear statement rule would be appropriate in those circumstances.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So two answers to that, Judge. The first is that Respondent's argument has exactly the same effect in terms of Congress's meaning as to states and in terms of its effect as to Indian tribes and foreign nations. If you rule against the United States here, you will necessarily be saying that it waived sovereign immunity for them too.
Judge: You're just fighting my question. I'm saying it is different. It is one thing to waive a tribe, a state, a foreign government's immunity. Congress purporting to exercise that power under the Constitution is an extraordinary power. It's permissible sometimes, but, generally, we think of it as inconsistent with the structure of our Constitution, and that's why we have traditionally required a clear statement rule.
It's less clear to me what justifications we have for requiring magic words to waive sovereign immunity when it's the sovereign itself opening itself up to suit.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So we, of course, don't think it's magic words, but that's not your question. This Court has repeatedly said that it applies the same standard when evaluating—
Judge: I'm asking why.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So the Court has identified a number of justifications for—
Judge: What do you think it's saying?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: The clear statement. I'd combine two.
Judge: Okay.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: First, as Federalist 81 said way back at the founding, it was well established then that the sovereign could not be haled into court without its consent.
And, second, tracing all the way back to Blackstone, it was well settled that courts should not interpret statutes to apply to the sovereign unless that was the only permissible reading.
And so, by the middle of the 19th century, courts had recognized that in light of those two principles, you couldn't read a congressional enactment to waive sovereign immunity unless that was the only plausible reading, and, of course, by now, this Court has repeated that so many times that it's a well-established background principle against which Congress legislates.
Judge: Thank you.
Judge: Just on the clear statement rule part of your argument, so that part, I mean, we said that sometimes the better interpretation of a statute will not necessarily prevail because of the clear statement rule.
So even assuming the interpretation of the statute is against you, the better interpretation, you can still prevail under the clear statement rule?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yes, that's absolutely right. We just need a—
Judge: Okay. So that's the starting point. And then, for the why, you know, ultimately, it's money that Congress has the power to appropriate, right? And we want to be careful about that.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yes, we think that's correct.
Judge: Okay. And that's basic separation of powers?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yes, absolutely.
Judge: Okay. So how much would it cost here if you were to lose this case?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So we don't have a precise estimate of that, Judge. I will say that there is a case pending in the Seventh Circuit right now that's being held for this case in which the plaintiff has attempted to assert a class action.
We understand that the damages, if they were to prevail, would be in the millions of dollars. I don't know exactly what that would be, but Congress would have anticipated that the potential liability here would be significant, I think.
Judge: And punitive damages too, right?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So we, of course, don't think those would be available. But I think, if you accepted that this is clear enough—
Judge: If you accept their argument down the line, punitive damages could be available?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: That's correct.
Judge: Okay. And then, on Employees, you've mentioned that case. And that case seems structurally—I mean, I'm going to ask a lot to the other side about this—seems structurally similar to this statute, how this one developed.
But the other side basically says don't pay attention to Employees, and I think another Judge alluded to that, because it's old, a 1973 case. So I want your response to that.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So, I mean, first, we don't think this is how this Court treats precedents. Again, as I said, we think that this Court has reaffirmed Employees and recognized that Employees began the retreat from Parden, but even just looking at the text of Employees, I think it's consistent with the argument and the distinctions that I'm drawing today.
The Court there said that the cause of action by its literal terms covered state agencies, but it recognized that the relevant question was whether Congress had intended to bring the states to heel by lifting their immunity. So it recognized that was a distinct question, and then it said Congress wouldn't have done that silently.
And, in particular, it said that if Congress had intended to do that, it would have done one of two things. It either would have addressed immunity expressly, or it would have amended the cause of action, and in that context, what that would have meant was adding a specific reference to state agencies to the cause of action.
And because Congress hadn't done either of those things, the Court in Employees found there wasn't a clear waiver. We think the same thing is true here.
Judge: So then, to pick up on another Judge's questions earlier, if you see Employees on one side of the line and you see cases like Kimel on the other side of the line, where the reference to the public entities is in the cause of action itself, that's slicing it pretty thin.
Like, what sense does that make or what principle would undergird sticking to Employees in the way that you're advocating?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So I think, in the cases like Kimel, there's a superfluity argument that unless you hold that Congress has waived sovereign immunity, that text in the cause of action will do absolutely no work. And so the Court has said Congress wouldn't have effectively negated that and has been willing to infer a waiver of immunity.
But Respondent has conceded that our interpretation doesn't produce any superfluity here. So even if you think that probably members of Congress intended to waive sovereign immunity, there's no necessary implication that they must have. And under the clear statement rule and under Employees, that's enough to rule for us.
Judge: Can you give me just one quick example of how it's not superfluous—
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So—
Judge: —if you were to prevail here?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: "Person" would cover entities other than private parties that furnish information to those credit reporting agencies.
Judge: No, the coverage of government, how the coverage of government wouldn't be superfluous even if you were to prevail here.
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: So the coverage of government ensures that the government can obtain credit reports. I mean, the coverage of government was there before the cause of action, so the one thing we know is that the coverage of government is not there in order to allow recovery under the cause of action.
Judge: Thank you.

The Plaintiff's goal or motive behind their words is to protect Congress's sovereign immunity and maintain the separation of powers, as well as to avoid potential significant financial liability.

The Plaintiff's intent can be summarized in three sentences as follows:
1. The Plaintiff argues that the clear statement rule should apply when Congress is waiving its own sovereign immunity, as it protects the sovereign from being haled into court without its consent and interprets statutes to apply to the sovereign only if that is the only plausible reading.

DEFENDANT:
The Defendant's lawyer argues that the clear statement rule is necessary to protect the sovereign (in this case, the United States government) from being sued without its consent, a principle established at the founding. The rule is based on two principles: (1) it was well established that the sovereign could not be haled into court without its consent, and (2) courts should not interpret statutes to apply to the sovereign unless that was the only permissible reading. The clear statement rule is a well-established background principle against which Congress legislates.

The Defendant's goal is to prevent the court from interpreting a statute to waive sovereign immunity for the United States government unless that is the only plausible reading.

This distinction is important because it excludes cases involving manufacturers, which have historically been off-limits under Section 1. It’s crucial to prevent a flood of new cases that were previously considered outside this scope.
Judge: Before 1925, were there workers who were not covered by any arbitration regime?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Are you referring to industry workers?
Judge: I think the question is about seamen who didn’t work for maritime shipping companies. Did they fall into a gray area where they weren’t covered by any arbitration regime?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Just to clarify, are you referring to seamen who were outside the Shipping Commissioners Act or not part of the shipping industry? That would include recreational boats and those on coastwise voyages, like lumber schooners. These would traditionally be considered manufacturers, and they would be outside the Shipping Commissioners Act. However, there are no cases interpreting this Act, so we have to draw analogies from the rail industry.
Judge: And in the rail industry, it was relatively clear that a worker had to be an employee of the railroad?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Yes, that was crystal clear. The federal arbitration provisions governing railroad employees required them to be employed by a common carrier. The same reasoning applied in Saxon, where a cargo loader was considered part of interstate commerce based on cases involving rail common carriers.
Judge: If you lose this case, how many workers and companies would be affected?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: The impact would be massive. In the past five years, new cases have emerged involving franchise restaurants, medical industries, and the entire food industry. For example, medical supplies need rapid shipment, and food products like bread and beer require point-to-sale distribution. Retail industries that manage their own warehouses would also be affected. This expansion would disrupt industries that have never been subject to such arbitration rules.
Judge: Couldn’t this issue be addressed through other legal doctrines?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: Not under the last-mile analysis, which is the main question here. These companies ship goods across state lines, and the historical understanding has been that Section 1 applies specifically to the transportation industry. Even in Saxon, the definition of seamen under Section 1 was based on a subset of the maritime shipping industry.
Judge: This might be a straightforward case for the lower court, but will it be as clear-cut in future cases? Won’t this lead to difficult line-drawing problems?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: No, 95% of these cases are straightforward. Companies like FedEx, UPS, and major freight carriers are clearly part of the shipping industry. Amazon, which ships both its own goods and others’ products, also falls under this category.
Judge: But what about companies where only a portion of their business—say 30%—involves transportation?
Defendant: ’s Lawyer: The Saxon test could apply here. If transportation is a frequent and substantial part of their business, they might be included. The test is no more complex than Saxon but provides an additional limitation to exclude cases that were previously not part of federal jurisdiction.
Judge: Are you saying that industry changes over time have made the legal language outdated?

Judge: Do you want to say why you think Almendarez-Torres is correct?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, Your Honor, I -- I don't really want to turn this into relitigation of Almendarez-Torres, which, again, even Petitioner has not asked for, but we think the Court was correct.
There are more states that I think support that than my friend would acknowledge.
There are some clear precedents from Alabama, Louisiana, South Carolina, and Kansas.
There are the superseding indictment statutes out of Virginia, West Virginia, Maine, and Massachusetts.
And although some of those statutes allow for jury trials on the back end, that was all historical evidence that was in front of the Court in Almendarez-Torres and the Court found that—and this goes to some of your earlier questioning, Your Honor—that there was at least enough non-uniformity on the issue to allow legislatures some wiggle room on this topic, which, again, Almendarez-
Judge: Who do you think has the burden on the historical practice?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, Your Honor, it's a little bit of, do you want to see two faces or a vase? I think, as we view it—
Judge: More than that. I'm going to need—
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Yeah. I—I—I thought you might.
I think, as we view it, the Court made a move in Apprendi that I think your colloquy with opposing counsel illustrated.
The Court made a move in Apprendi to extend the Sixth Amendment's treatment of things as an element to various features of criminal statutes that the legislature intended as sentencing factors.
And I think we would take the view that viewing that as somewhat of an extension of what the Sixth Amendment literally demands, there wouldn’t be any burden on us to show unanimous or near-unanimous practice.
So long as, as we think the history indicates, this was left to the legislature to describe, we think the legislature would retain that room today.
But, again, the Court doesn’t need to get into any of this today.
It is clear from the historical practice that there's really nothing like this.
At most, the amicus marshals some decisions that show—beyond simply reaffirming the correctness of Almendarez-Torres—that district courts or trial courts could conduct some sort of sequencing determination, which we think a court can do because it can find the time of the prior offense, but, at the very least, can find the time of the prior conviction.
And even those cases are relatively modern.
There is only South Carolina, so far as we can tell, under a 1955 statute and a 1972 decision that has allowed for litigation in front of a judge of the type of thing that the different occasions inquiry of ACCA might encompass.
And we're talking about, you know, precise timing questions.
So a court, we think, on its own could determine that on or—the jury necessarily found that an event occurred on or about April 7th, for example.
But the kind of timing questions that are involved under the ACCA's different occasions inquiry are going to be more fine-grained than that.
You could have acts occurring across a single day that are separate occasions.
Sells drugs in the morning.
In the afternoon, robs a store.
In the evening, comes home and beats his spouse.
Like, those would be three separate occasions.
There's clearly a chance to have a break in between those.
Judge: What about this one across—
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: This one, we think, is crystal clear.
We think it should clearly be found harmless on remand.
Judge: Not—we shouldn’t do it?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Oh, Your Honor, we're fine with you simply affirming on harmlessness grounds if that's what the Court chooses to do.
The Court's usual practice is to remand these things.
We think we've got a crystal-clear case on remand, and we will in most of these cases.
It'll be a vanishingly small number where we don't, but here, you have separate robberies that occurred on April 4th, April 8th, and April 11th—
Judge: And would there be some value—I mean, I guess, a value if you think—I’m not saying I agree with you—but if—if we agreed with you that this was a crystal-clear case, would there be some value to lower courts in saying, like, this is the kind of thing that, you know, under Wooden would still be different occasions?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Sure.
I mean, we think that's already clear to some degree from Wooden, which I take to generally say that if you've got offenses spaced as far apart as these are, that it's almost invariably going to be the case that they are on separate occasions.
But, if the Court wishes to explain that, that—that would be great for us.
In particular—or great by us.
For us as well.
In particular, just to address the harmless error argument that opposing counsel posited a few minutes ago, we don't think simply "I was in debt" is enough to make things the same occasion.
The kinds of circumstances where possibly a jury could—you know, we—we think, you know, it would be fair to find—obviously, this always goes to the jury, but we think would really be realistically found to be the same occasion if they occur across the course of several days—
might be what the Court posited in Wooden itself, like they’re part of a common criminal scheme.
So, for example, you burglarize a store to steal what you need to commit a kidnapping.
You commit an assault during the course of the kidnapping, and later you murder the victim.
It's possible that, you know, a jury could find that those were all the same occasions—
Judge: Mr.—
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: —even if it occurred over the course of a few days.
Judge: So, Counsel, on that, first of all, I commend the government for acknowledging the error below in this case. That's an admirable step of candor.
But, on this harmless error question, let me ask you first, how is a court supposed to conduct that when there hasn’t been a trial and in a world in which almost everybody pleads guilty these days? A really novel development during the course of our lifetimes.
So, here, the defendant was told that the three predicate ACCA crimes were different than these three crimes that you’re now asking us to—for a court to say are clearly separate occasions and—and, therefore, harmless error.
How is it harmless when he didn’t know what the charges would be against him when he pled guilty?
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, Your Honor, I think he—
Judge: Wouldn’t that have informed his bargain?
Perhaps he would have chosen not to plead guilty if you were going to drag back up convictions from when he was 18 that have nothing to do with his possession of a firearm today as a 40-something-year-old man.
Plaintiff: 's Lawyer: Well, Your Honor, first of all, I don’t think he was under any assurances that he would not receive an ACCA sentence.
In fact, he was—
Judge: No, but in the information, the government specifically listed three other predicate offenses, not these.
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff's lawyer is arguing that the Court was correct in the case of Almendarez-Torres, citing various state statutes and precedents to support their claim. They believe that the Court's decision in Apprendi extended the Sixth Amendment's treatment of things as an element to various features of criminal statutes, and as a result, there would be no burden on them to show unanimous or near-unanimous practice.

The Plaintiff's lawyer also argues that the ACCA's different occasions inquiry is more fine-grained than the historical practice, and that a court could determine the timing of separate occasions on its own. They provide examples of separate offenses that could occur over several days but still be considered separate occasions.

The Plaintiff's lawyer acknowledges the error in the case at hand and suggests that it should be found harmless on remand.
DEFENDANT:
The Defendant's lawyer is arguing that the Court was correct in the case of Almendarez-Torres, which held that the sentencing factor of prior convictions for purposes of the Armed Career Criminal Act (ACCA) does not need to be found by a jury. The Defendant's lawyer provides several examples of state statutes and court decisions that support this position.

The Defendant's lawyer suggests that the Court made a move in Apprendi to extend the Sixth Amendment's treatment of things as an element to various features of criminal statutes that the legislature intended as sentencing factors. The Defendant's lawyer argues that this extension does not place a burden on them to show unanimous or near-unanimous practice, as long as the historical practice indicates that this was left to the legislature to describe.

The Defendant's lawyer also argues that the ACCA's different occasions inquiry is more fine-grained than the historical practice, and that a court could determine the timing of separate occasions on its own.

Bailiff: All parties, please raise your right hands. Be seated. Come to order, please. Litigants have been sworn, Your Honor.
Judge: Thank you, Douglas. Mark Minsky, you are suing James. You've asked us not to refer to your last name or the salon's name, and we will respect that. You're suing him as owner of the salon for $315, a replacement cost for eyeglasses that you say were stolen from the salon. Talk to me. Tell me what happened.
Plaintiff: Okay, so I went there to have my haircut back on Thursday, March 24th, in the evening. And I sat down in the stylist's chair. Uh, there was no space to put my glasses on her personal shelf, so there was a Parson table to my left, and I reached out, and I took them off and placed them within arm’s reach on this table to my left. When the haircut was complete, I turned to my left to pick up the glasses. They were gone.
Judge: How far away from your body were your glasses?
Plaintiff: Maybe 3 feet. Arm’s reach.
Judge: Right. So wouldn’t you see somebody if somebody had taken your glasses?
Plaintiff: I did not. I guess my peripheral vision is not the best.
Judge: So at that point?
Plaintiff: I mentioned it to the stylist. She had some employees, I presume some hair washers as I recall, come and help me look. We looked in a variety of—
Judge: How are you looking? Because if you have no glasses on, are you not very—there is nothing worse than when I lose my glasses. I need to enlist an army to help me because if—without—if I don’t have them, I can’t find them. So it’s just a self-fulfilling—
Plaintiff: Yes, very difficult, to say the least. So we looked for quite a while, you know, probably over an hour. And as you just pointed out, I do need my glasses to see. My vision is actually 20 over 150 in one eye, 20 over 300 in the other.
Judge: Right, but why are you suing the salon? What did the salon do? You lost your glasses. Why—why is it responsible for you losing your glasses?
Plaintiff: Somebody removed them from where I had placed them.
Judge: Why is the salon responsible? Let’s assume somebody did take them. Who’s the somebody? Why is the salon responsible?
Plaintiff: Because I walked—I—it happened there. I walked in there. I placed it, you know, within—
Judge: What did they do wrong? Normally, when somebody has to pay you for something, they have to have done something wrong. So what did they do wrong? Do you have a—do you have a camera in there?
Defendant: No.
Judge: Did you at one point tell him that you had a camera?
Defendant: It was a salon somebody else owned. I didn’t—
Judge: Did you at one point tell him that you had a camera going?
Defendant: I never said that, no.
Judge: Okay. According to you, somebody told you that?
Plaintiff: Yes, he has a business partner, and he told me that there was a camera. He was going to review it, and when he did, he calls me and tells me that the videotape showed I came in without glasses.
Judge: Okay. And I asked—come on out. Who are the ladies who are there? Are they witnesses? ‘Cause one of them is chicken-necking, so I think she—
Defendant: This is Nancy, who interacted the whole night with him.
Judge: Okay. Did you guys ever see a camera, like see the—see the tape? Was there a tape?
Defendant: No, there was—
Judge: Was he just saying that to him to get him off?
Defendant: He was just saying it.
Judge: But this is very interesting—kind of rude.
Plaintiff: Right.
Judge: Right. Because you're saying the glasses were stolen. If you’re saying you came in without them, then you’re calling him a liar.
Defendant: I never said that to him.
Plaintiff: His partner did.
Judge: His partner did. But his partner's not the one who’s being sued.
Plaintiff: Right, but his partner said it in the course of handling it for him.
Judge: Okay, so why would his partner say that?
Plaintiff: To deflect the situation.
Judge: Okay, but you’re assuming that.
Plaintiff: Yeah.
Judge: Do you have anything beyond that? Do you have a police report?
Plaintiff: I did file a police report.
Judge: Okay. And?
Plaintiff: Nothing came of it.
Judge: Right. Because what’s a police officer gonna do? Say, "Hey, everybody in the salon, empty your pockets?" That’s not how it works.
Plaintiff: Right.
Judge: Right. I mean, unless somebody was caught on camera—which there is no camera—you don’t have proof that somebody stole it.
Plaintiff: I understand.
Judge: And you’re suing the salon, but there’s no legal theory under which the salon owes you $315.
Plaintiff: I understand.
Judge: Verdict for the defendant.

Judge: This is an old question, but, you know, eating is a basic human function as well, that people have to do, just like sleeping. So if someone is hungry and no one is giving him food, can you prosecute him if he breaks into a store to get something to eat?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Absolutely, absolutely. Breaking into a store is a common crime that not everybody engages in, unlike sleeping, which is what we have here, which is really—
Judge: But it's a necessity for the person who needs food.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: It's not a necessity to break into a store. And with respect to—
Judge: Well, you're fighting the hypothetical. I'm saying this person needs food.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: And the Eighth Amendment does not require that that person be excused from doing it. I think there's a certain amount of common sense and practicality to this, and it's, I think, well understood that just like drug use is not something the Eighth Amendment excuses you from, neither is eating.
And the problem of eating is addressed at the local level as the history and the poor law show—that the community takes care of its own residents. And it's common now, as it was at the founding, for churches and individuals to offer their help through charity in the community.
And that's what happens in Grants Pass. Various organizations feed the homeless people. And there are social services to help the homeless people. So this is consistent except for the absolute ban on sleeping in the city. Otherwise, the community's response is what has been done down through history.
Judge: Thank you, counsel.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: It is the city's absolute ban—
Judge: Thank you.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: —that interrupts that continuity.
Judge: Justice, do you have any questions?
Justice: Could you explain how your rule would be carried out by police officers on a day-to-day basis? Let's say that there are 500 beds in a particular town, and it's 3:00 or 4:00 in the afternoon on a winter day.
What is an individual police officer supposed to do? Would the officer go around and count the number of people who are getting ready to sleep outside? Let’s say it’s 6:00. Would the officer count the number of people getting ready to sleep outside for the night and then ask each one of them whether they've tried to find a bed at a shelter?
Would the officer ask whether that person would be willing to go to a shelter if a bed is available without any conditions? Or whether the bed would have to be available under conditions the individual wants, like not going to a shelter that won’t take their dog?
Can you explain how this would work on a daily basis?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Well, first of all, with respect to the individual encounter, I think the way this would work in the real world—it’s important to understand what happens on the ground in these situations.
I think in the circumstances you’re describing, what would happen is that the person encountering the homeless individual would know whether there is a spot available. I don’t think the homeless person would be required to check each day with each shelter if there are multiple shelters.
And in larger cities, these initial encounters are not handled by law enforcement. They're typically handled by social services agencies that are in contact with people who are camping and know what their circumstances are. These agencies would be able to say: We know that at such-and-such shelter, there are beds available—would you be willing to go?
Justice: What if there’s a question about whether there are enough shelter beds available? Your rule wouldn’t apply if there are enough beds available, right? If there are 500 shelter beds and only 200 people trying to sleep outside, then your rule wouldn’t apply?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Right, right.
Justice: So you have to compare the number of beds available with the number of people who want to sleep outside?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Right, yes.
Justice: So that would be the threshold question?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Right. And I just want to clarify one point about that. It’s not simply a matter of the number of beds against the number of homeless people, such that if there is a deficit, the city can't enforce the law at all.
If you have individualized questioning and you know that there are vacancies available—even if not for everyone—but there is a vacancy for the person being interviewed, then yes, that person, if offered a bed and refuses, could be prosecuted or cited.
Justice: What if the person says, "Yeah, I know there's a bed available at the Gospel Rescue Mission, but they won’t take my dog"?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I don't think the inability to take your dog to a shelter is a sufficient reason. There are shelters in some larger cities that may well take pets, but—
Justice: I know I could sleep in the home of a family member, but they really hate me and treat me badly.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I—
Justice: I’m just wondering how this is going to be administered on a daily basis.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: And I think, with all respect, that example—if the family is going to accept them, then that’s the question. Whether there is a place to sleep.
But I don’t know that it would very often come down to "that family hates me." On the other hand, if it's a woman who left a domestic abuse situation, she couldn't be expected to go back to that home.
Justice: Right.
Defendant: 's Lawyer: So there's a lot of common sense involved. And again, the first encounter that a police officer or someone else has with a homeless person is very unlikely to be a situation where the person would be immediately issued a citation.
Justice: Okay. You mentioned a couple of things I wanted to follow up on. Does it matter whether the person grew up in the town or not?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: No.
Justice: So if they tell a police officer or social worker, "I'm from Fargo, but if I have to sleep outside, I'd rather do it here than in Fargo," that doesn’t matter?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: No, and that’s not because of any Eighth Amendment rule, but under this Court’s decisions, various constitutional provisions prohibit attaching that sort of limitation to a newcomer.
Justice: I used to live in New Jersey, where there are a lot of small municipalities. I could go for a 20-minute walk in the evening and be in three or four different towns.
If there aren’t enough beds in one town, but there are beds in a nearby town, does that mean the first town is out of luck?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: I think the way you're describing it, that set of small, closely-knit communities might be considered one community. The person wouldn't be banished from where they lived just because a shelter exists in a neighboring area.
Justice: Some tiny municipalities don’t even have parks. If someone is going to sleep outside, the only place would be on the street. What happens then?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Certainly not on the street because of safety and traffic concerns. But even in the smallest town, there are probably locations where a person could sleep.
Justice: All right. Thank you.
Judge: Justice, do you have any further questions?
Justice: I don’t want to be repetitive, but what are we vacating and remanding for? An individualized finding of what?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Well, the class was defined simply based on aggregate numbers, without an individualized determination—frankly, in our view, not even a sufficient determination of the two named plaintiffs.
Justice: So you're talking about standing?
Defendant: 's Lawyer: Yes, and possibly other legal issues, such as whether the named plaintiffs' situations were representative of the broader case.
Justice: All right. Thank you.
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff's lawyer argues that the city's absolute ban on sleeping in public spaces disrupts the historical community response to homelessness. They suggest that the Eighth Amendment does not require the city to excuse individuals from finding alternative means to meet their basic needs, such as food or shelter.

The Plaintiff's lawyer proposes a solution where law enforcement or social services agencies would first determine if there are available shelter beds in the area. If there are, they would offer these beds to homeless individuals.
DEFENDANT:
The Defendant's lawyer argues that the absolute ban on sleeping in public places, as enforced by the city, is unconstitutional. They suggest that the Eighth Amendment does not excuse individuals from basic necessities like finding food or shelter, but it does not require the city to provide these necessities either. The lawyer argues that the community, through charities and social services, should be responsible for addressing homelessness.

The lawyer proposes a solution where police officers would not enforce the sleeping ban if there are available shelter beds, and the homeless individual refuses to take them due to specific conditions (such as not being able to bring a pet or not wanting to go to a shelter that doesn't meet their preferences).

Dialogue:
Judge: All parties, please raise your right hand. Back to the People's Court. Next case on the docket: The Plaintiff says the Defendant ruined his hot tub cover by using it as a bench, but the Defendant says not guilty. It's the case of "Undercovered." Thank you, Douglas. William Taylor?
Plaintiff: Yes, ma'am.
Judge: You are suing your former tenant, Lori Phillips, for $425.00, the amount you say she owes you to replace a hot tub cover that, according to you, she broke. What happened?
Plaintiff: Your Honor, last summer, from about May to August 2011, Lori rented a bedroom from me in my house. We have a hot tub in the rear and also a swimming pool. She utilized both of these; that wasn’t an issue at all. The issue was how she used the hot tub.
Judge: How many people lived in the place?
Plaintiff: Around five altogether. Myself and four other roommates. It’s a five-bedroom split-level, kind of a Brady Bunch-style split-level. Right around the middle of August, I saw her coming out of the pool, going to the hot tub on the other deck, and lifting the cover over. So now we have it halfway over like that, covering half the hot tub. Then she proceeded to get in it.
Judge: And you questioned her about this?
Plaintiff: Yes. I saw her at least on one occasion doing this and asked, "What are you doing?" She said, "Oh, I'm just getting warmed up because the pool is too cold." I said, "Oh, okay, well, as long as you don’t get trapped underneath it." That was all I cared about.
Plaintiff: One other time, when I was mowing the lawn, I saw her utilizing the hot tub that way again. On the edge, where she had the cover folded over, and on the outer reinforced side of the hot tub, she would push herself up out of the hot tub in that manner.
Plaintiff: On another occasion, shortly before she moved out, my girlfriend Angela, who is also my roommate, noticed Lori sitting on it—
Judge: Is Angela here to testify?
Plaintiff: No, I have a notarized statement from her.
Judge: Why isn’t she here?
Plaintiff: She’s afraid of flying.
Judge: So she left you hanging?
Plaintiff: Yes, they all did.
Judge: Wow. Okay, continue.
Plaintiff: Angela informed me of seeing that also. At that time, I didn’t think much of it. But when closing the cover—
Judge: When did you discover the damage?
Plaintiff: Shortly after she moved out.
Judge: Did you return a security deposit?
Plaintiff: There was no security deposit.
Judge: How long after she moved out did you notice the problem?
Plaintiff: About six weeks.
Judge: And after six weeks, you then tell her the hot tub cover is damaged?
Defendant: He accused me of something else first. He came after me for not giving a 30-day notice before moving out.
Judge: And did you?
Defendant: No, I gave two weeks’ notice.
Judge: And what were you supposed to give?
Defendant: 30 days. I was guilty of that.
Judge: So you paid the rest of the rent because you felt you owed it?
Defendant: Yes.
Judge: And after paying, that’s the first time he mentions you ruined his hot tub cover?
Defendant: Yes, in April. That was six months later. I got a letter at work.
Judge: When did she move out?
Plaintiff: Third week of August.
Judge: Why did you wait four months to bring it up?
Plaintiff: When she made her last payment in December, I told her about the cover and said I would try to repair it.
Judge: Did you ever try to repair it?
Plaintiff: Yes. When I opened it, I saw more damage.
Judge: Did you get a professional opinion?
Plaintiff: No.
Judge: Do you have proof of the cover’s age and depreciation?
Plaintiff: No, but it’s two years old, with a 10-year lifespan.
Judge: Proof?
Plaintiff: I have a warranty for the replacement one.
Judge: That’s not proof for this one. Ms. Phillips, did you ever lean on the hot tub cover?
Defendant: No.
Judge: Did you use it half-flapped?
Defendant: Yes, but I entered on the other side.
Judge: Did you sit on it?
Defendant: No.
Judge: Does the hot tub have steps?
Plaintiff: No, just low seats.
Judge: My verdict is for the Defendant. Your proof is very thin.
PLAINTIFF: The Plaintiff, William Taylor, is suing his former tenant, Lori Phillips, for $425 to replace a hot tub cover that he claims she damaged. The Plaintiff alleges that Lori used the hot tub cover as a bench or seat on multiple occasions, which caused the damage. He provides anecdotes of witnessing Lori using the cover in this manner and mentions that his roommate, Angela, also observed this behavior.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant, Lori Phillips, is being sued by the Plaintiff, William Taylor, for $425 to replace a hot tub cover that was allegedly damaged by the Defendant's use. The Defendant admits to using the hot tub cover as a makeshift seat or step, but denies sitting on it or causing significant damage. The Defendant claims that the Plaintiff only brought up the issue four months after the Defendant moved out, and the Plaintiff did not attempt to repair the cover or obtain a professional opinion about its condition.

The Plaintiff's goal or motive behind their words seems to be to establish that Pamela was unreasonably angry about the $20 and that this anger led to the vandalism of the Plaintiff's car.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant's statements suggest that there is a long-standing tension between her and her sister (the Plaintiff) due to a misunderstanding about some money their mother left for them. The Defendant also mentions a past incident where she threatened to damage the Plaintiff's car and spit in her face during a birthday party, which the Plaintiff claims caused her emotional distress and led to the car damage.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Yes, Your Honor.
Judge: This Court has said that when -- and this is Thunder Basin footnote 8. Because court of appeals review is available, this case does not implicate the strong presumption that Congress did not mean to prohibit --
Judge: Yeah, but --
Lawyer of Plaintiff: -- all forms of judicial review.
Judge: Yeah, but if the -- the statute -- what do you say, though -- I mean, it seems to me 1101(a)(47), when it defines final order, really is pegged to the -- the Board's decision one way or the other.
And here we have none.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: So, Your Honor, I acknowledge that Congress could have spoken more directly to this situation and --
Judge: Well, it could have spoken to this situation at all, right?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Well, I think it could have spoken more directly.
I'll acknowledge that, Your Honor.
But I think it's clear in light of Section 1228(b), which does speak directly to this situation in two critical places.
So Section 1228(b)(4)(F) calls an order entered under this -- under that subsection a final order of removal, using exactly the same language that Section 1252 does itself.
And that's only confirmed by Section 1228(b)(3), which imposes a stay or a bar on the removal of the alien for 14 days from the date of issuance of that order and ordered that the alien has the opportunity to apply for judicial review, confirming that that order is final when it is issued.
Judge: If -- if --
Lawyer of Plaintiff: And I think --
Judge: If we didn't have Stone, just to shift gears a little bit, would you read 1252(b)(1) as jurisdictional?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Your Honor, I acknowledge that the filing deadline likely would not satisfy the clear statement test apart from Stone.
Judge: Okay.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Just to mention that -- the definition a little bit more, Your Honor, I think the best way to understand this is that it is a statute-wide definition.
It applies by its terms to the entirety of the INA.
So it covers Section 1228(b).
And I think the best way to read it is to reconcile it with 1228(b), particularly in light of the history of the two provisions.
The predecessor to Section 1228(b) that originally dispensed with the need for a hearing before an immigration judge was adopted in 1994.
DOJ adopted regulations implementing it in 1995. They did away with the need for any Board review.
So by the time Congress came to AEDPA in 1996 to add this definition, it already had this part of the statute.
It already knew this statute called this a final order of deportation, and yet it adopted this definition. I think the best way to read it together with that is to reconcile it, to say that Board -- the period for Board review expires immediately when no Board review is allowed.
But if you disagree with me on that, I think Section 1228(b) itself answers this question, and it's strongly supported by Section 1231 and what this Court said about administrative finality in Guzman Chavez.
As the government acknowledges in its reply --
Judge: What do you say about the possibility that some courts of appeals won't allow these protective appeals to sit on their books for years on end?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Your Honor, I think this Court could provide guidance about the situations in which those motions for abeyance should be granted.
The Court has done something similar in the habeas context in Rhines v. Weber, where it authorized a stay-in-abeyance procedure for district courts so that habeas petitioners could go into district courts and exhaust their -- go into state court to exhaust their claims before coming back.
This Court could authorize something like that.
I don't think that's necessary here, Your Honor, because I think the meaning of the filing deadline is plain.
But the Court could provide guidance in that way.
Judge: Counsel, you say that Stone's rejection of tolling indicates that it's a true jurisdictional holding.
But tolling can be unavailable in equitable proceedings as well, can't it, when the deadlines are -- are equitable?
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Yes, Your Honor.
It is true that some claim processing rules also foreclose tolling, but they don't do it in the way that Stone did.
So this Court in Irwin held that non-jurisdictional statutory deadlines are presumptively amenable to equitable tolling.
We see nothing like a rebuttal in Stone of that presumption of equitable tolling.
Instead, we see the automatic and reflexive connection of the Court's holding that the deadline is jurisdictional to the consequence that it doesn't allow for equitable tolling.
That's the exact opposite of what happened in Beggerly, the case that this Court analyzed in Wilkins and said didn't count as a definitive jurisdictional precedent.
It had an extensive analysis of equitable tolling.
And the Court said if it was a true jurisdictional deadline, it wouldn't have needed that extensive analysis.
And that's exactly what we have in Stone; a direct and immediate connection between the conclusion that the deadline is jurisdictional and the consequence that it doesn't allow for equitable tolling.
Judge: Well, you also argue that the citation of the cert deadline and the appeal deadline is very, very significant here, but those seem to be quite different in the sense that you're dealing with the vertical deadlines of quite some significance.
And I wonder whether that is a particularly compelling analogy.
Lawyer of Plaintiff: Your Honor, I think it was the analogy that the Court found compelling in Stone.
It was the analogy that supported the Court's ultimate conclusion that the deadline was jurisdictional and engaged in this extensive analysis, comparing the deadline to file a notice of appeal from a final order of deportation to the time to notice an appeal from a district court judgment, which, as you point out, this Court has recognized as jurisdictional, both before and after Arbaugh.
The Court also compared the effect of the motion for reconsideration to the effect of a Rule 60(b) motion, which doesn't strip the court of appeals of jurisdiction.
And I think that -- the force of that analogy is that it shows the Court understood the filing deadline as a window of time at which the court of appeals could assert jurisdiction, regardless of any motion for reconsideration, and after which it could not assert jurisdiction, just like in the notice of appeal context.
I think all of that analysis supported what happens on pages 405 of Stone and shows that the Court truly meant what it said when it said that the deadline was jurisdictional.
Judge: But just to underscore Justice Gorsuch's point, under our modern jurisprudence, you would agree that it's not jurisdictional, that you're relying on the Stone precedent as the reason why we should hold that it's jurisdictional here?
PLAINTIFF:
The Plaintiff's lawyer is arguing that the filing deadline for appeals in immigration cases is a jurisdictional requirement, not just a procedural one. They are basing this argument on the Supreme Court case of Stone v. The Plaintiff's lawyer is also suggesting that the Court could provide guidance on when motions for abeyance (which would allow appeals to remain on hold) should be granted, drawing an analogy with the Court's previous decisions in the habeas context.

Furthermore, the Plaintiff's lawyer is arguing that the Court's analysis in Stone, comparing the deadline to file a notice of appeal from a final order of deportation to the time to notice an appeal from a district court judgment, supports their argument that the deadline is jurisdictional.

In summary, the Plaintiff's intent is to persuade the court that the filing deadline for appeals in immigration cases is a jurisdictional requirement, not just a procedural one, and that the Court should follow the precedent set in Stone v.
DEFENDANT: The Defendant, the Lawyer of Plaintiff, is arguing that the filing deadline for appeals in immigration cases is a jurisdictional requirement, not just a procedural one. They are basing this argument on the Supreme Court case of Stone v. INS, which they believe establishes this jurisdictional requirement.
